title: Investigating the effects of interactive social media communication on community engagement: evidence from Abu Dhabi health sector
abstract:
This research examines how social media is used within the Abu Dhabi healthcare system, and how it contributes to public health communication and community engagement. The study addresses four key aims: to measure the extent to which interactive communication via social media increases individual community engagement; to assess the effectiveness of social media in content-driven public health communication; to determine the impact of engagement on health outcomes; and to identify the most common issues raised by communities on social media and to explain how the public health system responds in practice. The study is theoretically framed using the Health Belief Model (HBM) and Social Influence Theory (SIT), providing a means to examine both cognitive and sociocultural aspects of health behaviour and communication in the digital context.

A qualitative approach was employed, comprising 28 semi-structured interviews (9 with public health professionals and 21 with community respondents), alongside archival data from over 200 social media interactions on platforms such as Twitter and Instagram. Data were analysed using systematic thematic analysis. Five key thematic domains emerged: Dialogic Resonance, Perceptual Cartography, Communal Ethos (defined as shared cultural values, norms, and trust influencing how communities respond to health communication), Mobilized Cognition, and Digital Reverberations. These domains, comprising 20 codes, reflect the iterative, participatory, and adaptive forms through which digital media engagement for health communication is enacted.

The findings suggest that interactive social media communication enhances community engagement by fostering feedback loops, dialogic resonance, and relational trust. Social media serves both as a distribution and participatory channel, shaping perceptions and influencing behaviour through emotional content and peer dialogue. Evidence indicates that such engagement improves health outcomes, particularly in vaccination uptake and preventive behaviour. The study also identifies key community concerns, including trust, clarity, cultural relevance, emotional tone, misinformation, and accessibility, which can be addressed through live question-and-answer sessions, multilingual messaging, partnerships with trusted influencers, and empathetic, culturally attuned content.

The study contributes conceptually by reconceptualising health communication as a dynamic, co-created process rather than a top-down broadcast. It integrates social and psychological theories to explain digital behaviour and introduces constructs such as Echo Governance and Discursive Pulse . It contextualises HBM and SIT within a multicultural, digitally sophisticated environment and develops a recursive communication model in which trust functions simultaneously as an input, a mediating force, and an outcome of effective engagement. Managerially, the study provides practical implications for creating culturally sensitive, emotionally intelligent, and inclusive communication strategies and emphasises the value of using real-time analytics to support adaptive and transparent public health messaging.

Although the study offers valuable insights, it is limited by its regional focus, lack of longitudinal data, and absence of quantitative validation. Future research may apply the model across diverse geopolitical contexts, track long-term behavioural patterns, and employ qualitative methods designs to enhance generalisability and predictive power. Nonetheless, the research provides a holistic and theoretically grounded account of the evolving role of social media in transforming public health communication in Abu Dhabi and proposes a new framework for trust-based, community-driven digital health interventions.

Acknowledgements 
This research would not have been possible without the support, encouragement, and 
guidance of many individuals and institutions to whom I owe my deepest gratitude. 
First and foremost, I extend my heartfelt thanks to my supervisor, Prof Wilson Ozuem 
whose intellectual guidance, patience, and insightful feedback have been instrumental 
throughout this research journey. Their critical reflections and encouragement pushed 
me to explore ideas with greater depth and clarity, shaping this study into its final form. 
I am profoundly grateful to the healthcare professionals and members of the Abu 
Dhabi community who generously shared their time, experiences, and perspectives 
during the interviews. Their openness and thoughtful contributions gave meaning and 
authenticity to this study. Without their willingness to participate, this research would 
not have been possible. 
I also wish to acknowledge the academic and administrative staff who offered support 
and resources that facilitated the smooth conduct of this research. Their 
professionalism and assistance in navigating institutional processes ensured the 
study’s ethical and methodological integrity. 
To my family, I owe immeasurable thanks for their unwavering love, patience, and 
understanding. Their constant encouragement sustained me through moments of 
challenge and inspired me to persevere. 
Finally, I would like to dedicate this work to all those committed to improving public 
health communication and community wellbeing. It is my hope that this study 
contributes in some way to the growing understanding of how dialogue, trust, and 
engagement can strengthen the relationship between health systems and the societies 
they serve. 
 
 
 
 
 
3 
 
Abstract  
This research examines how social media is used within the Abu Dhabi healthcare system, and how it 
contributes to public health communication and community engagement. The study addresses four key 
aims: to measure the extent to which interactive communication via social media increases individual 
community engagement; to assess the effectiveness of social media in content-driven public health 
communication; to determine the impact of engagement on health outcomes; and to identify the most 
common issues raised by communities on social media and to explain how the public health system 
responds in practice. The study is theoretically framed using the Health Belief Model (HBM) and Social 
Influence Theory (SIT), providing a means to examine both cognitive and sociocultural aspects of health 
behaviour and communication in the digital context. 
A qualitative approach was employed, comprising 28 semi-structured interviews (9 with public health 
professionals and 21 with community respondents), alongside archival data from over 200 social media 
interactions on platforms such as Twitter and Instagram. Data were analysed using systematic thematic 
analysis. Five key thematic domains emerged: Dialogic Resonance, Perceptual Cartography, 
Communal Ethos (defined as shared cultural values, norms, and trust influencing how communities 
respond to health communication), Mobilized Cognition, and Digital Reverberations. These domains, 
comprising 20 codes, reflect the iterative, participatory, and adaptive forms through which digital media 
engagement for health communication is enacted. 
The findings suggest that interactive social media communication enhances community engagement 
by fostering feedback loops, dialogic resonance, and relational trust. Social media serves both as a 
distribution and participatory channel, shaping perceptions and influencing behaviour through emotional 
content and peer dialogue. Evidence indicates that such engagement improves health outcomes, 
particularly in vaccination uptake and preventive behaviour. The study also identifies key community 
concerns, including trust, clarity, cultural relevance, emotional tone, misinformation, and accessibility, 
which can be addressed through live question-and-answer sessions, multilingual messaging, 
partnerships with trusted influencers, and empathetic, culturally attuned content. 
The study contributes conceptually by reconceptualising health communication as a dynamic, co-
created process rather than a top-down broadcast. It integrates social and psychological theories to 
explain digital behaviour and introduces constructs such as Echo Governance and Discursive Pulse . It 
contextualises HBM and SIT within a multicultural, digitally sophisticated environment and develops a 
recursive communication model in which trust functions simultaneously as an input, a mediating force, 
and an outcome of effective engagement. Managerially, the study provides practical implications for 
creating culturally sensitive, emotionally intelligent, and inclusive communication strategies and 
emphasises the value of using real-time analytics to support adaptive and transparent public health 
messaging. 
Although the study offers valuable insights, it is limited by its regional focus, lack of longitudinal data, 
and absence of quantitative validation. Future research may apply the model across diverse geopolitical 
contexts, track long-term behavioural patterns, and employ qualitative methods designs to enhance 
generalisability and predictive power. Nonetheless, the research provides a holistic and theoretically 
grounded account of the evolving role of social media in transforming public health communication in 
Abu Dhabi and proposes a new framework for trust-based, community-driven digital health 
interventions.

1 CHAPTER 1: RESEARCH INTRODUCTION  
1.1 ENQUIRY OVERVIEW 
This study examines the role of social media as an interactive communication platform 
and community engagement tool within Abu Dhabi’s public health system. While two-
way communication is globally recognised as vital for improving public health, and 
social media has played a key role in linking communities with healthcare providers, 
there remains limited empirical evidence as to its effectiveness in promoting public 
engagement and behavioural change in the UAE—despite high social media usage in 
Abu Dhabi. 
Abu Dhabi presents a distinctive socio-cultural context shaped by values such as 
stiga, an Emirati concept of moral oversight, and sumud, the notion that strength 
lies in enduring hardship, alongside British political influence and traditional Arabic 
generosity. These factors create unique communication demands for health 
messaging. Understanding how social media fosters trust, responsiveness, and 
participation within this cultural setting is essential. 
The purpose of this study is to assess the role of participatory social media in 
promoting public health knowledge, attitudes, and practices. It examines the nature of 
shared content, community responses, and how public health organisations address 
concerns raised online. Guided by the Health Belief Model and Social Influence 
Theory, the study explores how personal health beliefs and collective action are 
shaped in virtual environments. 
By examining community interaction and the responsiveness of Abu Dhabi’s health 
system, this study addresses a key gap in the literature. It also offers practical 
guidance for developing culturally appropriate health communication strategies to 
enhance public trust, engagement, and health outcomes through effective use of 
social media.  
1.2 BACKGROUND OF THE STUDY 
The importance of communication in improving public health is widely recognised by 
health systems globally (Madhumathi et al., 2021). Social media promotes interactive 
communication between public health systems and communities, contributing to 
9 
 
greater health awareness, healthier lifestyles, and improved access to care (Ozuem 
et al., 2023; Madhumathi et al., 2021). Cultural and societal factors influence how 
social media is used in health communication and engagement (Ni et al., 2021; Ngai 
et al., 2020), and many scholars have called for further research in this area (Burgess 
et al., 2021). This need is especially pressing in culturally distinctive contexts like Abu 
Dhabi, particularly during global health crises. Despite widespread use of digital 
technologies, limited research has explored the influence of social media on 
community health and engagement in the region (Bhattacharya et al., 2017; Ni et al., 
2021). This study aims to examine how social media functions as a two-way 
communication tool within Abu Dhabi’s public health system—encouraging community 
engagement, influencing health outcomes, and shaping institutional responses to 
public concerns. By focusing on a single, culturally specific region, it contributes 
valuable insight into interactive health communication in a specialised public health 
context. 
The use of social media to facilitate interactive communication within health systems 
is a much-discussed issue in the literature (Madhumathi et al., 2021; Park et al., 2016). 
Yet the ability of social media to support and increase community engagement in 
public health systems in specific regions like Abu Dhabi remains under-studied. 
Takacs et al. (2019) highlight the importance of effective community engagement 
during public health emergency preparedness. However, their study does not examine 
how social media facilitates this participatory process or its specific role in emergency 
contexts. This gap suggests a need for further research into the mechanisms through 
which social media can support community involvement in public health preparedness. 
Engagement encompasses both participation and communication (Petsios et al., 
2017), while interactive communication refers to any user action during health-related 
exchanges—such as posting content, joining campaigns, replying to messages, or 
participating in online discussions (Yao & Li, 2014; Bhattacharya et al., 2017). 
“Interactive” implies real-time, two-way exchanges where all participants both send 
and receive messages. In this study, community engagement refers to the involvement 
of individuals—patients, service users, or the general public—who are directly or 
indirectly affected by health sector decisions. This includes participation in decision-
making and activities shaping the direction and delivery of health services. Examples 
include access to information, reciprocal communication, and targeted outreach 
10 
 
(Bhattacharya et al., 2017). However, studies have yet to examine these mechanisms 
within Abu Dhabi’s public health context. Given that the impact of social media on 
health communication and engagement varies across cultural and social settings (Ni 
et al., 2021; Ngai et al., 2020), there is a clear need to investigate how these dynamics 
operate in the culturally and demographically unique setting of Abu Dhabi. This notable 
gap warrants further exploration. 
1.3 THE CULTURAL AND SOCIAL ENVIRONMENT IN ABU DHABI AND PUBLIC HEALTH 
COMMUNICATION 
Cultural values, attitudes, and practices toward medical care differ significantly in 
multicultural settings like Abu Dhabi, with health information-seeking behaviour 
varying from that observed in Western contexts. As Naeem and Ozuem (2021) 
highlight, engagement with social media among health professionals varies across 
cultures and regions. This is an important consideration in the planning and 
implementation of health-based social media (HBsM) strategies. Abu Dhabi, and the 
UAE more broadly, have a unique cultural context that emphasises respect for 
authority, social cohesion, and the collective good (Campbell & Rudan, 2020). These 
cultural values significantly shape how public health communication and community 
engagement are understood and practiced. In such a context, messaging that 
emphasises collective welfare, community solidarity, and the credibility of healthcare 
authorities is likely to be more effective than communication rooted in individualistic 
notions of health. 
The religious and social culture of Abu Dhabi necessitates a tailored approach to 
public health education. For example, during Ramadan, health messaging may need 
to address fasting practices in alignment with religious beliefs (Campbell & Rudan, 
2020). Given the high levels of internet access and social media use in the city (Naeem 
& Ozuem, 2021), these platforms present a valuable engagement tool. Yet little is 
known about how to use them most effectively within this unique cultural and social 
context. Abu Dhabi’s public healthcare system also serves a multicultural, multilingual 
population with a large expatriate demographic, requiring communication strategies 
that reflect diverse linguistic and cultural needs (Campbell & Rudan, 2020). These 
factors highlight the need for a more specialised investigation into the effective use of 
social media for public health engagement in Abu Dhabi. Strategies developed in 
11 
 
Western or other cultural contexts cannot be assumed transferable without adaptation 
to Abu Dhabi’s distinctive environment. 
1.4 RATIONALE OF THE STUDY 
While social media is recognised as an important public health communication tool 
(Heldman et al., 2013; Keller et al., 2014), its impact on public perception and trust in 
health services remains underexplored, particularly in Abu Dhabi. Key dimensions 
such as transparency, responsiveness, and perceived trustworthiness in health-
related social media communication require further investigation (Burgess et al., 
2021). For example, Bhattacharya et al. (2017) noted that although US federal health 
agencies strategically used Facebook for engagement, their study did not examine 
how such macro-level factors shape public perception. 
Trust in health information shared via social media is shaped by factors such as 
perceived credibility and transparency of the source (Graffigna et al., 2021). However, 
the mechanisms through which these factors influence public trust in Abu Dhabi’s 
health system remain poorly understood. Public engagement is also influenced by the 
communicative behaviour of health organisations; for instance, Park, Reber, and Chon 
(2016) found that Twitter use by health bodies significantly shaped public perception. 
Yet such findings cannot be directly generalised to Abu Dhabi’s distinct social and 
cultural environment, highlighting the need for context-specific research. The COVID-
19 pandemic further underscored the urgency of this inquiry, with Landi et al. (2022) 
noting missed opportunities for dialogic engagement via social media—an area still 
largely unexamined in the Abu Dhabi context. 
Several researchers have called for further investigation into the role of social media 
in public health communication (Burgess et al., 2021). This need is particularly urgent 
in light of recent global public health emergencies and is especially relevant in 
culturally and socially specific settings such as Abu Dhabi. Despite a growing body of 
literature, there remains a notable gap concerning the effects of social media on public 
perception and trust, particularly within this regional context. This study addresses that 
specific gap by offering insights into how social media can be used more effectively to 
build public trust and engagement in Abu Dhabi’s public health sector. 
The extant literature has also demonstrated the role social media can play as a 
mechanism for raising awareness about infection prevention and health promotion 
12 
 
(Madhumathi et al., 2021; Park et al., 2016). While Landi et al. (2022) highlight the 
potential of social media for dialogic engagement during the COVID-19 crisis, its 
influence on health service utilisation and behaviour change in Abu Dhabi has not 
been studied. Ni et al. (2021) stress the importance of contextualising health 
information interactions, noting that access to health knowledge via social media 
varies across cultural settings. As such, there is a significant knowledge gap regarding 
how social media participation affects health behaviour, awareness, and service 
uptake in Abu Dhabi (Takacs et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022). There is limited evidence 
relating to the public health system’s use of social media to communicate with the 
community, and the impact of such communication is also unknown. Even though 
works such as Alamri et al. (2019) and Bhattacharya et al. (2017) have explored some 
aspects of these interactions, a comprehensive examination of particular concerns 
arising within an Abu Dhabi context has not been provided. Little is known about how 
healthcare systems respond to these challenges. While the effectiveness of social 
media as a tool for public engagement and marketing by health organisations has 
been demonstrated (Park et al., 2016), there is currently no evidence of the outcomes 
of such campaigns in terms of community response and satisfaction. 
The influence of social media in driving public health engagement is an emerging field. 
A number of studies, including Ngai et al. (2020) and Keller et al. (2014), examine the 
motivations and barriers to public engagement in social media communication. Yet 
neither of these studies considers the specific context of Abu Dhabi or the extent to 
which the public health system is responsive to local needs. Since these dynamics are 
multifaceted, and the potential to improve public health and community engagement 
in Abu Dhabi has not been thoroughly investigated, the city still requires a more in-
depth examination. 
The influence of social media in driving public health engagement is an emerging field. 
A number of studies, including Ngai et al. (2020) and Keller et al. (2014), examine the 
motivations and barriers to public engagement in social media communication. Yet 
neither of these studies considers the specific context of Abu Dhabi or the extent to 
which the public health system is responsive to local needs. Since these dynamics are 
multifaceted, and the potential to improve public health and community engagement 
in Abu Dhabi has not been thoroughly investigated, the city continues to require a 
deeper examination of how social media-mediated communication affects both 
13 
 
public responses and institutional practices (Massey et al., 2021; Campbell & 
Rudan, 2020). 
1.5 RESEARCH PROBLEM 
Expeditious changes in communication technologies have changed not only the way 
healthcare institutions communicate with their public but also expose several problems 
to provide clear, credible and interactive health information. Rapid developments in 
communication technologies have transformed how healthcare institutions 
interact with the public, but they have also revealed persistent challenges in 
delivering clear, credible, and genuinely interactive health information. Public 
health communication has traditionally followed a top-down, broadcasting model in 
many settings—including Abu Dhabi—where information is communicated by 
authorities to largely passive recipients (Al-Dmour et al., 2020). Although this model 
allows consistency and control over messages, it can stifle opportunities for 
meaningful dialogue, feedback, and the development of public trust—elements that 
are critical to health literacy and behaviour change (Abu-Rish et al., 2018; Alhabash & 
Ma, 2017). 
In recent years, health authorities have embraced social media as an alternative and 
adjunctive means of engagement. But while, in theory, this could democratise 
information-sharing between organisations and the people they serve, there are still 
significant challenges in using these platforms effectively for two-way communication. 
Public health agencies in Abu Dhabi (e.g., DOH and SEHA) have harnessed social 
media platforms like Twitter, Instagram, and Facebook for sharing health news and 
advisories. The nature of interaction is, however, frequently unidirectional, with little 
more than campaign-type awareness-raising and limited evidence of sustained, 
reciprocal public engagement (Al-Kandari & Al-Sumait, 2020). This highlights the 
difficulty of transitioning from a model focused on information dissemination to 
one 
that 
encourages 
interactive, 
trust-building, 
and 
participatory 
communication. 
The COVID-19 pandemic revealed both the shortcomings and potentials of digital 
public health communication. During this period, social media became a lifeline for the 
Abu Dhabi healthcare system in keeping the public informed of ever-changing safety 
protocols, vaccine initiatives, and prevention measures. The speed of these mediums 
14 
 
meant that crucial safety information could be distributed quickly (WHO, 2021). Yet 
the widespread circulation of misinformation and inconsistent public responses 
demonstrated the difficulty of maintaining message accuracy and authority in a 
saturated digital environment (Kemp, 2021). Public scepticism, the spread of 
unverified information, and fluctuating levels of trust underscored the need for 
communication approaches that prioritise transparency, interaction, and 
accountability (Al Khaja et al., 2022). 
Meanwhile, Abu Dhabi’s Vision 2030 agenda towards digital transformation has 
opened up new horizons and higher aspirations in public communication. The 
government’s focus on smart healthcare delivery and digital inclusion underscores a 
realisation that effective communication is fundamental to sustaining public health 
solutions (Department of Health Abu Dhabi, 2022). However, the application of 
interactive communication structures is still emerging, with institutional communication 
primarily involving information exchange rather than user-centred participation, 
collaborative dialogue, or behaviour-reinforcement mechanisms. In addition, 
cultural and social processes are critical in shaping how the population interprets and 
adopts health communications. Research has found that in collective societies like the 
Gulf States, information is influenced less by institutional legitimacy and more by 
network-based trust, communal norms, and shared relational experiences (Al-
Dmour et al., 2020; Alhabash & Ma, 2017). Therefore, the dynamics of online 
interactions between healthcare authorities and the public should be considered not 
only in terms of individual health beliefs but also within the broader context of social 
networks, cultural expectations, and community-based trust. 
The combined use of the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory in this study 
captures this dual aspect: health behaviour is influenced not only by how individuals 
assess risks and benefits but also through shared norms, trust-based mechanisms, 
and socially reinforced cues. The communication task, therefore, becomes not merely 
to deliver correct information but to foster digital interactions that encourage 
awareness, stimulate preventive behaviours, and sustain confidence in 
healthcare institutions. Overcoming this challenge depends on examining how Abu 
Dhabi’s public healthcare sector uses social media to engage with diverse community 
groups, the impact of these interactions on perceived credibility and trust, and the 
barriers that hinder two-way communication. Addressing these gaps, this study 
15 
 
explores the lived experiences of both healthcare workers and members of the 
community to provide a rich account of how social media functions as a mediating 
space for health communication in Abu Dhabi. The research findings aim to inform 
wider discussions on digital public health engagement and offer insights that can 
support the development of community-focused communication strategies by 
identifying what facilitates or obstructs interactive communication. Such an approach 
is particularly relevant for Abu Dhabi, given its post-COVID strategic emphasis on 
resilience, innovation, and improved public trust in the healthcare system. 
1.6 RESEARCH AIM 
This study sets out to assess the role of social media as a tool for communication and 
community engagement within Abu Dhabi's public health system. It also seeks to 
understand its impact on public health outcomes and to analyse the dynamics of 
community interactions and concerns addressed through this medium. 
1.7 RESEARCH OBJECTIVES 
1: To explore the role of interactive social media communications in enhancing 
community engagement within the public health system in Abu Dhabi. 
2: To investigate the effectiveness of social media as a tool for public health 
communication and its impact on the overall perception of the public health system. 
3: To evaluate the impact of social media-enhanced community engagement on public 
health outcomes in Abu Dhabi. 
4: To explore the nature and dynamics of communication between the Abu Dhabi 
public healthcare sector and the community through social media platforms. 
1.8 RESEARCH QUESTION 
Interactive communication via social media is transforming the public health industry, 
as well as contributing to public engagement with the health system in Abu Dhabi. A 
new component of dialogic action has emerged in the interaction between providers 
and users. As a communication medium, social media offers a two-way channel 
through which public health systems disseminate information and, in return, receive 
information and feedback from the community. This reciprocal exchange has fostered 
a more inclusive and participatory public health dialogue, enabling members of the 
16 
 
public to actively engage, voice concerns, and influence discussions about health. 
Relevant issues, health emergencies, and policy interventions are now discussed in a 
highly publicised and transparent manner, attracting attention that supports a 
collective vision for health management and policymaking. These ongoing 
conversations provide not only a means for citizens to share their health-related 
concerns and experiences but also offer health authorities a valuable insight into public 
sentiment and lived experience, potentially aligning health interventions more closely 
with community needs and preferences. 
In contrast, the ability of social media to function effectively in public health 
communication depends largely on whether it can influence public perception and 
build sustained trust. Understanding how digital narratives are produced, 
circulated, and interpreted is therefore essential to assessing how these 
narratives shape community norms, expectations, and health behaviours. This 
intersection of social media engagement and public health outcomes occurs in a 
complex media environment, where public feedback and online narratives can both 
reflect and shape health strategies and policies. Therefore, a detailed investigation is 
needed to explore the main themes and issues emerging from these social media-
facilitated interactions, to understand the extent to which the public health system is 
responsive and adaptive to them, and to analyse the multidimensional effects of digital 
communication on public health dialogue and practice in Abu Dhabi. 
1.9 SIGNIFICANCE OF THE STUDY 
Social Influence Theory, as developed by early scholars such as Kelley (1955) and 
Kiesler et al. (1969), provides a useful theoretical lens through which to understand 
residents’ health behaviours as shaped by community dynamics on social media in 
Abu Dhabi, particularly within a digitalised society. The key components of 
identification, internalisation, and compliance allow for a more nuanced examination 
of how online personalities and digital spaces influence both individual and communal 
health behaviours. Social Influence Theory has frequently been applied to unpack the 
socio-psychological dynamics of internet behaviour and interaction. From the 
perspective of identification, people imitate behaviours to emulate those they love or 
admire (Kelley, 1955). Abu Dhabi’s public health interventions could therefore benefit 
from harnessing the aspirational appeal of social media by carefully shaping how 
17 
 
health messages are communicated. For instance, celebrities and influencers in the 
Emirate could serve as powerful agents in shaping public health behaviour, acting as 
relatable and aspirational figures whose influence extends to health attitudes and 
practices. This highlights how digitally visible role models may contribute to 
reinforcing desirable health norms within the community. 
1.9.1 The Medical Health Belief Model 
Rosenstock originally developed the Health Belief Model (HBM) in 1966, which was 
later extended by Becker (1974). The HBM includes several critical elements closely 
aligned with the objectives of this investigation, and these include: 
• Perceived Susceptibility: This refers to beliefs about the likelihood of 
contracting a disease. The concept is relevant to understanding how risk 
perception influences health-related behaviours, such as compliance with 
preventive measures or acceptance of vaccination. A perceived high level of 
risk may, in principle, act as a motivator for protective health behaviours. 
• Perceived Severity: This involves individual attitudes regarding the 
seriousness of an illness. The research aim aligns with exploring how 
perceptions of severity influence one’s health behaviour and adherence to 
preventive measures. This insight helps illustrate how the immediacy and 
seriousness of a perceived health threat affect the urgency and nature of 
health-promoting action. 
• Beliefs about Outcomes: This includes perceptions of the effectiveness of 
recommended health actions in reducing risk or severity. This study examines 
how both adherence and engagement are shaped by perceived benefits of 
health interventions, offering guidance on how best to promote adherence by 
highlighting the most persuasive features of such actions, including vaccination. 
• Perceived Barriers: This concerns the individual’s belief in the tangible and 
psychological costs of taking recommended actions. It is vital to identify the 
reasons individuals may resist or fail to comply with health recommendations, 
in order to address and overcome these barriers. 
• Cues to Action: These are the prompts or stimuli that motivate an individual to 
adopt a suggested health behaviour. Understanding these signals is essential 
18 
 
to designing interventions that effectively initiate and sustain health-related 
behaviours. 
• Self-Efficacy: This refers to an individual’s belief in their ability to take action 
and overcome obstacles. This sense of self-confidence is a key focus of the 
research, as it sheds light on how belief in one’s capabilities translates into the 
adoption and maintenance of health practices, potentially informing programme 
design aimed at empowering individuals and improving health outcomes. 
1.9.2 Social Influence Theory 
Social Influence Theory, developed by Kelley (1955) and elaborated by Kiesler, 
Collins, and Miller (1969), complements the HBM by accounting for social behavioural 
influences, particularly those mediated by others, including: 
• Identification: This involves aligning oneself with another person's beliefs, 
attitudes, or behaviours. It highlights the influence of admired individuals—such 
as leaders or social media influencers—on health behaviours. Understanding 
this process could enable more effective use of social influencers to promote 
and normalise specific health behaviours, shaping local social norms. 
• Internalisation (Acceptance): This refers to the integration of attitudes or 
values into one’s identity. Exploring internalisation processes is essential for 
understanding how personally resonant beliefs and values can drive health-
protective behaviours beyond mere compliance. 
• Compliance: This involves adopting behaviour to gain rewards or avoid 
punishment. It relates to the investigation of how extrinsic motivators influence 
health practices. Understanding compliance mechanisms can inform the design 
of incentive or deterrent schemes that enhance adherence to recommended 
actions and, in turn, positively influence broader public health outcomes. 
These theories and their components are closely integrated with the research aims, 
forming the theoretical foundation not only for examining the role of social media in 
health communication, but also for developing culturally and socially responsive 
interventions tailored to Abu Dhabi’s context. The combination of the Health Belief 
Model and Social Influence Theory provides this study with a robust, multi-faceted 
theoretical framework, allowing for a deeper understanding of the various roles social 
media plays in shaping health behaviours and community engagement within Abu 
19 
 
Dhabi’s health sector. The HBM supports analysis of how individual health perceptions 
and beliefs, once formed, are transmitted and reiterated through social media, 
subsequently influencing personal health behaviours. In contrast, Social Influence 
Theory offers a conceptual lens for understanding how collective actions emerge and 
evolve through social media interactions. This dual perspective enables a 
comprehensive examination of both individual and collective psychosocial processes 
that drive health behaviours and community engagement on digital platforms. The 
terms used throughout this thesis—such as Echo Governance, Discursive Pulse, 
Perceptual Cartography, Dialogic Resonance, and others—are not externally imported 
theoretical constructs. Rather, they are analytic codes and higher-order themes 
generated inductively through the qualitative analysis of participant data. These 
expressions emerged from the systematic coding, categorisation, and thematic 
development stages of the study and therefore reflect the language, meanings, and 
interpretive patterns present within the dataset itself. Their purpose is to represent, as 
closely as possible, the conceptual structures inherent in the participants’ accounts, 
and they are defined and elaborated in detail in Chapter 4 alongside the full thematic 
framework that underpins the study. 
Together, these approaches strengthen the study’s capacity to explore health 
communication in a manner that recognises both individual cognition and the 
wider social environment in which digital interactions occur. This composite 
theoretical approach not only enhances the study’s capacity to explore culturally and 
socially contextualised health communication strategies but also strengthens its ability 
to assess the complex impact social media can have on health beliefs and behaviours 
in a digitally engaged and socially dynamic environment like Abu Dhabi. By carefully 
integrating these theories, the study aims to generate strategies that are theoretically 
sound and deeply rooted in the social and cultural patterns of the community it seeks 
to reach and support, ultimately informing and improving the effectiveness of public 
health communication and interventions via social media. 
1.10 STRUCTURE OF THESIS 
The present thesis has been structured in six interrelated chapters, to be developed 
in a congruent, progressive and scientifically grounded manner. The organisational 
framework mirrors the pattern of thinking from conceptualisation through to empirical 
20 
 
analysis and theoretical integration, thus helping to fulfil the research aim and 
objectives. The present study investigates social media as a vehicle for interaction and 
engagement between Abu Dhabi’s public healthcare system and the broader 
community, providing an integrated understanding of communication dynamics, trust 
and behavioural influence in digital health. 
Chapter One – Introduction – provides the background, purpose and significance of 
the research. It starts with a discussion on the growing significance of social media as 
part of health communication in the context of global and regional developments. The 
conversation places the public health system in Abu Dhabi within the broader national 
vision for digital transformation and citizen engagement across the United Arab 
Emirates. The chapter then outlines the research problem: understanding how two-
way communication between health authorities and the public via social media affects 
public awareness, engagement and trust in health messaging. It also describes the 
aim of the study, which is to explore how social media can facilitate effective two-way 
communication between healthcare organisations and the public. The four research 
objectives inform the investigation, specifically focusing on understanding the 
processes involved in social media-mediated interactions between health 
professionals and the public. 
The rationale of the study (and an overview of its practical insights for policymakers, 
health professionals and communication strategists) is addressed in the introduction. 
It contributes to the scholarship by situating the study in relation to wider discourses 
in digital communication, public health promotion and social influence theories. The 
final part of Chapter One summarises the thesis section by section and sets the 
context for each new chapter, leading to the conclusion of this opening chapter. 
Chapter Two: Literature Review 
In Chapter Two, the literature pertinent to this study is reviewed with an appraisal of 
social media implementation in healthcare environments. The review starts by 
examining social media as a communication channel and its historical transition from 
transmitting one-way information to serving as platforms for public engagement. Next, 
the chapter reflects on international and regional research exploring the consequences 
of social media for health education campaigns, crisis communication and community 
trust-building. 
21 
 
Emphasis is given to the theoretical approaches underpinning the investigation, 
following closely the concepts of the Health Belief Model (HBM) and Social Influence 
Theory (SIT). The text analyses these frameworks in detail and discusses their 
conceptual nuances, limitations and interrelationships. It describes how the HBM 
addresses individual health behaviour relative to perceived threats and benefits, and 
how SIT incorporates the influence of peer behaviour, social norms and trust networks 
on decision-making. This theoretical convergence supports the model combination 
used in the study. 
In addition, the chapter introduces alternative theoretical approaches such as the 
Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) and the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM), 
providing justification for not adopting them. The review ends with a discussion of 
major research gaps—particularly how social media interactivity, credibility and 
engagement function in healthcare communication settings outside Western 
environments such as Abu Dhabi. These gaps provide the conceptual underpinning of 
the methodology and analysis in this study. 
Chapter Three: Research Methodology 
Chapter Three describes the methodological framework used to meet the aims of this 
study. It starts by outlining the study’s philosophical stance, which lies within an 
interpretivist approach that emphasises subjective meaning, context and lived 
experience. This approach supports the use of qualitative methods—specifically 
semi-structured interviews—to ensure rich insights from healthcare workers and 
community members within Abu Dhabi. 
The Research Design section explains the two phases of data collection, including 
information on sampling and justification for the inclusion of two separate participant 
groups to reflect both professional and community perspectives. The demographic and 
professional characteristics of the participants are displayed in Tables 4.1 and 4.2 
(later referenced in Chapter Four), including roles, experience, gender and patterns of 
social media use. 
Ethical issues are clearly addressed, including consent, privacy and data security. The 
section also explains that translation was not required, as all participants were English-
speaking, thus avoiding potential bias in interpretation. The analysis method adopts 
thematic analysis, guided by Naeem et al. (2023), to ensure consistency in coding, 
22 
 
theme generation and interpretation. The chapter concludes by outlining the credibility 
and confirmability of the study through triangulation, reflexivity and transparent 
reporting to support transferability. 
Chapter Four: Data analysis and results 
The empirical findings based on the qualitative interviews are presented in Chapter 
Four. The chapter begins by describing the sample characteristics and distribution of 
participants. The mention of treatment and control groups is not applicable in 
qualitative research, and this section has been clarified accordingly. The 
presentation is thematic, capturing themes that emerged from participants’ accounts. 
Each theme is grounded in participants’ voices, adding authenticity and contextual 
depth to the findings. 
The results highlight several critical areas of interaction between the public health 
system and the community through social media, including access to health 
information, interactivity and feedback mechanisms, perceived credibility of online 
communication and emotional engagement with public health messaging. The chapter 
contrasts the views of healthcare professionals and community members on the role 
of social media in health engagement. 
Thematic analysis is applied consistently across themes aligned with each research 
question. In addition, the chapter integrates relevant literature when discussing 
findings, offering a nuanced appraisal that connects empirical insights with existing 
knowledge. The concluding part of the chapter integrates these findings to show 
how digital communication practices influence trust, participation and health 
awareness in Abu Dhabi. 
Chapter Five: Discussion and Conceptual Framework Building 
Chapter Five consolidates the main results with the theoretical frameworks and 
literature in order to elaborate a new conceptual model. It begins with a restatement 
of the conceptual model presented in Chapter Two and demonstrates how it has been 
tested, refined and expanded through empirical analysis. Figure 5.1 illustrates the 
proposed combined framework between HBM and Social Influence Theory. 
The chapter explains how each construct in the model is supported by themes, 
participant quotations and observed patterns from the findings. For example, 
23 
 
perceived susceptibility and cues to action relate to HBM, while social validation, trust 
and credibility relate to SIT. This synthesis results in a refined model of social media-
supported health engagement in Abu Dhabi. 
The theoretical and practical implications of the model are discussed, showing how it 
provides a deeper understanding of digital communication in healthcare. Where 
appropriate, references have been updated to reflect recent developments in 
public health informatics and social media analytics. The discussion critically 
reflects on the alignment and discrepancies between theory and evidence. Figure 5.1 
therefore represents a key theoretical outcome of the research, visually summarising 
the complex relationships between communication, beliefs and social influences. 
Chapter Six: Conclusion, Contributions and Future Work 
Chapter Six highlights the contributions of the study. It re-emphasises how the 
research aims have been achieved by summarising the key insights. It then outlines 
the theoretical contribution of Figure 5.1 as an applied conceptual model of social 
media engagement in healthcare, adding to current scholarship in digital public health 
communication. 
The study’s limitations are acknowledged openly, including issues relating to sample 
size, geographical scope and the generalisability associated with qualitative research. 
The Future Research section proposes how the developed model might be tested 
quantitatively or applied in other cultural or institutional contexts. The chapter 
concludes by reinforcing the wider implications of the research and its contribution to 
communication strategy, policy development and public engagement within the UAE 
and beyond. 
1.11 SUMMARY  
Any examination of the role of social media in public health discourse and outcomes 
in Abu Dhabi necessitates an in-depth investigation into the complex dynamics of 
community engagement and policy-making in the health sector. These processes are 
increasingly shaped, if not transformed, by interactive communication via social media 
platforms. Within this communication model, social media outlets function as two-way 
conduits for information, both disseminating health messages and gathering critical 
24 
 
voices and feedback from the community. This interactive system fosters a more 
democratic public health dialogue, enabling individuals to actively participate, express 
views and serve as essential actors in health discussions and policy development. 
In analysing the communication environment shaped by social media, a critical issue 
emerges concerning the potential of these platforms to influence public opinion and 
trust in the health system. The process by which narratives crafted and circulated 
through social media penetrate public consciousness and shape community 
behaviours and attitudes towards health remains a key area of inquiry. The dynamic 
interplay between social media-driven community engagement and public health 
occurs within a complex context, where public opinion and individual stories possess 
the capacity to both reflect and inform health strategies and policy decisions. This 
relationship warrants a focused investigation into the central themes and issues raised 
through social media exchanges, alongside a critical analysis of the responsiveness 
and adaptability of the public health system to these concerns. Such inquiry is 
essential for understanding the multi-layered effects of social media-enabled 
communication on public health discourse and practice in Abu Dhabi. 
To explore these issues, this study poses a series of questions aimed at understanding 
the extent to which social media and community engagement within the healthcare 
system interact in a reciprocal manner. Key areas of inquiry include the interactivity of 
communication via social media platforms and its impact on community engagement, 
the effectiveness of social media as a communication tool in the public health sector, 
the influence of social media-facilitated community engagement on health outcomes 
and the most prevalent themes and issues raised by the community through these 
interactions. Addressing these questions provides insight into the mechanisms 
through which social media communication affects public perceptions and behaviours, 
as well as the pathways through which it may influence individual health behaviours 
and community participation. 
This research highlights the importance of a strong theoretical foundation by 
employing two key models: the HBM and Social Influence Theory. These models serve 
as central frameworks for understanding and interpreting the impact of social media 
on both personal and collective health behaviours. The HBM, through its constructs—
perceived susceptibility, perceived severity, perceived benefits, perceived barriers, 
25 
 
cues to action and self-efficacy—offers a means to examine how individuals assess 
their vulnerability to health issues and what motivates their behavioural responses. It 
provides insight into personal health beliefs and how these beliefs, when mediated 
through social media, may influence individual health behaviours. 
With the concepts of identification, internalisation and compliance as its foundation, 
Social Influence Theory offers a framework for understanding how collective 
behaviours emerge and evolve through social media interactions. It provides a robust 
perspective on the socio-psychological processes that underpin health behaviours and 
community participation on social media. In a setting such as Abu Dhabi, which is 
characterised by an active social and digital culture, it is essential to consider local 
belief systems and social structures in the development of health communications. 
Effective communication must resonate with target audiences, be internalised by the 
community and organically promote engagement and adherence to health norms and 
practices. 
By combining the Health Belief Model with Social Influence Theory, this study is 
grounded in a strong and adaptable theoretical framework capable of deconstructing 
the multiple channels through which social media influences health behaviours and 
community involvement within Abu Dhabi’s health landscape. This integrated lens 
supports the development of culturally and socially responsive health communication 
strategies. It also critically assesses the layered impacts of social media on health 
beliefs and behaviours in a digitally connected and socially dynamic context. The 
careful integration of these theories positions the study to generate strategies that are 
theoretically sound, culturally attuned and socially embedded. This maximises the 
impact and reach of public health communication and intervention through social 
media.

2.1 INTRODUCTION  
Digital media has undoubtedly transformed healthcare communication, playing a 
significant role in community engagement and the evolution of patient-centred models 
of care. This chapter draws on a range of literature and some empirical evidence to 
provide a comprehensive synthesis of the intersection between social media 
platforms, healthcare communication, and public engagement. The aim is to construct 
a cohesive narrative that outlines the historical development, current use, and future 
projections of social media in the healthcare sector. The next section begins by 
discussing the historical trajectory of social media in healthcare and explores how 
various 
applications 
have 
functioned 
as 
tools 
for 
enhancing 
healthcare 
communication. It highlights the increasingly influential role social media has 
played in connecting healthcare systems with communities. 
The following section discusses the role of community engagement and patient-
centred care and describes how social media acts as an enabler for a more democratic 
model of healthcare. It outlines the definition of patient-centred care and examines the 
influence of social media on community engagement and the individualisation of 
healthcare. A later section expands on the potential of social media as a tool for public 
social engagement, detailing the possibilities, limitations, and opportunities associated 
with this form of interaction. Another section addresses public participation within the 
broader social context, placing emphasis on social dynamics and the role of 
community identity in fostering healthcare communities. The latter half of the chapter 
then shifts to a discussion of public health branding through social 
engagement, clarifying the concept and its associated taxonomy. This leads into 
a further section examining social brand engagement and public trust, exploring the 
relationship between branding strategies, social media engagement, and their 
influence on public trust and credibility. Based on a systematic review of existing 
literature, the concluding section summarises the key findings, highlights existing 
research gaps, outlines directions for future research, and offers a call to recognise 
the growing and long-term impact of social media on healthcare communication and 
public engagement. 
27 
 
2.2 CONCEPTUALIZATION OF SOCIAL MEDIA IN PUBLIC HEALTH  
Social media refers to online technologies and practices used to connect people, 
enable communication, and facilitate the dissemination of information among users. It 
encompasses a variety of platforms, including Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, and 
LinkedIn, and has been employed to support real-time communication between 
communities and organisations such as the police (Chen et al., 2019; Ozuem et al., 
2023). 
Healthcare communication is defined as the process of sharing health information 
among healthcare providers, patients, and the wider public to improve health 
outcomes (Pangborn et al., 2023). Bringing these together, social media serves as a 
modern platform for healthcare communication, enabling the enhancement of public 
health messaging, patient engagement, and information exchange (Lai & Chong, 
2023). As noted by Chen et al. (2019), when compared with earlier reviews such as 
that published in 2013, social media has undergone significant thematic evolution, 
further highlighting its dynamic and expanding role in the healthcare sector. 
Focusing on Abu Dhabi, this study seeks to gain a more nuanced insight into the 
relationship between social media communication and public engagement. Given the 
widespread use and significant influence of social media, it has the potential to drive 
increased community involvement, promote patient-centred care, and enhance health 
branding across Abu Dhabi’s healthcare sector (Koumpouros et al., 2015). Social 
media can improve health communication by serving as a real-time, interactive 
platform for health-related discussions, influencing health behaviours, and fostering 
user communities (Huo et al., 2019). It also functions as a medium through which 
healthcare professionals can exchange information, respond to patient queries, and 
gain insights from patient feedback, facilitating two-way communication that can lead 
to improved health outcomes (Belt et al., 2013). Gesser-Edelsburg (2021) highlights 
the importance of strengthening online health and risk communication, particularly in 
times of crisis such as the COVID-19 pandemic, noting that social media interactivity 
could be more fully leveraged. 
Taking this into account alongside the research objectives above, this study aims to 
conceptualise public social engagement via social media as a crucial component in 
branding public health, building trust, and enhancing the credibility of public health 
28 
 
services in Abu Dhabi. It also seeks to understand the social context of public 
engagement within social media communities focused on public health, consistent with 
recommendations by Freire et al. (2023) to “use social information in participatory 
communication,” as demonstrated in their study on the influence of social media use 
on patient selection of healthcare providers. 
‘Reputation in healthcare,’ within the context of incorporating social media into 
healthcare communication, has received mixed portrayals across the sector (Chaudhri 
et al., 2021), shaping public perceptions of healthcare providers and services. In 
narrative-driven cultures, the storytelling that emerges through social media from 
frontline healthcare workers has played a significant role in shaping public 
understanding and representation of healthcare experiences during pandemics 
(Pangborn et al., 2023). However, the use of social media in healthcare 
communication is not without its challenges. Issues related to professionalism and the 
potential for healthcare risk have emerged, highlighting the need for critical and 
responsible use of social media in health-related communication (Chen et al., 2021). 
Drawing on the cultural setting of Abu Dhabi, this study incorporates considerations of 
cultural appropriateness by recognising how values, norms, religious beliefs, and 
practices influence both social media use and public health communication. Social 
media serves as a vital tool for investigating interactive communication strategies that 
support community engagement and patient-centred care within Abu Dhabi’s 
healthcare context. Acknowledging the importance of patient engagement and the 
transformative potential of social media in healthcare, this project introduces a 
comprehensive framework for examining the relationship between social media and 
healthcare communication. It aims to explore the effects of this relationship on public 
engagement, patient-centred care, and trust in the public healthcare system in Abu 
Dhabi. By assessing the extent to which social brand engagement influences public 
health, the study offers implications for enhancing public trust and credibility in 
healthcare services, thereby contributing to a deeper understanding of social media’s 
role in healthcare communication. 
The interactivity that underpins social media platforms triggers a two-way flow of 
information and insights and fits within the spirit of healthcare communication, which 
ideally should be multi-directional, involving communication across and between 
29 
 
health senders, receivers, and the general public (Chen et al., 2019). As Abu Dhabi’s 
healthcare continues to evolve and change, the use of social media strengthens the 
ability to engage and reach different groups, which in turn results in more inclusive, 
versatile healthcare communication (Lai & Chong, 2023). Social media is, and 
increasingly will be, a disruptor in healthcare communication processes. It has played 
a significant role in expanding discussions beyond traditional patient–provider 
interactions to include community dialogue, forum-based exchanges, and participatory 
healthcare conversations, thereby supporting the development of collective learning 
and shared decision-making (Koumpouros et al., 2015). This broader dialogue is 
particularly vital in a polyglot society such as that of Abu Dhabi, which encompasses 
diverse demographic layers and constitutes a rich and multifaceted public sphere. 
Due to the interactive nature of social media platforms, there are increasing 
opportunities for healthcare professionals to extend the reach of health information 
and raise awareness about health issues, thereby contributing to the advancement of 
patient-centred care and the development of public health branding within Abu Dhabi’s 
healthcare sector (Chaudhri et al., 2021). These platforms serve as hubs that 
interconnect disparate entities within the healthcare ecosystem, fostering cooperation, 
facilitating knowledge transfer, and helping to bridge gaps in public health 
communication. In doing so, they strengthen the mutually beneficial relationship 
between patients and providers (Pangborn et al., 2023). 
Understanding these underlying dynamics is critical when examining the nuances of 
health-related dialogue on social media. The role of social media in fostering public 
trust necessitates an exploration of the complex relationships among providers, the 
public, and the media (Chen et al., 2021). Through an interpretative approach, this 
examination investigates the intricate performances and practices of social interaction, 
uncovering the layered meanings embedded in the healthcare communication 
landscape of Abu Dhabi. 
In comparison to previous work, the thematic analysis in this study focuses specifically 
on aspects such as social media communication and its impact on public trust, public 
perceptions, and the overall branding of public health services in Abu Dhabi (Chen et 
al., 2019). Through a detailed exploration of these themes, a clearer understanding 
30 
 
will emerge of how social media shapes healthcare communication dynamics and 
influences societal perceptions of public health. 
This process has the potential to transform healthcare communication practices by 
reinforcing the importance of social engagement and collaborative learning within a 
community of practice. When social media intersects with healthcare communication, 
the combined influence can significantly reshape communication norms, leaving little 
room for traditional models to remain unchanged. 
This study may also generate practical recommendations related to public health 
messaging, digital engagement, and communication strategy. These insights can 
inform stakeholders, government agencies, healthcare providers, and individuals, 
enabling more effective decision-making regarding the role of social media in 
healthcare communication. In turn, this can help foster improved public engagement, 
enhance patient experiences, and increase trust in Abu Dhabi’s public health services. 
In addition, the study examines the challenges and barriers associated with using 
social media in healthcare communication, with particular emphasis on 
misinformation, privacy concerns, and ethical issues. This approach provides a holistic 
perspective on the advantages and limitations of employing social media to strengthen 
healthcare communication (Chen et al., 2021). By applying a reflective lens to these 
risks and engaging with the ethical and responsible use of social media in healthcare, 
the study contributes depth and fresh insight into the broader discourse on the 
potential and pitfalls of digital communication in an evolving healthcare landscape. 
Through a rigorous analysis of the interconnections between social media and 
healthcare communication, the study addresses the research aims and delivers a 
detailed account of how social media interactions shape healthcare delivery, public 
participation, and trust in Abu Dhabi’s healthcare system. These findings will be of 
relevance to healthcare professionals, policymakers, and the public, as all 
stakeholders become increasingly engaged in shaping a more integrated, inclusive, 
and innovative model of health communication in the digital era.  
31 
 
2.3 SOCIAL MEDIA IN HEALTHCARE 
The intersection of social media and healthcare represents a major shift in how the 
public engages with health communication, and it is highly relevant to the aims and 
objectives of this research. This development reflects the broader spread of digital 
technologies across industries, particularly within Abu Dhabi’s healthcare sector, 
where progress and persistent challenges coexist. The rise of healthcare-focused 
social media has created a strategic platform for the dissemination and exchange of 
health-related knowledge. In its early stages, social media primarily facilitated 
communication between healthcare professionals and patients (Belt et al., 2013). Its 
contribution to supporting patient-centred care has since expanded, reinforced by 
increasing levels of community participation (Chaudhri et al., 2021). 
According to Chen et al. (2019), there has been a significant shift from one-way patient 
education toward more interactive and reciprocal communication between patients 
and healthcare providers, with this interactivity influencing behaviour and encouraging 
greater involvement in personal health management (Koumpouros et al., 2015). The 
dynamic role of social media becomes especially prominent during crises such as the 
COVID-19 pandemic, where patient narratives have added nuance and depth to public 
understanding of frontline healthcare challenges (Pangborn et al., 2023). However, 
integrating social media into healthcare systems is not without difficulty. Issues such 
as inappropriate disclosure of personal health information (PHI) and the rapid spread 
of misinformation have prompted the development of professional guidelines to 
manage these risks (Chen et al., 2021). 
Despite these challenges, social media has contributed to maintaining public trust, 
strengthening health branding, and increasing participation in community events and 
health campaigns (Chaudhri et al., 2021; Lai & Chong, 2023). In Abu Dhabi, social 
media plays a particularly significant role in advancing patient-centred care and 
shaping public health branding. It supports more personalised communication, 
builds trust in healthcare services, and fosters positive relationships between patients, 
communities, and healthcare providers (Gesser-Edelsburg, 2021). This multifaceted 
impact—spanning patient engagement, public health branding, and trust in services—
makes social media an increasingly integral part of health management and delivery. 
32 
 
The historical development of social media in healthcare, from an initial “information 
phase” focused on simple dissemination to its current role in fostering patient-provider 
partnerships and community-level engagement, illustrates its potential to address 
concerns related to misinformation and privacy (Belt et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2021). 
Public trust and engagement strategies have adapted in response to shifting 
communication practices, particularly during public health emergencies (Pangborn et 
al., 2023; Gesser-Edelsburg, 2021). This evolution positions social media as a vital 
tool for crisis communication and trust-building in healthcare contexts. This transition 
is particularly significant in Abu Dhabi, where rapid healthcare expansion and rising 
expectations have shaped how social media is understood and used. As the 
healthcare system has developed, social media has played a key role in informing 
public perception and engagement with services (Huo et al., 2019; Freire et al., 2023). 
The expanding role of these platforms highlights their growing importance in shaping 
health discourse, public involvement, and trust within the community. 
2.4 SOCIAL MEDIA SITES FOR HEALTHCARE COMMUNICATION 
Using social networking sites as health education communication tools among UAE 
college students presents important implications and challenges within Abu Dhabi’s 
current healthcare environment. Social networking has become a significant element 
of healthcare communication and holds particular relevance for the Emirate. Platforms 
such as Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, LinkedIn, Reddit, and TikTok have become 
essential bridges for facilitating healthcare communication (Eghtesadi & Florea, 2020). 
These systems offer transformative potential by promoting patient-centred care, 
strengthening community involvement, and enhancing trust in healthcare systems. 
The widespread use and interactive nature of platforms such as Facebook, Instagram, 
and Twitter have reshaped the healthcare communication landscape (Eghtesadi & 
Florea, 2020). LinkedIn, in particular, has become a major professional networking 
space within the healthcare sector (Eghtesadi & Florea, 2020). The informal, real-time 
communication style of platforms like TikTok and Reddit is increasingly being 
harnessed to modernise public health messaging and respond quickly to emerging 
needs (Eghtesadi & Florea, 2020). These platforms enable diverse forms of interaction 
and appeal to different user groups, making them effective tools for healthcare 
33 
 
outreach. Facebook and Instagram—both highly visual—offer immersive user 
experiences and have played a key role in contingency planning during global 
pandemics by communicating critical health messages (Eghtesadi & Florea, 2020). 
Their adoption aligns with the broader aim of improving patient-centred care, 
expanding public health branding, and enhancing public trust (Hu et al., 2019). 
Given their real-time interactivity and extensive reach, social media platforms have 
become important vehicles for strengthening community engagement. This aligns with 
the first research objective, which examines how interactive communication via social 
media can enhance community involvement (Miah et al., 2017). Such platforms create 
direct communication channels between healthcare professionals and the public, 
influencing perceptions and attitudes toward health and healthcare services (Hu et al., 
2019). Their ability to shape public discourse and construct the image of healthcare 
provision links directly to the second and fourth research objectives (Nabi et al., 2023). 
However, 
despite 
these 
benefits, 
challenges 
remain—particularly 
around 
professionalism, privacy, misinformation, and ethical considerations (Torous et al., 
2019). Addressing these challenges requires robust digital literacy initiatives, 
ethical guidance, and strong regulatory measures to ensure responsible and informed 
social media use in healthcare contexts (Torous et al., 2019; Jeyaraman et al., 2023). 
A multi-layered strategy is needed to mitigate risks and harness the potential of these 
platforms. This includes ethical guidelines, digital literacy training, and rigorous content 
validation frameworks. Such steps are essential for maintaining trust and safeguarding 
the integrity of healthcare communication (Torous et al., 2019). Proactive monitoring, 
ethical oversight, and public education will further reinforce responsible use (Choo et 
al., 2015). Social media has significantly reshaped communication within Abu Dhabi’s 
healthcare landscape. It has enabled greater community participation, 
strengthened patient-centred care, and contributed to the branding of public health 
services through the interactivity and reach of platforms such as Facebook, Instagram, 
LinkedIn, Twitter, Reddit, and TikTok. However, concerns around professionalism and 
ethics require continued attention to ensure the responsible use of these tools. 
34 
 
2.5 COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT IN HEALTHCARE, DEFINED 
The multiple meanings of community engagement in healthcare, as discussed by 
Ozuem and Willis (2022), reflect the complex and context-dependent nature of the 
concept. Comparing the different perspectives offered by researchers enables a 
clearer examination of how various levels of involvement occur across diverse 
communities and healthcare contexts. In Berger’s (2019) model of community 
engagement, quantitative metrics play a central role, serving as tangible indicators of 
success in health engagement initiatives. This emphasis is important for aligning 
engagement activities with intended outcomes, ensuring that involvement efforts 
remain meaningful and measurable. 
However, a metrics-driven approach may risk overlooking the emotional and 
experiential aspects of community participation—elements that are vital for 
understanding individual and collective needs, motivations, and expectations. The 
study by Duku et al. (2022) is particularly valuable in showing the economic 
consequences of community involvement in healthcare, especially in socio-
economically diverse settings. These definitions help clarify how community 
engagement influences healthcare utilisation and access. While such approaches 
offer critical insights into structural and economic outcomes (Ozuem & Rafangni, 
2021), they may fall short in capturing the subjective, relational, and human 
dimensions that shape the real-life impact of community participation. 
Alicea-Alvarez et al. (2016) and Durey et al. (2016) emphasise the significance of 
urban and Aboriginal community perspectives, highlighting the importance of 
contextually sensitive strategies that engage entire families and ensure inclusivity 
across diverse populations. Berger (2019) also stresses the value of community 
involvement within broader measurement frameworks, considering how engagement 
metrics inform healthcare decisions at both organisational and systemic levels (Ozuem 
et al., 2020). Although this perspective supports a predominantly quantitative 
approach to evaluating community engagement (Ozuem & Bowen, 2019), it risks 
narrowing the understanding of engagement by overlooking lived experience. 
Duku et al. (2022) examine the role of community engagement in healthcare utilisation 
and health insurance enrolment in Ghana, drawing attention to how social involvement 
35 
 
shapes access in resource-constrained settings. While their analysis provides crucial 
economic insights, the focus on financial outcomes may limit a holistic appreciation 
of community engagement as a multidimensional construct that includes cultural, 
emotional, and relational components. In contrast, Alicea-Alvarez et al. (2016) 
introduce the concept of “Neighbourhood-Engaged Care,” which highlights the active 
work required of healthcare providers to engage communities in addressing health 
disparities. While this definition contributes to a learning-oriented approach, it may 
frame engagement too narrowly in educational terms, without fully recognising the 
broader social structures affecting participation. 
Durey et al. (2016), focusing on Indigenous child health in Australia, advocate for 
culturally grounded, context-specific approaches. Although valuable, their contribution 
is constrained by its narrow demographic focus and may not fully translate to other 
contexts. Reboe-Benjamin et al. (2023) examine community participation among 
refugees and their engagement with general health services. Their research 
foregrounds the experiences of marginalised populations (Ozuem & Azemi, 2018), yet 
such case-specific insights may not always capture the broader realities of community 
engagement across all healthcare settings. 
The complexity of these conceptualisations aligns closely with the present research 
aim of exploring how social media dialogism intersects with public engagement in Abu 
Dhabi’s healthcare sector. Multiple definitions highlight the varied dimensions of 
community engagement (Ozuem et al., 2021b), from economic considerations to 
individual-level interactions, each relevant to analysing the impact of interactive 
communication via social media (Navarro et al., 2020; Koumpouros et al., 2015). 
Berger’s (2019) metrics-based approach may be particularly useful for evaluating 
social media as a communication tool for public engagement (Ozuem et al., 2021c), 
especially when assessing measurable forms of social interaction and public health 
branding. 
Context-specific definitions from Durey et al. (2016) and Alicea-Alvarez et al. (2016), 
which incorporate aspects such as the social context of participants and the influence 
of social capital, offer valuable perspectives on how public participation in social media 
communities may unfold within Abu Dhabi’s healthcare environment. These 
36 
 
frameworks support the investigation of patient-focused care and frame public social 
engagement as a mechanism for strengthening public health branding. 
Insights from studies such as Reboe-Benjamin et al. (2023), which explore refugee 
experiences in healthcare, help illustrate how social brand engagement can enhance 
trust and credibility in Abu Dhabi’s public health services. Collectively, these diverse 
interpretations of community engagement contribute to a more pragmatic and 
contextually sensitive understanding of inclusive engagement across varied 
healthcare settings. Such insights are essential for evaluating how social media 
platforms foster community participation, support patient- and family-centred care, and 
reinforce trust and credibility within Abu Dhabi’s healthcare system. 
2.6 PATIENT-CENTRED CARE: THE BASICS 
Patient-centred care focuses on involving patients and their families as active 
participants—not only in shaping new care models but also in making decisions 
regarding the most appropriate treatment options for their individual needs (Berger, 
2019). This model is fundamental to improving the quality of care by fostering trust 
and mutual understanding between patients and healthcare providers. Its relevance 
to this research is considerable, as it underpins how social media interactions and 
public engagement can strengthen public health in Abu Dhabi. 
Patient-centred care aligns closely with the first research objective, which explores 
how interactive communication via social media can support this care model within 
Abu Dhabi’s healthcare system. A thorough understanding of patient-centred care is 
essential for assessing the influence of social media communication and community 
engagement on public health branding and public confidence in healthcare providers 
across the emirate. Tailoring healthcare delivery to reflect individual preferences and 
expectations is critical for improving both service quality and health outcomes (Durey 
et al., 2016). Platforms such as Facebook, widely recognised as leading social media
