tools, can enhance community involvement and support patient-centred care by 
enabling two-way dialogue between healthcare providers and the public (Koumpouros 
et al., 2015). Such interaction allows for a deeper understanding of patient 
perspectives and concerns. Encouraging dialogue, conversation, and feedback 
through social media significantly strengthens patient engagement, reinforcing the 
37 
 
effectiveness of these platforms as communication tools and promoting a patient-
centred care model (Bornkessel et al., 2014). Accurate measurement of public social 
engagement requires analysing these interactions and the broader social context in 
which they occur. 
Understanding community needs, expectations, and preferences is essential for 
designing patient-centred care models that reflect patient values (Navarro et al., 2020). 
Framing public social engagement as a catalyst for patient-centred care is crucial, as 
it embeds a more empathetic, inclusive, and culturally relevant image of public health 
within societal consciousness (Farley et al., 2021). In this context, social brand 
engagement plays a significant role in building public trust and reinforcing the 
legitimacy of healthcare services by ensuring that patients feel respected, heard, and 
meaningfully involved (Powell et al., 2016). Social media also supports patient-centred 
care by fostering ongoing communication and reconnection between healthcare 
providers and communities. Such interaction is central to developing a truly patient-
centred healthcare system (Alicea-Alvarez et al., 2016). However, the potential 
benefits of using social media in this context depend on healthcare providers’ 
willingness to engage meaningfully and authentically with patients. Without such 
engagement, the transformative potential of social media will remain limited (Doherty, 
2023). 
Engagement involves more than interaction; it encompasses the value derived from 
those exchanges (Berger, 2019). The progression of patient-centred care in social 
media dialogue is rooted in trust—an essential factor in establishing credibility and 
legitimacy for public health services. This is especially important when addressing the 
healthcare needs of Abu Dhabi’s diverse population (Opoku Duku et al., 2022). The 
study’s focus on patient-centred care provides a foundation for examining the multiple 
dimensions of social media communication and community engagement within Abu 
Dhabi’s healthcare sector. The interaction of these elements contributes to the 
emergence of a more responsive and culturally informed healthcare system, ultimately 
strengthening patient-centred care, public health branding, and trust in healthcare 
services across the region. 
It is important to consider the role of social media in promoting patient-centred care 
within Abu Dhabi’s healthcare context, where community engagement is crucial for 
38 
 
enhancing the effectiveness of health systems. Berger (2019) emphasises the 
complexity of engagement, noting that it may include patient, public, consumer, and 
community dimensions. Social media serves as a valuable medium for two-way 
communication, with the capacity to increase community involvement, enhance 
patient-centred care, and address urban health disparities (Berger, 2019; Alicea-
Alvarez et al., 2016). As a dynamic communication tool, social media holds strategic 
relevance for strengthening public social engagement and broadening participation in 
healthcare discourse (Navarro et al., 2020). Emerging forms of digital engagement 
also contribute meaningfully to clinical outcomes and healthcare delivery. 
Understanding the cultural context in which the public engages with social media 
communities requires an examination of the broader social environment and regional 
public health agendas (Durey et al., 2016). The relationship between communities and 
health systems is central to improving healthcare services in densely populated and 
culturally diverse settings. Public social engagement also acts as a driver of public 
health branding, shaping how collective identity and public perception develop over 
time. Bornkessel, Furberg, and Lefebvre (2014) highlight the potential of social media 
to support quality improvement and patient-centred care through sustained digital 
engagement. Their networked model underscores the importance of incorporating 
patient-driven perspectives and consolidating public health branding within digital 
environments. 
2.7 THE CONTEXT OF USING SOCIAL BRAND ENGAGEMENT (SBE) 
The importance of social brand engagement in building public confidence and trust in 
Abu Dhabi’s healthcare system has become increasingly evident. Koumpouros, 
Toulias, and Koumpouros (2015) highlight the role of patient engagement and social 
media marketing in addressing specific healthcare needs and improving patient 
satisfaction. Social media enables ongoing dialogue between healthcare providers 
and the community, fostering a relationship built on trust, transparency, and 
reputational credibility. 
The integration of social media into healthcare processes has contributed to a shift 
toward more mainstream models of care that emphasise patient experience and self-
reported health, including among refugee populations (Reboe-Benjamin et al., 2023). 
39 
 
The inclusion of peer community health workers in community mobilisation has also 
been important in addressing barriers to preventive interventions within diverse 
populations (Farley et al., 2021). Investigating the relationship between social media 
communication and community engagement reveals how evolving digital dynamics 
influence patient-centred care, public health branding, and trust. This analysis helps 
to explain how social media reshaping healthcare delivery in Abu Dhabi is and 
underscores the growing importance of adopting patient-centric approaches to 
strengthen community engagement and public trust.  
Social media has become an integral part of daily life and has had a significant impact 
on community engagement across sectors, including healthcare. Its capacity to foster 
dialogue, knowledge exchange, and connection makes it a valuable tool for healthcare 
professionals, patients, and citizens. Social media platforms offer spaces for 
individuals to engage, learn, and build relationships within the wider healthcare 
community. Among the most widely used platforms in this area—Twitter, LinkedIn, and 
Facebook—there is considerable potential to enhance quality improvement initiatives 
and support patient-centred care (Bornkessel et al., 2014). These networked 
environments facilitate conversation and interaction between varied groups, enabling 
more inclusive and collaborative approaches to healthcare delivery. For example, 
Twitter has been identified as a powerful tool for disseminating information and 
initiating engagement in academic medicine (Choo et al., 2015). Similarly, these 
platforms encourage more meaningful dialogue between healthcare workers and 
patients, promoting co-creation of knowledge and supporting improvement in 
healthcare practice. 
In addition to enabling communication, social media also plays a strategic role in 
addressing health disparities and improving health outcomes in urban communities. 
Community engagement initiatives delivered through social media have shown unique 
potential to prepare future healthcare professionals for “Neighbourhood-Engaged 
Care,” a model designed to address health inequities in underserved urban areas 
(Alicea-Alvarez et al., 2016). These initiatives demonstrate how digital tools can 
shape education, strengthen cultural preparedness, and help healthcare 
workers understand diverse community needs. Social media is also increasingly 
recognised for its role in healthcare reputation management. Chaudhri et al. (2021) 
40 
 
argue that a complex interdependence exists between expressions of “care” on social 
media and public perceptions of healthcare organisations. In this context, strategic use 
of social media for reputation management becomes critical. Platforms serve as 
environments where public attitudes can be monitored, enabling healthcare leaders to 
align communication with stakeholder expectations and broader community values. 
As members of the Voice of the Customer (VOC) community began integrating 
LinkedIn as a dedicated professional network, the role of social media in promoting 
healthcare workforce development became more visible. Platforms like LinkedIn 
enable practitioners to network, collaborate, and engage in career-related discussions, 
supporting professional growth and exchange. While social media holds significant 
potential as an engagement tool, it also brings challenges. These include instances of 
unprofessional behaviour, inappropriate content-sharing, and subsequent risks to 
patient trust and safety. Chen et al. (2021) emphasise the need for clear ethical 
standards and coherent professional conduct guidelines to reduce these risks. 
The influence of social media on healthcare behaviour is further illustrated in research 
by Freire et al. (2023), who examine how it shapes patient decision-making and health-
seeking behaviour. Their work highlights how social media functions as a source of 
information, opinion shaping, and behavioural motivation—factors essential for 
patient-centred care. 
Social media platforms also act as dynamic reservoirs of public sentiment. For 
instance, Hu et al. (2019) analysed public perceptions of healthcare services on 
Chinese social media to show how online discourse reflects evolving expectations, 
needs, and frustrations. Monitoring this feedback allows healthcare providers to adjust 
services in more responsive and personalised ways. Berger (2019) argues that the 
broad range of engagement opportunities offered by social media requires deeper 
investigation to establish robust benchmarks for measuring public interaction. Such 
benchmarks would support healthcare professionals in evaluating the depth and 
quality of community involvement. 
The importance of social media in health communication becomes even more 
pronounced during global health crises. Platforms such as Facebook, Instagram, 
Reddit, and TikTok have proven essential to contingency planning by allowing health 
41 
 
authorities to distribute timely, accurate, and actionable information (Eghtesadi & 
Florea, 2020). Their instrumental use during emergencies illustrates the potential of 
social media to support public health protection and crisis management. 
However, rapid public uptake of social media also increases scrutiny and raises 
complex ethical and regulatory concerns. Torous et al. (2019) argue that as social 
media becomes a central environment for healthcare engagement and even research, 
evolving ethical frameworks are necessary to protect privacy, moderated 
participation, and data integrity. Effective use of social media in healthcare therefore 
requires thoughtful, balanced approaches that ensure responsible engagement. 
Taken collectively, these insights demonstrate that social media has become a 
transformative component in healthcare communication—shaping behaviour, 
strengthening community involvement, and influencing patient-centred care. Its 
dialogical and communal nature, alongside its expansive reach, reinforces its role as 
a cornerstone of modern healthcare engagement. 
2.8 CHALLENGES AND OPPORTUNITIES 
Several authors have examined the opportunities and challenges that social media 
brings to healthcare communication. Much of this discussion has focused on the 
interaction of dynamic online communities, control of information, and the ethical 
dimensions of the digital environment. Alicea-Alvarez et al. (2016) highlight how social 
media can enhance outreach in urban areas and reduce health disparities. They 
emphasise the importance of considering the unique needs, preferences, and cultural 
contexts of different urban groups. In contrast, Bejtkovský (2021) identifies the 
potential of platforms like LinkedIn to promote health-related content in professional 
environments but notes the challenge of maintaining professional boundaries. 
Belt et al. (2013) reveal that the internet and social media are widely used sources of 
health information within the Dutch population, raising concerns about ensuring that 
public preferences are matched with evidence-based, accurate health content. Berger 
(2019) shows that social media improves different types of engagement within 
healthcare systems, but highlights the difficulty in finding reliable and meaningful 
metrics to measure engagement outcomes. Bornkessel et al. (2014) explore 
42 
 
networked patient-centred care enabled by digital engagement and raise questions 
about maintaining quality and credibility in online patient involvement. 
Chaudhri et al. (2021) propose a privacy-preserving approach to healthcare reputation 
management on social media. They demonstrate how online expressions of “care” 
influence institutional image while noting the challenge of managing public perceptions 
in a constantly shifting digital context. Chen et al. (2021) highlight how social media 
surveillance helps identify unprofessional behaviours, but also raise concerns about 
inappropriate conduct and widespread misinformation. Durey et al. (2016) illustrate 
the opportunities for social media to support culturally sensitive engagement in 
Aboriginal Australian communities, though this is paired with the need to understand 
local cultural values. Similarly, Eghtesadi and Florea (2020) emphasise the importance 
of leading social networking sites in pandemic contingency planning, particularly the 
need for strong moderation and information governance. 
Koumpouros et al. (2015) discuss patient engagement and social media marketing as 
vital to healthcare improvement, but stress the need to balance engagement with 
ethical advertising practices. Lai and Chong (2023) explore how social media 
influences healthcare engagement among older populations, noting challenges 
related to time perspective, familiarity with digital tools, and perceived value. Miah et 
al. (2017) look at low-income communities and show how mobile social media can 
serve simultaneously as a support tool and a barrier, depending on how accessible 
and relevant the information is. Nabi et al. (2023) examine the role of social media in 
building social capital and trust in healthcare facilities in Bangladesh, again linking 
digital interaction with the potential to enhance trust in resource-limited settings. 
Moreover, Navarro et al. (2020) highlight challenges in global surgery engagement, 
including the tension between authentic storytelling and broader global narratives. 
During the COVID-19 pandemic, Pangborn et al. (2023) emphasise the power of 
frontline healthcare storytelling in shaping emotional responses and public 
understanding. Pizzuti et al. (2020) discuss healthcare workers’ attitudes toward social 
media as an educational tool, identifying a mix of practicality, scepticism, and varied 
perceptions. 
Powell et al. (2016) explore barriers to patient engagement across interdisciplinary 
teams, stressing the challenge of recognising barriers that differ between professional 
43 
 
groups. Reboe-Benjamin et al. (2023) show how Facebook-based engagement can 
support refugee healthcare navigation, but also highlight the complexity of doing so in 
culturally and socially sensitive contexts. Torous et al. (2019) draw attention to the 
ethical and regulatory concerns connected to social media’s expanding role in 
healthcare and research. Together, this discourse illustrates a rich and diverse 
ecosystem of inquiry. It reveals both the substantial opportunities and significant 
challenges introduced by social media for healthcare communication, engagement, 
and delivery. These insights can inform future digital healthcare tools, software design, 
policies, and interventions aimed at improving engagement and communication 
across social media platforms.  
Public engagement is characterised as the inclusion of people or communities in 
decision-making processes, opening governance and research to diverse and 
alternative forms of knowledge. The multidimensional social dynamics involved in 
public participation are critical to strengthening democratic processes and remain 
essential for improving the effectiveness and inclusivity of participatory 
approaches (Király & Miskolczi, 2019; Rask et al., 2018). Understanding the role of 
social dynamics in public participation is crucial for developing successful and 
equitable engagement processes. Public involvement encourages collective problem-
solving in response to social challenges, contributing to sustainable transformation 
and supporting community resilience (Klinga et al., 2018). In this way, it underscores 
the importance of fostering trust, affirming community values, and strengthening 
societal well-being (Franceschini & Marletto, 2019). 
Marginson (2016) discusses the interrelationship between social structures and 
participation, highlighting the global shift toward mass systems of higher education. 
As participation broadens, the resulting social stratification demands careful and 
systematic approaches that ensure inclusivity and responsiveness to different 
societal needs and expectations. This dialogic relationship between societal structures 
and participatory dynamics highlights the complexity of public engagement, where 
interactions between different societal layers shape the potential for improved 
inclusivity. Expanding this further, Callaghan and Horne (2023) explore localised and 
highly varied participatory practices, focusing on the relational nature of community-
embedded interactions. Their analysis helps illuminate the diverse ways engagement 
44 
 
unfolds, including the specific, and at times unique, modes of community life that 
condition participation. 
Franceschini and Marletto (2019) add insight into the role of trust within public 
participation. Trust forms the foundation of participatory processes because it 
influences reciprocity, cooperation, and mutual respect between stakeholders. 
Establishing trust is essential for developing participatory frameworks, linking 
community aspirations with practical outcomes, and reducing tension between ideal 
goals and real-world implementation. Martínez-Parra et al. (2018) argue that 
sociocultural factors significantly influence public participation in healthcare. Their 
work demonstrates how cultural norms, expectations, and broader social values shape 
public involvement. This fusion of cultural and participatory elements highlights the 
need for culturally congruent participation models that recognise community-specific 
values and align with expectations around healthcare delivery. 
Owusu-Addo et al. (2018) and Nguyen and Connelly (2014) also reflect on the 
influence of social norms on health outcomes and labour participation. For example, 
unequal distribution of unpaid care work and varied effects of cash transfers on health 
inequalities in sub-Saharan Africa show how social and economic conditions shape 
participation and health outcomes. These interdependencies call for holistic and 
multidimensional approaches that incorporate economic, social, and health-related 
components into participatory frameworks. The nuances of social interaction across 
these studies underscore that participation is deeply embedded within structural, 
cultural, and emotional dimensions. Elements such as mutual trust, hierarchy, cultural 
resonance, and economic context collectively influence the form and quality of 
engagement. This complexity requires an approach that captures diverse expectations 
and needs, especially as participatory landscapes continue evolving. 
Community formation involves generating connections within groups, promoting 
shared objectives, and cultivating mutual respect. This process creates opportunities 
for collaboration, strengthens group cohesion, and builds collective resilience. In 
healthcare, social support—whether emotional, informational, or practical—is central 
to effective engagement. Support systems such as peer groups, counselling, and 
community programmes help individuals and families navigate health challenges while 
connecting them more meaningfully to the healthcare system. These mechanisms 
45 
 
include support groups, peer-led initiatives, and the exchange of knowledge and 
resources. 
The convergence of community building and social support is critical to patient-centred 
and holistic healthcare. It prevents gaps in support, strengthens continuity of care, and 
fosters an environment where empathy, understanding, and culturally sensitive 
practices can flourish. Community building enhances collective resilience, encourages 
help-seeking behaviours, and facilitates information-sharing that leads to better health 
decisions. 
In diabetes-related support communities, for example, workshops, seminars, and 
shared learning sessions bring together patients, families, and healthcare 
professionals to exchange experiences and foster mutual understanding. These 
activities provide emotional support—such as peer counselling and friendship—and 
practical assistance with day-to-day management, including dietary planning and 
treatment routines. Such bonds reinforce group identity, strengthen community 
networks, and make support more accessible and meaningful. Ultimately, community 
development and social support contribute to improved self-management, mental 
health, and overall well-being. When combined with comprehensive support services, 
they equip healthcare communities to meet diverse needs and promote better long-
term health outcomes. 
2.9 CONCEPTUALIZATION OF SOCIALITY: SOCIAL MEDIA AS A TOOL AND AN ENVIRONMENT 
Social engagement in the context of social media is an emerging construct within 
healthcare. Social media provides a unique space where healthcare providers and 
users can communicate, share, and receive health-related information (Belt et al., 
2013). Yet this interaction extends beyond the basic exchange of information. Instead, 
it reflects the networked and participatory model of patient-centred care described 
by Bornkessel et al. (2014). This model shifts healthcare communication away from a 
traditional provider-centric structure toward one that prioritises patient involvement, 
collaboration, and ongoing engagement. 
Mediated social networking can effectively serve multiple healthcare purposes, 
including health promotion, education, and stakeholder interaction (Bejtkovský, 2021). 
46 
 
It offers a range of tools and platforms that healthcare providers can use to build 
relationships, increase engagement, and deliver accurate information to the public 
(Choo et al., 2015). For instance, applications like Twitter are increasingly recognised 
as effective channels for sharing information and facilitating knowledge exchange 
among healthcare professionals (Choo, Burke, & Walker, 2015). However, the 
expanding use of these platforms brings concerns related to professionalism, 
accuracy, and public trust, particularly as boundaries between professional and 
personal communication may become blurred (Chen et al., 2021). These concerns 
highlight the need for clear ethical guidelines for professional social media use within 
healthcare. 
Social media analytics offer further insight by enabling the identification and evaluation 
of “super-engagement” behaviours that contribute to enhanced healthcare delivery 
(Doherty, 2023). These tools can reveal user preferences, communication patterns, 
and behavioural tendencies, allowing providers to tailor communication strategies and 
improve the patient experience. The complex role of social media within healthcare 
aligns with a vision of a more informed, participatory, and collaborative system 
(Jeyaraman et al., 2023). When used ethically and responsibly, social media can help 
cultivate a culture of engagement that enhances care quality and supports community-
based approaches to health. 
2.10 PUBLIC ORGANIZATION AND THE BRANDING CONCEPT 
The concept of corporate branding in the public sector, including healthcare, can be 
understood as a strategic activity aimed at establishing a distinctive identity that is 
respected by the wider community and that fosters trust and loyalty. This goes beyond 
commercial marketing and instead represents a deliberate effort to create meaningful 
impact within local communities and broader society (Alicea-Alvarez et al., 2016). The 
rise of digital platforms has reshaped how public organisations approach brand-
building. Social media platforms, for example, connect healthcare providers with 
communities through continuous, two-way communication (Belt et al., 2013). In 
today’s digital environment, branding is not only about recognition—it is about 
maintaining a credible, trustworthy digital presence. 
47 
 
Networks such as LinkedIn have become important for promoting healthcare services, 
especially for chronic disease management and professional networking within the 
health sector (Bejtkovský, 2021). When used effectively, these platforms can enhance 
community involvement, which is essential for enabling public organisations to 
understand, respond to, and anticipate community needs (Callaghan & Horne, 2023). 
Branding in the public sector is therefore not limited to image-building. Rather, it 
involves developing trust, ensuring transparency, and presenting healthcare 
organisations as credible, community-focused bodies. Social media is central to 
this objective, offering healthcare organisations opportunities to communicate with the 
public, respond to concerns, share health information, and showcase their 
contributions to community well-being (Chaudhri et al., 2021). This type of online 
engagement influences public opinion, shapes organisational reputation, and 
encourages sustained community involvement (Hu et al., 2019). Moreover, the 
analysis of user engagement on platforms such as YouTube and other social media 
channels allows healthcare organisations to tailor their branding strategies to align 
with public expectations (Doherty, 2023). This strengthens service provision and builds 
a stronger sense of public trust. Ultimately, branding in healthcare represents a 
nuanced strategy that supports community engagement, corrects misinformation, and 
encourages a positive public perception of health and wellbeing. 
2.11 TAXONOMY OF PUBLIC HEALTH BRANDING 
Public Health Branding (PHBr) is an interdisciplinary methodology that integrates 
community engagement, digital interaction, and evaluation within traditional public 
health communication. They emphasise that healthcare providers must build strong 
relationships with urban communities and work with community-health workers to 
ensure access to preventive care in vulnerable populations. Moreover, Duku et al. 
(2022) and Durey et al. (2016) reinforce the need for healthcare services to be tailored 
to specific populations—particularly Aboriginal communities—highlighting that 
proactive, culturally sensitive engagement is essential. 
Researchers also stress the role of digital engagement. Bejtkovský (2021) identifies 
LinkedIn as a valuable platform for professional health communication, while Choo et 
al. (2015) and Bornkessel et al. (2014) emphasise the usefulness of Twitter in 
48 
 
promoting public health messaging and supporting patient-centred care. Meanwhile, 
Koumpouros, Toulias, and Koumpouros (2015) focus on the importance of patient 
engagement and social media marketing in building a trustworthy public health brand. 
Evaluation remains a core pillar of PHBr. Berger (2019) calls for the development of 
robust engagement metrics, while Doherty (2023) uses web analytics to examine how 
healthcare students interact with online materials. Franceschini and Marletto (2019) 
argue that public trust should be considered a key metric in public health branding. 
Taken together, this typology demonstrates the importance of combining traditional 
and digital communication strategies with strong evaluation frameworks to build public 
trust and maximise the effectiveness of public health branding campaigns. Social 
media now plays a central role in shaping the branding and reputation of public health 
organisations. Healthcare professionals and institutions use platforms such as 
Facebook and Twitter to distribute essential health information, interact with the public, 
and strengthen their public-facing brand (Koumpouros, Toulias, & Koumpouros, 
2015). According to Van der Belt et al. (2013), social media has become one of the 
most widely used sources of health information globally, highlighting its significance 
for modern healthcare communication. 
Social media enables more patient-driven content, encourages feedback, and 
supports timely communication between providers and communities (Bornkessel, 
Furberg, & Lefebvre, 2014). However, Chaudhri et al. (2021) caution that social media 
can also present risks to reputation if content is poorly managed or if inaccurate 
information spreads. Additionally, misinformation remains a significant challenge, and 
without proper monitoring it can undermine public trust (Gesser-Edelsburg, 2021).  
Moreover, Bejtkovský (2021) further examines how LinkedIn supports professional 
branding and networking, demonstrating how platform-specific strategies influence 
public perception. Active engagement on social media can offer valuable insights into 
public concerns and preferences (Chen et al., 2019), allowing health organisations to 
produce more targeted and relevant public health content. Social media is a powerful 
tool for shaping brand perception, but it requires strategic, careful management to 
safeguard credibility and trust. 
49 
 
2.12 PUBLIC TRUST AND SOCIAL BRAND ENGAGEMENT 
A theoretical approach to Social Brand Engagement (SBE) in public organisations 
involves adopting proactive, interactive communication strategies that strengthen the 
relationship between public health organisations and their stakeholders online. SBE 
combines dialogic communication, community participation, and sustained digital 
interaction to foster trust, loyalty, and a patient-centred ethos.  Moreover, Heldman, 
Schindelar, and Weaver (2013) highlight how health organisations can enhance 
community engagement by using social media more effectively. They argue that 
authentic interaction—not merely broadcasting information—has a disproportionately 
positive effect on public trust. Sharp (2011) provides a case study showing how brand-
driven engagement can support public health interventions and improve public 
perception. 
Dunn, Pearlman, Beatty, and Florin (2018) explore psychosocial motivations behind 
young people’s engagement with drug-prevention campaigns, offering insight into how 
Online Health Communities (OHCs) function. Suomi et al. (2021) highlight the 
importance of internal branding by showing how employee engagement shapes 
external public trust. 
Guillaume and Loufrani-Fedida (2023) demonstrate how inclusive stakeholder 
involvement strengthens branding effectiveness in public services, while Eugenio-Vela 
et al. (2020) offer a framework for place-branding that is relevant to public health 
contexts such as Abu Dhabi. Azer and Alexander (2022) illustrate how communication 
and engagement strategies influence vaccination behaviour, reinforcing the 
importance of trust-based messaging. Taken together, this literature provides a strong 
foundation for analysing SBE in Abu Dhabi’s public health sector, supporting the goal 
of amplifying the patient voice and reinforcing trust in public health services. 
In a time when public trust is often fragile, branding has moved beyond commercial 
identity to become a key mechanism for building credibility—especially in healthcare. 
Alicea-Alvarez et al. (2016) show how community-centred engagement can reduce 
health inequalities, using branding to communicate a provider’s commitment to 
community needs. The COVID-19 pandemic further highlighted the importance of 
branding in public health. Azer and Alexander (2022) demonstrate that credible 
50 
 
branding influences vaccination uptake by shaping public perceptions of safety and 
reliability. 
Bejtkovský (2021) examines how professional platforms like LinkedIn contribute to 
digital health branding, reinforcing credibility and strengthening public engagement. 
Studies by Belt et al. (2013) and Bornkessel et al. (2014) reveal that social media 
offers new pathways for health communication, making information more accessible 
and supporting patient-centred care. Moreover, Callaghan and Horne (2023) 
emphasise the role of trusted brand identities in encouraging local health participation. 
Chaudhri et al. (2021) explore how healthcare reputation is shaped online, noting that 
strong branding generates trust, while unprofessional conduct online can harm 
credibility (Chen et al., 2021). Gesser-Edelsburg (2021) calls for more socially oriented 
health communication during crises, arguing that trust in the message depends on the 
perceived integrity of the brand. Huo et al. (2019) and Koumpouros et al. (2015) 
highlight how patient empowerment and social marketing strengthen branding and 
community engagement. Nabi et al. (2023) provide empirical evidence that social 
media can build social capital and trust in healthcare organisations. So, branding has 
become a central component of health communication, closely tied to public trust, 
social capital, and institutional credibility.  
2.13 POLITICS OF SOCIAL MEDIA IN ABU DHABI 
The Abu Dhabi government has adopted a multifaceted approach to regulating social 
media platforms—one that is digitally responsive and rooted in the Emirate’s political, 
cultural, and social context. Social media governance in Abu Dhabi involves a 
balancing act: protecting state security, public order, and national image while 
simultaneously using digital communication to advance government objectives. 
Abu Dhabi and the wider UAE exert substantial influence over the digital sphere 
through legislation, regulatory frameworks, and enforcement strategies designed to 
oversee online content. This approach aligns with a national strategy that prioritises 
social stability and national security. In this context, social media is not only a channel 
for controlled messaging but also a potential site for ideological disruption, including 
content produced by groups considered a threat to the state. Several key laws shape 
this environment. The UAE Cybercrimes Law establishes strict penalties for offences 
51 
 
such as spreading misinformation, defamation, or promoting ideas seen as threatening 
national unity. This legislation acts as a legal mechanism for restricting content 
that may destabilise or criticise the state. Likewise, the UAE Penal Code (Federal 
Law No. 3 of 1987) criminalises defamation and insult, especially when directed at the 
state, its rulers, or religious symbols. These legal tools form part of a broader 
framework designed to ensure that digital communication aligns with state narratives 
and reinforces state authority. 
This balancing act is also evident in how the government frames the limits of 
acceptable speech on social media. Although the UAE Constitution guarantees 
freedom of expression, this right is not absolute—particularly in areas involving 
national security, religious values, or public morality. Authorities justify these 
restrictions on the grounds that unregulated digital speech may “sow discord,” promote 
hatred, or undermine national cohesion. As government oversight of digital platforms 
is often presented as necessary for the protection of society. From this perspective, 
stability and security are positioned as prerequisites for exercising any other 
rights, including expression. Citizens are therefore free to express themselves 
online—provided they remain within legal boundaries. Those who violate these laws 
face legal consequences. 
The Telecommunications Regulatory Authority (TRA) is the principal body responsible 
for monitoring social media activity in Abu Dhabi and the wider UAE. It enforces 
national internet regulations by ensuring social media platforms comply with local laws. 
This includes coordinating with platform providers to remove illegal content and block 
access to sites or accounts deemed harmful or inappropriate. The TRA also uses 
advanced technologies for digital surveillance and threat detection, analysing 
online communications to identify potential risks to national security or public order. 
Beyond enforcement, the TRA runs public awareness campaigns encouraging 
responsible social media use and explaining the consequences of online misconduct. 
These efforts form part of a state-led strategy to create a “safe” digital space that 
upholds national values, protects state security, and allows carefully managed public 
engagement. 
Within this framework, social media regulation in Abu Dhabi is a layered and evolving 
project that combines legislation, surveillance systems, and public communication 
52 
 
strategies. The government’s approach reflects the Emirate’s broader political 
priorities, where individual autonomy and freedom of expression are often secondary 
to national stability, security, and ideological cohesion. The TRA ultimately ensures 
that social media use remains within tightly defined ethical and legal 
boundaries. 
More broadly, Abu Dhabi—and the UAE as a whole—uses social media strategically 
for governance and statecraft. Platforms serve not only to disseminate official 
narratives but also to shape public attitudes and reinforce collective identity. This 
reflects an awareness of the central role social media plays in shaping discourse in a 
hyper-connected society with high digital engagement levels. One of the key ways Abu 
Dhabi asserts control over the digital environment is through curated messaging that 
promotes official narratives. Government accounts on platforms like Twitter, 
Instagram, and Facebook publish content emphasising national unity, economic 
development, and cultural heritage. Messages are often packaged with visual 
media—infographics, short videos, and photographs—to enhance engagement 
and shareability. The government also collaborates with influencers and public 
figures to expand the reach of its messages and tailor them to different audience 
demographics. 
This strategy combines direct communication with more subtle forms of persuasion. 
Official updates, announcements, and interactive posts project responsiveness and 
transparency, while narrative storytelling around national achievements fosters pride, 
cohesion, and loyalty. Social media analytics enable the government to track public 
sentiment and adjust communication strategies in real time. 
A major component of this strategy is controlling misinformation and politically 
sensitive content. Strict laws require social media companies to comply with local 
regulations, including removing or blocking content deemed false or threatening. 
Meanwhile, government agencies counter misinformation by issuing rapid 
clarifications and official updates on their own platforms. These measures both limit 
the spread of disinformation and reinforce the reliability of official sources. 
53 
 
2.14 USE OF SOCIAL MEDIA BY THE HEALTH INDUSTRY IN ABU DHABI 
The Abu Dhabi Department of Health (DoH) has intentionally adopted social media as 
a key channel for public communication, particularly for sharing health information and 
promoting public health campaigns. In a region with high internet penetration and 
significant daily digital engagement, the DoH recognises social media as a powerful 
and efficient means of reaching large segments of the population. Its social media 
activity serves multiple functions: disseminating health information, promoting 
preventive behaviours, and strengthening public engagement. 
The DoH’s social media strategy serves several core objectives. These include 
increasing awareness of public health issues, encouraging prevention and healthy 
living, and providing up-to-date public health guidance. Social media is used to update 
the population on policies, healthcare services, and emerging health concerns. This 
supports wider efforts to improve health literacy and empower individuals to make 
informed decisions. Another key objective is strengthening public trust through 
transparent, responsive communication channels that address questions and 
concerns directly. 
To ensure accuracy and reliability, the DoH uses strict verification processes before 
publishing content. Medical professionals review posts to ensure alignment with 
evidence-based guidance and current public health recommendations. Much of the 
content is developed in collaboration with international health organisations and major 
healthcare providers, reinforcing credibility. These measures are designed to reduce 
misinformation and promote trust. The DoH also collaborates with government 
agencies and private-sector partners to widen the reach of its messaging. During 
COVID-19, for example, the DoH worked closely with NCEMA and MoHAP to deliver 
consistent guidance on safety protocols, vaccination campaigns, and public health 
updates. These partnerships helped ensure coordinated messaging across sectors. 
The DoH uses multiple platforms based on audience reach and content type. Twitter 
is used for real-time updates, while Instagram and Facebook support visual and 
interactive educational content. YouTube hosts longer-form materials such as 
webinars and health explainers. To reach Abu Dhabi’s diverse population, the DoH 
employs a multilingual communication strategy, using Arabic, English, and other 
54 
 
widely spoken expatriate languages. The Ministry of Health and Prevention is 
particularly attentive to cultural sensitivities when preparing content on topics such as 
mental health or lifestyle diseases. Culturally appropriate messaging helps reduce 
stigma and encourages open discussion.  
The DoH also uses analytics tools to evaluate engagement, monitor sentiment, and 
identify emerging misinformation trends. This data-driven approach enables rapid 
adjustments to communication strategies, ensuring responsiveness to public needs. 
Crisis communication is a central element of the DoH’s social media strategy. Detailed 
protocols guide real-time communication during pandemics, outbreaks, and other 
emergencies. During COVID-19, the DoH used social media extensively to update the 
public on case numbers, vaccination campaigns, and safety measures, while also 
countering rumours and false information. This approach significantly reduced public 
anxiety and supported effective crisis management. The DoH’s purposeful use of 
social media—supported by strategic partnerships, cultural sensitivity, and real-time 
monitoring—has strengthened its role as a trusted public health communicator in Abu 
Dhabi. Through its digital strategy, the DoH promotes public awareness, encourages 
prevention, and fosters a more informed and health-literate population. 
2.15 THEORETICAL UNDERPINNINGS 
This section explores the Health Belief Model (HBM), a psychological framework that 
explains why individuals engage—or fail to engage—in health-related behaviours. 
Understanding the origins and development of the HBM is essential for contextualising 
the foundational assumptions that underpin the model. Exploring its historical 
development also helps clarify the basic principles that shape both its theoretical 
structure and practical applications across diverse health contexts. Understanding the 
core components of the HBM is particularly important, as these elements explain the 
complex interaction between health perceptions, behavioural intentions, and 
actual behaviour. This provides insight into how individuals interpret health 
information, perceive risks, and decide whether to act. 
The model’s relevance to health communication is especially significant. It provides a 
structure for understanding how people interpret public health messages and how 
such perceptions influence behavioural responses. The section also discusses the 
55 
 
relationship between the HBM and social media, demonstrating the model’s 
adaptability within today’s digital environment—where health information is frequently 
disseminated and interpreted online. Although the HBM has contributed extensively to 
health behaviour research, it is not without limitations. A critical analysis of these 
limitations clarifies the model’s strengths and constraints, offering insight into how it 
might evolve or be supplemented by other frameworks. The rationale for applying the 
HBM to this study is then presented, demonstrating its importance as a conceptual 
foundation. 
Following this discussion of the HBM, the next part introduces Social Influence 
Theory—another major framework that explains how people’s attitudes, beliefs, and 
behaviours are shaped by others. This section examines the origins and evolution of 
Social Influence Theory, its core mechanisms, and its categories. It also explores how 
social influence shapes public behaviour in digital contexts, especially through social 
media platforms. The application of Social Influence Theory to this study is discussed 
by linking its key constructs to the research aims and design.  
2.16 HEALTH BELIEF MODEL 
The Health Belief Model (HBM) was originally developed in the 1950s by social 
psychologists working at the US Public Health Service who sought to understand why 
individuals failed to engage in disease prevention and ignored screening tests even 
when they had no symptoms. Over time, the model has been expanded and adapted, 
becoming one of the most widely used frameworks in global health behavioural 
research (Alejandro, Bruce, & Leo, 2022). The HBM assumes that health behaviour is 
shaped by an individual’s beliefs or perceptions about disease and the actions 
available to reduce risk (Al-Metwali et al., 2021). It identifies six components that guide 
the design of health messages: perceived susceptibility, perceived severity, perceived 
benefits, perceived barriers, cues to action, and self-efficacy (Ellithorpe et al., 2022). 
For example, Al-Metwali et al. (2021) found that beliefs about susceptibility to and 
severity of COVID-19 were major predictors of vaccine uptake among both healthcare 
workers and the public. 
There are meaningful connections between the HBM and social media, particularly 
when analysing how people interpret health information online. Mao et al. (2023) used 
56 
 
the HBM to evaluate how social media engagement influenced responses to COVID-
19 health promotion messaging, demonstrating the model’s flexibility in explaining how 
individuals react to online information. Tian et al. (2023) combined HBM constructs 
with social media analytics to explore how hospital-originated Twitter posts shaped 
public responses during the pandemic. Their findings show how perceptions of 
susceptibility and severity directly affect digital engagement with health messages. 
Hita et al. (2023) investigated media-driven behaviours such as social distancing and 
panic buying, highlighting how external cues—especially from social media—can 
trigger rapid behavioural responses. In this context, social media acts as a cue to 
action, prompting individuals to adopt or reject specific behaviours based on perceived 
benefits and barriers. Similarly, Alhaimer (2022) used the HBM to examine the 
effectiveness of public health messaging on social media in Kuwait during COVID-19, 
showing how trustworthiness and message quality influence beliefs and behavioural 
intentions in digital environments. 
Despite its usefulness, the HBM has several limitations. Scholars such as Odunsi & 
Farris (2023) and Hita et al. (2023) argue that it portrays beliefs and behaviours as 
relatively static and does not adequately capture the way perceptions can shift over 
time. Others, including Nwosu & Ward (2022), contend that the model is not 
consistently predictive and does not fully account for the complexity of human 
behaviour, especially when influenced by social, cultural, or emotional factors. 
The HBM has also been critiqued for insufficiently incorporating emotions such as fear, 
anxiety, and stress—known drivers of health decisions (Ellithorpe et al., 2022). Early 
iterations of the model excluded self-efficacy, limiting its usefulness for supporting 
behavioural change (Tajiki et al., 2022; Ayaz-Alkaya et al., 2020). Further critiques 
emphasise that the HBM lacks cultural adaptability and does not fully address diverse 
health beliefs across different communities (Al-Metwali et al., 2021; Nooriani et al., 
2019). While the HBM has been successfully applied—particularly in contexts 
involving social media health communication (Davies & Mann, 2023; Alhaimer, 
2022)—its limitations highlight the need for complementary frameworks that 
incorporate emotional, cultural, and socio-structural factors. 
57 
 
2.16.1 Application of the health belief model to the study 
The Health Belief Model is highly relevant to this research because it supports the 
exploration of how social media communication influences health engagement, 
patient-centred care, public trust, and public health branding in Abu Dhabi. The HBM 
acts as an intervention-oriented framework, explaining how an individual’s perception 
of health threats—combined with mediating factors—shapes preventive action (Mao, 
Wang, & Zheng, 2023; Alhaimer, 2022). It therefore provides a lens through which to 
examine how beliefs shaped by social media messages influence engagement, trust, 
and perceptions of public health services. 
The core HBM constructs—perceived susceptibility, perceived severity, perceived 
benefits, perceived barriers, cues to action, and self-efficacy—are directly relevant to 
evaluating how the public responds to social media-based health communication (Tian 
et al., 2023; Ellithorpe et al., 2022). For example, Mao et al.’s (2023) cross-national 
study on COVID-19 health promotion shows how differing beliefs, shaped by digital 
messages and community narratives, influence engagement behaviours and 
compliance with public health guidance. Understanding patient-centred care requires 
recognising the influence of social media on health beliefs and public engagement. 
Social media serves as a major channel for distributing health information and shaping 
public perceptions of risk, trustworthiness, and credibility (Yoo et al., 2018). The HBM 
can therefore help explain how different forms of digital communication shape health 
beliefs and behaviours. For example, Hita et al.’s (2023) findings on media-triggered 
behavioural responses illustrate how online cues influence compliance with 
preventive measures during crises. 
The HBM is also valuable in understanding public trust. Trust is multidimensional and 
closely linked to perceptions of credibility, reliability, and transparency within health 
systems (Tetteh et al., 2022; Davies & Mann, 2023). By analysing how health beliefs 
develop in response to digital messaging, the model helps reveal how social media 
interactions either strengthen or weaken trust in public healthcare services. These 
mechanisms highlight the continued relevance of the HBM across diverse health 
communication environments.  
58 
 
The model’s broad use across different health contexts—ranging from water demand 
management (Al Metwali et al., 2021) to vaccination behaviour—demonstrates its 
versatility in analysing varied beliefs and practices. This flexibility makes the HBM 
highly suitable for exploring perceptions of public health communication in Abu Dhabi’s 
multicultural setting. By acknowledging the diversity of health beliefs within the 
Emirate, the model supports the development of more equitable, culturally informed, 
and patient-centred communication strategies (Youssef et al., 2022; Alejandro et al., 
2022). The HBM’s predictive insights—particularly when paired with factors such as 
message credibility and health literacy—reinforce its relevance in assessing how 
social media messaging influences behaviour (Odunsi & Farris, 2023). This supports 
the study’s examination of how trusted, well-framed social media communication can 
shape health beliefs, strengthen public trust, and reinforce public health branding in 
Abu Dhabi.  
2.17 SOCIAL INFLUENCE THEORY 
The concept of Social Influence Theory emerged from decades of psychological and 
sociological research exploring how individuals’ thoughts, feelings, and behaviours are 
shaped by others. One of the earliest and most influential contributors was Herbert 
Kelman (1958), who identified three key types of influence—compliance, identification, 
and internalisation. These categories describe the different psychological processes 
through which influence occurs and explain how society shapes personal beliefs and 
behaviour. Since its introduction, Social Influence Theory has been adapted and 
applied widely across different domains, becoming a foundational perspective within 
social psychology for analysing how behaviour and beliefs are shaped by social 
environments. 
Kelman’s typology highlights how individuals may comply with social expectations to 
gain approval, identify with others to maintain relationships, or internalise external 
norms when they become aligned with personal values. Over time, the theory has 
been expanded and integrated with other models to capture its multidimensional 
effects. For example, Liu et al. (2020) combined Social Influence Theory with Uses 
and Gratification Theory to examine sustained content contribution on microblogs, 
emphasising how online social influence shapes digital user behaviour.  
59 
 
Within consumer behaviour research, Naeem and Ozuem (2021) used Social 
Influence Theory to understand panic buying during crises, demonstrating how peer 
norms and social media interactions can escalate behavioural responses. Advanced 
the theory further by identifying resonance, activation, and cultivation as mechanisms 
through which leaders shape group norms and collective values. Social Influence 
Theory has also played a significant role in explaining digital behaviour. Lin et al. 
(2020) used the theory to analyse mobile payment adoption, combining cost–benefit 
analysis and perceived value with social influence to explain uptake. Naeem (2021) 
applied the theory to internet banking, illustrating how digital environments intensify 
the effects of social norms on decision-making. The theory’s adaptability—from early 
work on persuasion resistance to its integration with the elaboration likelihood model—
reflects its long-standing relevance across changing social contexts. 
The three major components of Social Influence Theory: internalisation, compliance, 
and identification. Internalisation, highlighted by White et al. (2009), represents a deep 
alignment where values or behaviours become part of one’s personal belief system. 
This form of influence is stable and enduring, reflecting genuine acceptance rather 
than mere behavioural conformity. Compliance, as explained by Lin, Wang, and 
Huang (2020), involves behavioural alignment in response to external pressures, 
incentives, or social expectations. It is driven by extrinsic motivations rather than 
internal conviction. When individuals align themselves with a group or person 
because the association is psychologically meaningful. This form of influence can 
reshape personal norms as individuals internalise the identity of the group. 
Together, these elements illustrate how Social Influence Theory captures the complex 
interplay between personal beliefs, external social pressures, and group identification. 
The theory offers insight into how individuals interpret, adopt, and reproduce norms 
within their social environment—an important consideration in understanding how 
behaviour is shaped within healthcare settings and digital communities. 
2.18 THE IMPORTANCE OF SOCIAL INFLUENCE THEORY TO THE CURRENT STUDY 
Social Influence Theory is highly relevant to this research because it helps explain 
how digital interactions, social norms, and mediated communication shape public 
engagement, public health branding, and trust in Abu Dhabi’s healthcare context. The 
60 
 
theory provides a framework for examining how individuals respond to online health 
communication and how community norms form within digital environments. Liu et al. 
(2020) demonstrate how users’ motivations for contributing content become 
intertwined with social reinforcement, showing how personal incentives interact with 
social approval in shaping behaviour. This is particularly important in understanding 
how interactive communication on social media fosters community involvement and 
supports patient-centred approaches in Abu Dhabi. The theory highlights the 
simultaneous influence of individual motivations and social cues, offering a 
nuanced way to study engagement patterns in health-related digital communication. 
White et al. (2009) provide insight into descriptive, injunctive, and in-group norms, 
revealing how these different forms of influence shape perceptions of trust and 
credibility in public health services. Understanding these norms is essential for framing 
public engagement as a driver of public health branding. Leadership, communication, 
and normative influence further illustrates how shared values and collective identity 
are cultivated—an important dimension of building trust in Abu Dhabi’s health services. 
Naeem (2021) and Naeem and Ozuem (2021) explore the antecedents of social 
influence in digital environments and show how individuals co-construct moral norms 
through online interactions. Their findings help explain how social brand engagement 
on social media can improve trust in public health services in Abu Dhabi. These 
insights demonstrate the recursive relationship between digital communication, 
community norms, and behavioural outcomes, which is critical to understanding 
how public trust develops in a digital health ecosystem. Collectively, Social Influence 
Theory provides a multidimensional framework for analysing how social media 
communication shapes public engagement, patient-centred care, and trust in Abu 
Dhabi’s healthcare system. It offers valuable insight into the social mechanisms that 
underpin digital interaction and helps explain how online communication can reinforce 
or reshape public health perceptions and behaviours. 
2.19 THEORETICAL FOUNDATION 
Social Influence Theory and the Health Belief Model (HBM) together provide a 
coherent and multidimensional foundation for achieving the aims of this study. The 
HBM highlights the importance of individual perceptions, attitudes, and beliefs in 
61 
 
shaping health-related behaviour, while Social Influence Theory explains how social 
pressures, norms, and interpersonal dynamics influence those beliefs and 
behaviours—particularly in digital environments. 
The HBM is especially useful for understanding how people make decisions about 
engaging in preventive health behaviours and how they interpret risks and benefits 
communicated through social media. In the context of Abu Dhabi, where diverse 
cultural groups interact with public health messages online, the HBM helps 
explain variations in perception, trust, and behavioural intention. It offers a 
structural way of analysing how message framing and perceived credibility affect 
public engagement with digital health communication. Social Influence Theory 
complements this by illuminating how collective norms, peer behaviour, cultural 
expectations, and online community dynamics shape individual responses. In social 
media environments—where interactions are public, rapid, and highly visible—
perceptions of what others think or do can strongly influence behaviour. This is 
particularly relevant in Abu Dhabi’s digitally active society, where social media 
users frequently negotiate between personal beliefs and broader community 
expectations. 
Together, these two frameworks allow this study to explore not only how people 
interpret health information on social media, but also how social interactions—likes, 
comments, shares, endorsements, and peer norms—shape public trust, patient-
centred engagement, and public health branding. The combined use of these theories 
supports a deeper analysis of how digital communication influences both individual 
cognition and collective behaviour within Abu Dhabi’s healthcare ecosystem. By 
integrating the HBM with Social Influence Theory, the study can examine: 
• how individuals perceive risk, credibility, and benefits when encountering health 
messages online 
• how social cues from peers and digital communities shape engagement behaviour 
• how public trust develops from the interaction between personal beliefs and social 
norms 
• how digital communication contributes to the branding and credibility of public 
health services in Abu Dhabi 
62 
 
This combined theoretical lens therefore supports a rich, layered interpretation of how 
social media affects health communication outcomes, and it provides a strong 
conceptual basis for evaluating public engagement, patient-centred care, and trust in 
the Abu Dhabi healthcare system.  
2.20 CRITICAL EVALUATION OF THEORETICAL FRAMEWORKS 
The HBM and SIT theoretical framework underpinning this study offers an integrated 
model for understanding the influence of social media communication on public 
engagement, trust, and behavioural responses in healthcare. The HBM and SIT each 
represent distinct yet overlapping perspectives: whereas the HBM is concerned 
with how different behaviours may be adopted or rejected depending on one’s 
personal perception of their benefits and risks (Al-Metwali et al., 2021; Ellithorpe et al., 
2022), SIT seeks to account for how social and normative imperatives are accepted 
or internalised in relation to general role expectations and social norms (Kelman, 
1958; Naeem & Ozuem, 2021). These two frameworks, however, have limitations 
when used separately in the digital health communication context within a multicultural 
society like Abu Dhabi. An evaluation of these theories, and their subsequent 
synthesis, demonstrates their weaknesses as independent paradigms and their 
strength when combined into a hybrid model for use within this study. 
The Health Belief Model has been a popular theory for explaining cognitive factors that 
predict preventive health behaviour. It predicts whether people will adopt health-
related behaviours by examining perceived susceptibility, perceived severity, 
perceived benefits, perceived barriers, cues to action, and self-efficacy (Alejandro et 
al., 2022; Mao et al., 2023). It is attractive due to its simplicity and its applicability to a 
wide range of health topics, from vaccination adherence (Al-Metwali et al., 2021) to 
media-triggered behaviour change (Hita et al., 2023). Yet the model’s strong focus 
on individual cognition can also be both an advantage and a limitation. It 
presumes rationality in how individuals perceive risk and benefit, while overlooking 
emotional, cultural, and social components that can significantly influence behaviour 
(Ellithorpe et al., 2022; Odunsi & Farris, 2023). In the context of social media 
communication—where much decision-making arises from emotional contagion, 
63 
 
social comparison, and peer validation rather than rational processing—this limitation 
becomes particularly evident. 
Another limitation of the HBM is its inability to fully accommodate the dynamic and 
iterative nature of human behaviour. As Odunsi and Farris (2023) argue, beliefs and 
attitudes are not static structures and develop continually through exposure to new 
information, interactions, or experiences—especially in online environments. Social 
media users constantly encounter competing health information and evolving 
narratives, which can change their perception of risk and willingness to comply with 
public health guidance. These continuous feedback mechanisms are not fully 
represented in the HBM. Moreover, as Al-Metwali et al. (2021) and Nooriani et al. 
(2019) note, the model does not adequately adapt to culture, assuming a universality 
of cognitive processing that may not align with collectivist societies where decision-
making is shaped by communal trust, family influence, and religious norms. These 
oversights highlight the importance of incorporating a social framework such as SIT to 
more accurately reflect how digital interactions and cultural contexts shape health-
related behaviours. 
Social Influence Theory, based on Kelman’s (1958) distinction between compliance, 
identification, and internalisation, adds the crucial social dimension missing from the 
HBM. This theory captures the processes through which people change their 
behaviour, attitudes, or beliefs due to real or perceived social pressure. As Liu et al. 
(2020) illustrate, social influence works on a continuum—from superficial compliance 
driven by extrinsic motives to deeper internalisation of values aligned with community 
norms. In digital media environments, such influence manifests through peer approval, 
online advocacy, conversations, and social currency (likes, shares, and comments). 
These processes are especially relevant to understanding how members of the Abu 
Dhabi public interact with health-related content on platforms such as Twitter and 
Instagram. 
Despite its strengths, SIT is not a complete explanation for why people adopt or reject 
specific health messages. It does not incorporate key behavioural health elements 
such as perceived vulnerability, personal efficacy, or self-assessment of health 
threats. Without accounting for individual cognition, SIT risks overgeneralising 
behaviour as mere conformity to social pressure. Additionally, because SIT has been 
64 
 
applied to diverse fields ranging from marketing to leadership communication, its 
constructs can become ambiguous when applied to health contexts (Naeem, 2021). 
Accordingly, while SIT explains social forces and pressures within digital spaces, it 
must be combined with frameworks such as the HBM that link social influence to 
individual-level health decision-making. 
The linkage between HBM and SIT in this study is symbiotic. Together, they provide 
a comprehensive overview of how social media communication affects health-related 
engagement. Whereas the HBM explains why individuals act when they perceive 
personally relevant risk, severity, and benefits, SIT shows how these actions are 
modelled, valued, or discouraged in social encounters, cultural settings, and shared 
practices (Kelman, 1958). For example, a person may believe risks are high (HBM) 
but still choose not to act—such as by refusing vaccination—if they do not perceive 
adequate peer approval or do not trust message sources (SIT). In Abu Dhabi’s health 
system, where community solidarity and institutional confidence are central to 
compliance, the integration of these models captures both individual and collective 
aspects of health-related behaviour. 
This reciprocal relationship is also critical for understanding digital engagement with 
public health communication. In the era of social media, communication is no longer 
linear but occurs within a multi-directional participatory ecosystem. In this space, the 
elements of the HBM—such as perceived susceptibility or cues to action—directly 
overlap with SIT mechanisms such as internalisation and compliance. When the 
Department of Health in Abu Dhabi posts about vaccination campaigns, public 
responses depend not only on personal risk perception (HBM) but also on visible 
indicators of social validation, such as comments or endorsements (SIT). The 
integrated framework therefore captures how psychological and sociocultural 
mechanisms shape trust, engagement, and behavioural intention in digital public 
health communication. 
Although both models offer strong theoretical grounding, several alternative theories 
were evaluated and excluded. The Theory of Planned Behaviour (TPB) expands 
behavioural intention through attitudes, subjective norms, and perceived behavioural 
control (Ajzen, 1991), but its focus on intention rather than actual behaviour was 
less suitable for this study’s emphasis on engagement processes. The Technology 
65 
 
Acceptance Model (TAM) (Davis, 1989) is useful for understanding technology 
adoption but lacks attention to emotional, cultural, and normative variables critical to 
health communication and digital trust. The Diffusion of Innovations Theory (Rogers, 
2003) focuses on macro-level adoption patterns rather than the micro-level interplay 
between personal beliefs and social influence within social media. These models 
therefore offered limited relevance to the specific dynamics of electronic health 
messages in Abu Dhabi. 
The choice to focus on healthcare rather than marketing or politics reflects the unique 
ethical and societal stakes associated with public health communication. In 
commercial or political settings, social media followers may be encouraged toward 
partisan alignment. Health communication, however, takes place within a moral and 
civic domain, where engagement can directly shape public safety, institutional trust, 
and real-world behavioural outcomes. As Naeem and Ozuem (2021) note, digital 
interactions in the health domain carry heightened requirements for transparency and 
credibility, as they can influence behaviours such as vaccination uptake and 
adherence to health advice. Unlike commercial promotion, public health 
communication requires not only influence but also education, empowerment, and 
ethical responsibility. 
Healthcare communication in Abu Dhabi presents additional context-specific 
complexities that make the use of HBM and SIT particularly appropriate. The 
population is characterised by high ethnic, linguistic, and cultural diversity. As Alhaimer 
(2022) and Tetteh et al. (2022) observe, confidence in public health messaging 
depends not only on precision but also on perceived authenticity, empathy, and social 
connectedness. In this sense, social media is not only a communication tool but an 
interactive space where identities, values, and narratives are shaped. The integration 
of HBM and SIT therefore enables this study to analyse how individual health 
perceptions interact with social structures in digital communication—highlighting both 
opportunities and tensions in promoting patient-centred, trustworthy public health 
dialogue. 
Despite the distinct conceptual boundaries of both HBM and SIT, their integration 
produces a rigorous and comprehensive framework capable of explaining the 
complex interaction of cognition, emotion, and social influence in digital health 
66 
 
communication. Whereas the HBM provides a psychological explanation of health 
behaviour at the individual level, SIT situates that behaviour within broader social 
norms, trust systems, and shared cultural expectations. By employing both 
frameworks, this study connects cognitive and social dimensions of health 
engagement and provides a richer understanding of how social media shapes public 
trust, participation, and behaviour within Abu Dhabi’s healthcare system. This 
integrated model therefore offers a powerful lens for understanding digital health 
communication in a highly connected and information-saturated environment. 
The development of social media in healthcare, as outlined in this literature review, 
serves two purposes. It highlights both the transformative nature of social media in 
reshaping communication and its potential to foster more nuanced and context-
sensitive healthcare dialogues. These platforms have played a significant role in 
modernising the dissemination of information and reducing the information asymmetry 
that often characterises healthcare systems. They have become essential to the 
healthcare sector, particularly in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi.  
The relevance of Social Influence Theory is critical for the present study, which 
analyses how social media and public engagement interact—often unevenly—within 
Abu Dhabi’s health sector. Social Influence Theory offers a framework for 
understanding the ripple effects of interactions, communication, and social norms 
within digital spaces. The theory contributes to a deeper appreciation of public health 
branding, patient-centred care, and trust in health service delivery. 
Liu et al. (2020) offer insight into ongoing content-contribution behaviours on social 
networks, revealing how user motivation and reinforcement from online social 
presence intertwine to influence content sharing. Recognising this interplay is 
essential for understanding both the current and potential role of interactive 
communication in fostering greater community involvement and enhancing patient-
centred care in Abu Dhabi. This perspective highlights how personal motivation 
and contextual influences shape participation in health-related social media 
communities, thereby enriching this study’s examination of digital communication in 
healthcare. 
67 
 
The dual role of social media as both a communication tool that integrates personal 
perceptions with social context and a strategic mechanism for shaping public health 
messages. This offers insight into the sociocultural landscape that underpins 
public engagement, revealing how cultural norms operate within online health 
communities and how they shape the “social unconscious” of digital health 
communication. Further depth is added through the role of descriptive, injunctive, and 
in-group norms in shaping social behaviour. These dimensions strengthen our 
understanding of public confidence in health services and help frame public 
engagement as a vehicle for embedding health branding within collective 
consciousness. This by exploring how leadership communication fosters trust through 
resonance, activation, and the cultivation of shared values—key processes for 
reinforcing the credibility of Abu Dhabi’s public health services. 
Work by Naeem (2021) and Naeem & Ozuem (2021) on social influence antecedents 
in cyberspace, and the construction of socially accepted norms, also supports the 
relevance of social brand engagement in cultivating public trust. The authors highlight 
a cyclical relationship between social impressions and individual behaviour, offering 
a comprehensive perspective on how digital interactions shape public 
confidence in health institutions. So Social Influence Theory provides a 
multidimensional framework for unpacking the complex pressures, norms, and 
influences operating within healthcare-focused social media. It contributes 
meaningfully to the literature on how digital engagement shapes patient-centred care, 
public health branding, and institutional trust—particularly in a culturally diverse setting 
such as Abu Dhabi. 
The rationale for using Social Influence Theory and the Health Belief Model (HBM) lies 
in their combined ability to offer meaningful insight into the aims of this study. The 
HBM contributes to the framework by clarifying how individuals think and behave in 
relation to healthcare—specifically, how they decide to engage in preventive health 
behaviours. When applied within social media communication, the HBM provides a 
mechanism through which public engagement may be influenced and patient-
centredness encouraged, by explaining how individuals interpret and respond to 
health-related information within Abu Dhabi’s community healthcare context. 
68 
 
However, Social Influence Theory asserts that social pressures and norms 
significantly shape individual thoughts, perceptions, and behaviours. The theory 
highlights the extent to which digital interactions influence public perceptions 
of the health sector, showing how actors within social media spaces establish health 
branding and build trust. By applying SIT, this study can examine the mechanisms 
through which social media communication affects public engagement with, and trust 
in, health services in Abu Dhabi. 
Together, these theoretical models provide an integrated framework for analysing how 
social media participation influences individual health beliefs and behaviours in 
relation to public health engagement, patient-centred care, and institutional trust. Such 
a framework identifies strategic opportunities for leveraging social media 
messaging to strengthen public health branding and enhance trust in Abu 
Dhabi’s evolving healthcare landscape. The approach is therefore valuable for 
examining the multidimensional relationship between social media communication 
and public perceptions and behaviours in this unique context.  
2.21 SUMMARY  
The development of social media in healthcare, as outlined in this literature review, 
serves two purposes. It highlights both the revolutionary nature of social media in 
transforming communication and its potential to foster more nuanced and context-
sensitive healthcare dialogues. These platforms have played a significant role in 
modernising the dissemination of information and addressing the information 
asymmetry that often characterises healthcare systems. Social media has become 
essential to the healthcare sector, particularly within the Emirate of Abu Dhabi, 
where digital engagement is exceptionally high and government institutions actively 
integrate online communication into public health strategies. The relevance of Social 
Influence Theory is critical for understanding how social media and public engagement 
intersect within Abu Dhabi’s healthcare context. The theory captures the ways in which 
communication, digital interaction, and social norms shape individual decision-making 
in online environments. It provides insight into public health branding, patient-centred 
care, and trust—three central themes of this study. 
69 
 
Liu et al. (2020) emphasise how social reinforcement and online presence influence 
content-sharing 
behaviour. 
Recognising 
this 
interplay 
is 
essential 
for 
understanding the potential of interactive communication to strengthen 
community involvement and support patient-centred care in Abu Dhabi. This 
view enriches this study’s examination of how personal and contextual factors shape 
participation in health-related online communities. The dual role of social media as 
both a platform through which personal perceptions are shaped and a mechanism that 
amplifies sociocultural expectations. This deepens the understanding of how 
cultural norms operate within digital health spaces, shaping behaviours and 
attitudes in ways that are especially relevant to Abu Dhabi’s multicultural 
population. 
The descriptive, injunctive, and in-group norms as drivers of social behaviour. These 
norms shape public confidence in health services and help explain how public 
engagement becomes a vehicle for embedding public health branding within collective 
consciousness. Leadership communication fosters trust through resonance, 
activation, and shared values—processes highly relevant to building institutional trust 
in Abu Dhabi’s public health sector. Moreover, Naeem (2021) and Naeem & Ozuem 
(2021) provides further support by demonstrating how digital norms are constructed 
and how social impressions shape individual behaviour. This cyclical relationship
