between social influence and behavioural response offers a comprehensive 
insight into how social media interactions strengthen public trust in health 
institutions. 
The rationale for using Social Influence Theory and the Health Belief Model (HBM) in 
this study lies in their ability to offer complementary insights into how individuals and 
communities respond to digital health communication. The HBM clarifies how 
individuals assess health risks, weigh benefits, and make decisions regarding 
preventive behaviours. When applied to social media communication, it provides a 
structure for understanding how public engagement may be influenced and how 
patient-centredness may be encouraged. 
Social Influence Theory adds a crucial dimension, showing how digital norms, peer 
behaviour, and social expectations shape individual and collective responses to health 
communication. It highlights how online interactions influence public 
70 
 
perceptions of health services, trust in institutions, and participation in public 
health initiatives. Together, the HBM and SIT provide a multidimensional framework 
for analysing how social media participation influences health beliefs, behaviours, 
patient-centred care, and institutional trust. This integrated approach helps identify 
strategies for leveraging digital communication to enhance public health 
branding and strengthen trust in Abu Dhabi’s evolving healthcare landscape. By 
combining cognitive and social mechanisms, this framework provides a 
comprehensive explanation of how digital health communication operates in a highly 
connected environment shaped by cultural diversity and rapid information exchange.  
 
 
 
 
 
3 CHAPTER 3: RESEARCH METHODOLOGY  
3.1 INTRODUCTION  
Research Methods explores the philosophical bases, as well as the theoretical and 
strategic dimensions of leading methodologies used in social research. This 
exploration is essential, as it significantly shapes the researcher’s view and 
understanding of the nature of reality (ontology), the nature of knowledge 
(epistemology), and the ways through which knowledge is generated and 
disseminated (method). These basic philosophical stances determine the direction of 
knowledge work, regulating all decisions taken, including those concerning the kinds 
of questions asked and the methods employed. 
The chapter has been organized to address, in a linear manner, the complex layers of 
research methodology, starting with an examination of the various philosophical 
worldviews that underpin research designs. These traditions are not simply academic 
niceties, but offer a way of orienting the design of the research to its philosophical 
roots, giving the project cohesiveness and integrity in relation to its methods and 
71 
 
findings. After explaining the philosophical foundations, the chapter moves on to 
provide an extended discussion on how methodological decisions, particularly the 
choice between quantitative and qualitative approaches, are inspired by these 
philosophies. This section considers the consequences of each approach and their 
appropriateness given the researcher’s ontological and epistemological orientations. 
The discussion further explores how these decisions affect not only data generation 
and interpretation, but also the ethical concerns associated with conducting rigorous 
and respectful research. 
Weaving a path through the theoretical dimensions and linking them to practical 
research applications, the goal of the chapter is to empower researchers to make 
informed decisions regarding method selection, supported by a coherently argued 
philosophical rationale. This integrated approach ensures that the research conducted 
is methodologically robust, ethically rigorous, and of significant value to broader 
knowledge, facilitating the transfer of theoretically grounded research into practical 
applications in the real world. 
3.2 RESEARCH PARADIGM  
Research philosophy forms the foundation of research, and explains the source and 
justification of the methods and analytical techniques employed. It is critically important 
in terms of shaping how researchers conceive and interpret reality (ontology), 
understand the nature of knowledge (epistemology), and determine how that 
knowledge can be attained and represented (methodology). Such philosophical 
standpoints establish the groundwork from which research is undertaken, and they are 
integral to how studies are developed and what they ultimately accomplish, particularly 
in social research. 
The distribution of philosophical views is significant, as it influences the researcher’s 
approach to difficult questions about humans and society. The absence of a 
philosophical perspective can hinder the ability to explore such questions with direction 
and depth. Diversity in these perspectives is valuable and also necessary, as a 
broader range of viewpoints can contribute to a more comprehensive understanding 
of the complex social world. The epistemological position of a study raises the question 
of what counts as valid knowledge, and how it can be acquired. Walliman (2022) states 
72 
 
that in research, epistemology refers to the relationship between the knower and the 
known. It determines the questions posed and the conclusions drawn. For example, a 
scholar adopting a positivist perspective may be inclined to examine observable 
phenomena and favour quantitative tools that yield measurable evidence. In contrast, 
an interpretivist epistemology would focus on subjective meanings and the 
complexities of human perception, privileging qualitative approaches such as 
interviews and observations as a means to gain a richer understanding of human 
experience, as claimed by Poth (2023). Research philosophy forms the foundation 
of research since it explains the source and justification of the methods and 
analytical techniques employed. It is critically important when it comes to shaping 
how researchers conceive and interpret reality (ontology), understand the nature of 
knowledge (epistemology), and determine how that knowledge can be attained and 
represented (methodology). These philosophical standpoints establish the basis on 
which research is undertaken and they are integral to how studies are developed, 
particularly in social research. 
3.2.1 
Implications of philosophical standpoints  
The implications of philosophical standpoints in research are quite significant. They 
shape the design and conduct of research, and also influence how the findings are 
interpreted and applied. This is especially relevant in social research, where the 
shifting realities of human behaviour and social systems demand critical reflection on 
foundational assumptions. As Sloan and Quan-Haase (2017) argue, maintaining a 
consistent philosophical stance lends clarity and depth to the research process, 
guiding the researcher through the complex tasks of data collection and analysis. Such 
thinking helps to produce findings that are both trustworthy and meaningful. The 
philosophical underpinning of research is a core part of the research itself. Indeed, the 
philosophy influences how researchers view the world, and the kinds of questions they 
ask. It has a bearing on the methods they apply to find answers, and ultimately, the 
knowledge that they generate. It is essential, therefore, that these positions are 
considered and defended early in the research process to ensure that the study 
remains coherent and grounded in a robust philosophical framework. 
Ontology, in the context of research, is the philosophical study of the nature of reality—
what exists, what entities can be said to exist, and how they are categorised and 
73 
 
understood. In social research, ontology addresses fundamental questions about what 
can be known, who we are, and the nature of our various modes of being in the world 
(Walliman, 2022). These ontological concerns shape the assumptions researchers 
hold about the ‘reality’ of social phenomena, influencing every other aspect of the 
research process. Two major ontological positions are realism and relativism. Realism 
holds that real entities exist independently of human minds, thoughts, or beliefs. This 
perspective, aligned with positivism, assumes that phenomena are observable and 
can be objectively described by the researcher without influence from beliefs or 
interaction (Neuman, 2014). Relativism, by contrast, asserts that reality is socially 
constructed, shaped by human perception and culture. From this perspective, reality 
cannot be separated from the ways in which it is interpreted (Robson and McCartan, 
2016). 
3.2.2 
Ontological orientations 
The disparity between these two ontological orientations runs through every 
level of research activity. For realists, the task of the researcher is to identify and 
describe reality as truthfully as possible, often leading to the use of large, 
representative sample sizes that are believed to provide a more comprehensive 
account of the reality being examined. Findings under a realist approach are typically 
presented as truths that can be generalised to wider contexts. Relativists, by contrast, 
view the researcher’s role as that of an interpreter or meaning-maker, leading to the 
use of smaller, purposively selected samples that allow for detailed, contextual 
exploration (Saldana, 2021). 
Moreover, realist research is generally explanatory in orientation, seeking to specify 
cause-and-effect relationships and to draw conclusions about phenomena based on 
observable patterns. Relativist research, on the other hand, focuses on how 
individuals or groups experience and construct their social worlds, making its purpose 
interpretive and descriptive in nature (Hair et al., 2019). Research methods also differ 
considerably between these two ontological positions. Realists typically emphasise 
quantitative approaches that prioritise objectivity and replicability. They use 
experiments and surveys designed to minimise the influence of the researcher’s 
subjective perceptions on the findings. Relativists are more likely to adopt qualitative 
methods such as interviews, focus groups, and ethnography, enabling them to gather 
74 
 
in-depth information about the diversity of human experiences and social conditions 
(Creswell and Creswell, 2018). 
Researcher engagement likewise varies. In realism, the researcher is positioned as 
separate from the research process and acts as an objective observer. In relativism, 
the researcher is closely involved and often engages directly with participants to 
understand their views and lived experiences (Bell, Bryman, and Harley, 2022). 
Subjectivism and relativism are also related. Relativists, like subjectivists, hold the 
view that knowledge is constructed by humans. However, relativists argue that such 
construction is shaped by time, place, and social position. They also acknowledge 
that phenomena may be experienced and interpreted in different ways by different 
people, depending on their cultural, historical, and personal contexts (Matias, 2021).  
Relativism permits a nuanced investigation into the nature of interactive public 
discourse. Such discussions are inherently subjective, filtered through perceptions 
formed at individual, societal, and cultural levels. In the “landscape of participation,” 
understanding how different segments of the community interpret and respond to 
public health messages on social media can reveal multiple definitions and 
experiences of meaningful engagement and communication (Sloan and Quan-Haase, 
2017). 
In contrast, a realist ontological stance might not adequately support these aims, as it 
assumes an objective reality independent of human interpretation; one that can be 
conveniently measured. Such a perspective can overlook the subjective and 
interpretive processes through which public health campaigns are received and 
understood by communities. Realism might also constrain the study to quantifiable 
indicators of engagement, and may then fail to capture the depth of personal and 
communal impact, or the quality of interactions that shape public perceptions of 
healthcare system effectiveness. 
On the other hand, the subjectivist epistemological position, which aligns with 
relativism, permits methodological choices that reflect the complexity of everyday 
human experiences and social media interactions. Such a perspective is essential for 
understanding the influence of increased community engagement on public health 
outcomes. Such outcomes, after all, can be shaped by how messages are received 
and acted upon across diverse community segments. Subjectivism also enables a 
75 
 
discussion of how different audiences within Abu Dhabi might interpret and respond 
to health communication, a crucial factor in developing strategies that are culturally 
and contextually appropriate (Hammond and Wellington, 2020). Conversely, 
objectivism is less suitable for this research, as it seeks to establish universal truths 
based on observable phenomena in a way that is value-free and generalisable. Within 
the context of this study, such an objectivist approach could neglect the individual and 
collective experiences that inform community engagement on social media—
experiences that are essential to understanding the depth and efficacy of these 
interactions within Abu Dhabi’s public health system. 
Relativist and subjectivist standpoints are therefore key where the aim is to gain insight 
into the social media landscape of public health communication in Abu Dhabi. Both 
perspectives support a more nuanced understanding of the diverse and complex 
experiences of individuals at the community level, contributing to public health 
strategies that are much more sensitive and effective since they are grounded in the 
lived realities of the community. Research epistemology refers to the study of the 
theory of knowledge in research, including the methods, validity, and scope of 
scientific understanding. Epistemology is concerned with what knowledge is, how it is 
acquired, and how it helps to define appropriate methodologies for conducting 
research. While methods, results, and data may be objective, interpretation and 
conclusions are inseparable from epistemological considerations (Walliman, 2022). 
It remains important for researchers to be mindful of why the philosophy which 
underpins a particular study is consistent with its research design, methods and 
analysis in order to avoid an epistemological mismatch. In this type of inquiry, research 
is guided by a paradigm that acts as an intellectual and philosophical GPS, dictating 
everything from the questions asked to how findings are interpreted (Creswell & 
Creswell, 2018; Walliman, 2022). This paper has a foundation in the interpretivist 
paradigm, guided by a social constructionist epistemology based on a subjectivist 
ontology. This framing offers a relevant point of view from which to explore how 
residents and communities in Abu Dhabi make sense of social media as a tool for 
public health communication. In this perspective, qualitative semi-structured 
interviews and thematic analysis are not only methodological instruments but also 
76 
 
epistemic acts which bring into being meaning by the co-construction of it with 
participants led by the researcher (Saldana, 2021). 
Interpretivism developed as a reaction to the constraints of the positivist framework 
that claims that objective truths about the world can be derived from empirical 
observations and measurements. Quantification, prediction and generalisation 
dominate positivist research, which attempts to account for social reality with 
numerical variation and statistical association (Neuman 2014). Interpretivism, on the 
other hand, dismisses the notion that there is one universal objective reality and 
contends that knowledge construction occurs through subjective interpretation and 
social interaction (Bell et al., 2022). From this position, social phenomena are thought 
not to be comprehensible independent of the interpretations that individuals make of 
them. The interpretivist researcher is therefore interested in understanding not 
measurement, interpreting not predicting, and the particular rather than the general 
(Robson & McCartan, 2016). 
The interpretivist tradition is grounded in a set of key beliefs which are particularly 
pertinent to this study. First, it takes as given that reality is both manifold and socially 
constructed – shaped by individual experience, language and culture. Secondly, it 
acknowledges the inseparability of the researcher from the research process and that 
their interpretations, positionality, and reflexivity are part of what underpins the 
knowledge generated (Kara et al., 2021). Third, overarching interpretivism is depth 
rather than range: the focus is on deep and thick understanding of experiences within 
their context. This is in line with the purpose of this study to investigate health 
professionals’ and the general public’s perceptions about social media’s role in 
public health communication, as reported in Abu Dhabi. 
Qualitative interviews, a characteristic of interpretivist research, enable participants to 
describe the meaning and significance of experiences in their own words. 
Interviewing is open-ended and dialogic, where a relational space of meaning-making 
dialogue takes place rather than being strictly sought (Creswell & Creswell, 2018). 
This approach allows the researcher to acquire an understanding of participants’ 
viewpoints across their sociocultural context. Additionally, the interpretive analytic 
process (thematic analysis) supports the researcher in uncovering patterns and 
themes that are derived from participants’ narratives inductively rather than through 
77 
 
a priori conceptual definitions imposed by researchers (Saldana, 2021). Both the data 
collection and analysis practices are in harmony with the interpretivist philosophy of 
co-constructed knowledge and reflexive involvement. 
3.2.3 
Epistemological underpinning  
Social constructionism forms the epistemological underpinning of this research, since 
interpretivism serves as the overall philosophical orientation. Social constructionism is 
the position that knowledge, meaning and reality are produced through social 
processes of interaction and shared meaning rather than representing objective facts 
‘out there’ to be discovered (Bryman, 2021). Derived from Berger, (2019) work, social 
constructionism posits that our perception of reality is shaped by language, culture, 
and societal systems. These mediations structure not only what people perceive as 
reality, but also how they perform within it. 
In this study, social constructionism provides a valuable framework with which to 
examine the ways in which shared understandings of health, illness and institutional 
trust are constructed through social media platform communication. Public health 
communication is not just a one-way relay of information, but is socially mediated, 
throwing up a relationship that incorporates interpretation, negotiation and 
recontextualisation between the senders and receivers of messages (Mertens 2023). 
By way of example, a tweet from the Abu Dhabi Department of Health does more than 
simply send a message: some individuals will understand it differently based on 
their cultural background, prior experience, level of trust in the institution in question, 
and social environment. Social constructionism illuminates how these sense-making 
processes interact with engagement and behavioural outputs. 
The constructionist stance towards knowledge implies that once the focus of the 
researcher’s practices has become constructionist and epistemological, they accept 
a view of research as practice – in other words, accepting that research practice itself 
is a site for creating knowing. Interviews are not passive exchanges, but active 
dialogues with participants through which meaning is co-constructed by the 
researcher. In line with interpretivism’s rejection of objectivity, this position is 
consistent with taking a reflexive attitude that accepts that the researcher is always 
thinking about how their own assumptions and questions are informing the data (Kara 
78 
 
et al., 2021). This reflexivity is especially important in cross-cultural research sites 
such as Abu Dhabi, where language, social hierarchies and institutional organization 
affect communication and interpretation. 
The interpretivist and social constructionist underpinnings of the present study are 
congruent with its qualitative format, inductive reasoning, and methodological 
approaches. The study seeks to understand how people and communities make sense 
of, interpret, and interact with social media as a medium for public health 
communication. This requires an enquiry that understands the subjective, situated 
nature of human experience rather than looking for general laws or cause-and-effect 
relations. 
3.2.4 Data collection and epistemology  
The use of semi-structured interviews as the main tool for data collection is closely 
associated with interpretivism. Semi-structured interviews strike a balance between 
structure and flexibility, allowing respondents to share experiences and perceptions 
while giving the interviewer scope for follow-up questions when new aspects present 
themselves. This follows from the constructionist assumption that meaning is 
produced in context and through interaction (Sloan & Quan-Haase, 2017). Eventually, 
stories from every participant add to the developing explanation of how social media 
serves as a communicative link—or conversely, obstacle—between healthcare 
professionals and the public. Data arrangement is assisted by thematic analysis as a 
framework that aims to locate and interpret patterns of meaning. Thematic analysis is 
not a method that just organises data but also interprets it through the researcher’s 
experiences, helping to uncover analytic themes representing both participants’ voices 
and theoretical implications on the part of the researcher (Braun & Clarke 2006). 
This dialectic between participant meaning and researcher understanding is central to 
the interpretivist legacy. In this analysis, themes arising from the interviews provide 
insight into how one's beliefs are shaped and maintained in digital health 
communication environments with regard to credibility, trust, and engagement. 
Ontology refers to assumptions about what exists and what can be known about it. 
The ontology adopted in this study is a subjectivist one where reality can only be 
known based on individual and collective processes of making sense, which 
79 
 
distinguishes it from objectivist ontologies held by those who believe there is an 
observable truth independent of what humans make it out to be (Cassell et al., 2017). 
From this perspective, there are multiple reality-constructions, each emerging 
from social and cultural experience. It is in stark opposition with the realist ontology of 
positivism which asserts that one and only one objective reality can be perceived and 
measured. In this study, the subjectivist ontology helps to reveal the multiple realities 
that participants—healthcare professionals, policymakers and Abu Dhabi community 
members—perceive of social media communication in different ways. For example, a 
healthcare worker may see social media as part of professional duty around health 
promotion, while an individual in the community might experience it as a place to 
validate fears or give vent to anger, doubt or counter-narratives. Acknowledging these 
multiple realities is important to understanding the complexity of social media’s role in 
public health communication. 
The interpretive nature of the thematic analysis is also based on subjectivism, where 
the researcher’s personal perceptual interface qualifies in its own right as a valid 
enterprise of analytical activity. Rather than aspiring to detached neutrality, the 
researcher should recognise that they cannot separate themselves from their 
positionality, background and assumptions in how data are perceived (Poth 2023). 
This meta-cognition fits logically within the reflexive orientation of interpretivism and 
guarantees integrity as an interpreter. 
The research utilises an inductive approach, reflecting the interpretivist and 
constructionist view. Inductive reasoning “is derived from detailed observations of 
phenomena and works towards broader generalizations and theories” (Robson & 
McCartan, 2016). It enables themes and patterns to flow naturally from the data rather 
than being driven by pre-determined hypotheses, which is particularly suitable to 
underdeveloped or new areas of interest, such as the impact of social media on 
healthcare communication in the UAE.  
The use of inductive reasoning aligns with the interpretivist tenet that theory should 
emerge from lived experiences, rather than precede them (Creswell & Creswell, 2018). 
In other words, this means that the themes and constructs developed in this study are 
based on what participants are actually saying rather than being overlaid on their 
responses from theory. This bottom-up reasoning not only results in contextually 
80 
 
grounded insights, but is also in line with the experiential heart of the study 
(description vs. prediction). The inductive nature of the process means it is more 
flexible and adaptable, thus allowing the researcher to narrow down the focus of 
inquiry as emerging themes suggest themselves from the data. 
The interpretivist and constructionist approaches of this research coincide 
methodologically in the thematic analysis. This analytical method includes tracking, 
analyzing and interpreting patterns of meaning in qualitative data. It is not a purely 
descriptive process, but an interpretive one—it goes beyond surface-level patterns to 
deduce underlying meanings, relationships and implications (Braun & Clarke, 2006; 
Saldana, 2021). Thematic analysis as used in this paper consists of six iterative 
stages: becoming familiar with the data; coding; generating themes; reviewing themes; 
defining and naming themes; and writing up. Each stage represents a dialogue 
between the researcher and the data, consistent with the interpretivist view that 
meaning is formed through interaction rather than discovery. The researcher’s 
theoretical sensitivity based on interpretivism and social constructionism informs the 
analysis, offering insights into how participants generate meanings about trust, 
credibility and engagement in social media. The interpretation is therefore an ongoing 
process and not necessarily a final or absolute truth (Kara et al., 2021). 
In interpretivist and constructionist paradigms, the researcher is not an impartial 
observer but works with participants to produce meaning. Reflexivity—critical self-
reflection on one’s role, assumptions, and impact—is therefore key (Mertens, 2023). 
Reflexivity is achieved throughout this study via journaling, memo-writing and iterative 
data engagement. The researcher’s position within their own cultural context of Abu 
Dhabi’s healthcare and social environment is also very important, as the meaning of 
trust, the nature of authority, and communication can vary across cultures. The 
inclusion and reporting of such reflexive insights contributes to the credibility and 
transparency of this study. Reflexivity means that the results are not made ‘wholesale’, 
but are contextually situated interpretations of social processes. This reflexive 
position adds to the credibility of the interpretive research, for it supports the 
interpretivist concern with authenticity and depth (Poth, 2023). 
The interpretivist paradigm based on social constructionist epistemology and 
subjectivist ontology offers the philosophical consistency needed for this qualitative 
81 
 
study. It is therefore consistent with the application of semi-structured interviews and 
thematic analysis and provides a rich examination of how people in Abu Dhabi make 
sense of social media in public health communication. The interpretivist orientation to 
exploring lived experiences and the constructionist focus on knowledge as socially 
produced ensures that the investigation represents, in a nuanced and context-specific 
manner, what people know, understand and believe based on their experience. The 
inductive logic underpinning this study encourages theory to be derived from empirical 
observation, allowing for insights that have resonance with the local context of digital 
health communication activities. 
In this philosophical alignment, the research produced will be methodologically and 
epistemologically sound. It situates the researcher not as a neutral observer but rather 
an active interpreter; one whose job it is to promote understanding rather than to 
present objective truths. In this regard, the study adds to the corpus of interpretive 
social research that values meaning, context and reflexivity over prediction and 
control. This paradigm does not just accept but recognises and appreciates human 
diversity — a crucial stance for understanding the ways social media interactions are 
re-configuring health communication, trust and engagement within Abu Dhabi’s 
culturally diverse public health environment.  
3.3 RESEARCH APPROACH  
The deductive approach aligns more closely with positivism, since it aims to prove or 
disprove hypotheses in an objective and generalisable way. Whilst this approach is 
useful for research focused on testing specific theories or identifying causal 
relationships under controlled conditions, it is not as effective in studies that seek to 
understand deep, context-dependent meanings. Such contexts are better suited to 
qualitative data and a flexible research design (Robson and McCartan, 2016). In terms 
of inductive research, sample size is a key consideration, and qualitative studies 
typically involve small samples. The aim of such research is to gain a rich, contextual 
understanding of the phenomenon under investigation. The process often involves 
extensive engagement with the research context and participants, allowing the 
researcher to grasp the complexities of human behaviour and social settings. The 
82 
 
outcomes of inductive research are typically new theories or conceptual frameworks 
that provide fresh perspectives on the studied phenomena (Poth, 2023). 
For contrast, deductive inquiry involves larger sample sizes that permit quantitative 
analysis and generalisable results across contexts. The researcher’s role is often quite 
detached, and focused on collecting and analysing data to test pre-established 
hypotheses without incorporating subjective reflections (Walliman, 2021). The 
inductive method, in contrast, aligns with social constructionism and is better suited to 
studies aiming to explore new areas or address complex social issues. Such an 
approach values the construction of reality as experienced by individuals and 
supports the development of theories grounded in participants’ data. The inductive 
approach is thus particularly appropriate for exploratory research that contributes new 
insights to academic understanding. 
The use of inductive research in this study is based on its aligned nature with 
interpretivist and social constructionist paradigms, which together shape the overall 
philosophical standpoint of the research. Inductive reasoning involves moving from 
specific observations to develop broader generalisations or theoretical views, thereby 
allowing investigators to construct meaning out of lived reality rather than testing or 
confirming preconceived hypotheses (Creswell & Creswell, 2018). This method is 
particularly suitable for qualitative research aiming at the investigation of complex 
social phenomena since it allows the researcher to be flexible, exploratory, and 
sensitive to context. Since the current paper focuses on exploring how HCPs and 
laypersons in Abu Dhabi perceive and experience social media as a tool for health 
communication, an inductive approach is particularly well suited for exposing the 
subtle, multilayered meanings contained within participants’ narratives. 
The inductive process starts from empirical data gathering – usually through 
interviews, focus groups or observations; it then goes on to establish patterns, and 
categories of the phenomenon emerging from the data itself. Rather than beginning 
with a preconceived theory or body of hypotheses, inductive inquiry means building 
theory from the ground up, based on descriptions participants give of their worlds 
(Saldana, 2021). This bottom-up process is consistent with the interpretivist goal of 
comprehending the subjective meanings people assign to their actions and 
experiences. This study is based on participants’ understandings of trust, credibility 
83 
 
and engagement with public health information shared on social media, shaped by 
their cultural, institutional and social situatedness. The methodological orientation of 
inductiveness allows for multiple ways of thinking and representing how these 
factors are linked conceptually in a process that is both culturally grounded and 
theoretically insightful. 
An inductive orientation is especially appropriate for under-researched or context-
bound research, such as digital public health communication within Abu Dhabi. The 
literature on social media and health communication is dominated by studies 
conducted in the West, reflecting sociocultural, institutional and communicative 
contexts that are not necessarily applicable to the Gulf. The study uses an inductive 
approach in order not to project Western theories onto the data, and to let local 
experiences give birth to knowledge. Such attentiveness to the role of context, it is 
suggested, not only helps create evidence that is more authentic, but also contributes 
to knowledge production that can accommodate some of the social and cultural 
nuances of Abu Dhabi's healthcare system. 
In addition, an inductive approach is appropriate as social constructionism views 
knowledge and meaning as constructed through interaction and engagement 
(Bryman, 2021). In this study, the practice of conducting semi-structured interviews 
opens up a discursive space through which participants actively co-produce meanings 
related to their interaction with social media and public health communications. The 
interactionist approach by the researcher allows meanings to be dynamic, as it is 
through engaging in dialogue that findings emerge. It ensures that these co-
constructed meanings are not restricted by pre-specified factors or theoretical 
thresholds. On the contrary, they are "found" and "made sense of" through an ongoing 
dialogue with the data themselves that approximates sociality as to how new ideas 
emerge in collective understanding. 
The inductive attitude is also helpful when it comes to studying phenomena about 
which there is no prior theory, or for which existing theories do not cover recent 
developments well. The junction of social media, public confidence and health 
communication is a rapidly developing area that is dominated by ongoing technological 
and social change. As one researcher noted, "In the absence of positive cases, 
however, I used inductive reasoning to remain open to new patterns or ideas that do 
84 
 
not necessarily transpire within existing theory" (p. 9–10). For instance, the rise of new 
types of digital engagement (eg micro-influencers, interactive health campaigns and 
algorithm-driven content exposure) demands an agile analytic approach that can 
acclimatise to new observations rather than one with an intention to validate legacy 
constructs. From this inductive process,  the researcher can uncover emerging 
patterns and conceptual relations which may more accurately portray contemporary 
digital public health communication. 
Another important reason for preferring an inductive approach is that it can handle 
complexity. Social media communication is a multidimensional concept, expanding 
across social, emotional, and technical aspects. Without losing sight of their complex 
interactions, we cannot simply reduce it to a set of easily quantifiable variables. 
Inductive investigation accommodates this complexity by going in depth, not in 
breadth. The small purposive sample facilitates the in-depth investigation of 
participants’ experiences and means that analysis reflects both diversity and depth of 
opinion. The method also allows the researcher to 'pull out' unanticipated themes—
themes generated by the data, rather than imposed on them by a priori codes. These 
nascent insights are important as building blocks for new theories, especially in areas 
involving perception, emotion and interaction where humanity is frequently at the 
heart of the matter (Robson & McCartan, 2016). 
Further, the inductive process serves to remind us of the need for reflexivity within 
qualitative research. As the researcher’s interpretations are actively involved in 
constructing findings, this is promoted within the inductive approach, encouraging 
questioning and reflection around researcher assumptions and positionality that 
impact meaning-making (Kara et al., 2021). Such reflexivity serves to increase the 
transparency and trustworthiness of the research within the interpretivist focus on co-
constructed knowledge. Inductive researchers do not search for objectivity as in a 
positivist model; because what they are looking for is authenticity, which means 
showing honestly and integrity in the way in which meanings and patterns actually 
arise from data, through dialogue between researcher and participants. 
The results of inductive research are usually developed into a conceptual framework 
or theoretical model to explain the phenomenon. These models are empirically 
based, and they provide a solid platform for research and future practice. Following 
85 
 
the inductive process, four major themes regarding trust, engagement, credibility of 
communication and dynamics of interaction within the Abu Dhabi public health care 
system emerged. The themes themselves were not based on existing models but 
were generated from the reality of participants' lives. The model emerging from this 
synthesis, developed in Chapter 5, integrates these insights with existing theories of 
behaviour change such as the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory. This 
integrative review provides evidence of how inductive reasoning is used to develop 
theory that patients experience as true. 
Furthermore, the inductive approach is consistent with the research objective of 
exploring dynamics in public healthcare–community interaction via social media. The 
study is exploratory rather than confirmatory – it aims to reveal how processes of 
communication work in practice, not to verify a set of existing claims. This exploratory 
property calls for a methodology that focuses on open investigation, iterative analysis 
and the ability to react to emerging insights. Unlike the strictures of deductive logic, 
inductive reasoning is flexible and enables the researcher to change questions, 
analytical categories and interpretations as new data and understandings become 
available. This flexibility is necessary for representing the dynamic and reciprocal 
nature of social media communication, in which interpretations and actions are 
negotiated on an ongoing basis. 
Practically, the inductive approach also increases the credibility of findings for policy 
and practice. Through legitimating theory in lived experience, the research generates 
knowledge that is inherently action-oriented for use in public health communication. 
Patterns and themes discovered through inductive analysis of these strategies can be 
used by health communicators and policymakers to improve more effective, culturally 
sensitive, interactive social media campaigns. This induction-driven bottom-up 
approach serves to make such suggestions concrete and not mere abstract musings 
in terms of the lives and views of the subjects and their position. 
Third, the inductive procedure contributes to internal consistency between the 
philosophy, methodology and analysis of this study. This interpretivist orientation is 
reflected in the research through its emphasis on understanding over measurement, 
context over control, and depth over generalisation. And its linkage with social 
constructionism supports the idea of meaning as co-created, and knowledge as 
86 
 
dialogical. These philosophical and methodological symmetries, combined, justified 
the use of an inductive approach as the author’s most adequate option for 
approaching the research objectives. The reason why an inductive approach is 
considered here is its potential to explore (to be visited), rather than to test; to interpret 
(evaluation with no regression following mathematical models); as well as to build-up, 
not impose. It enables the researcher to uncover rich, context-embedded insights on 
the influence of social media in communication and engagement within the Abu 
Dhabi healthcare context. To allow theory to come up from participants’ experiences, 
the inductive process also ensures that the data remain adequate to reality within its 
social scope. This is a level of understanding that is firmly rooted in empirical data and 
philosophical consistency and presents us with an excellent groundwork for the 
development of new theoretical insights and practical recommendations for digital 
health communication.

The induction of research in this study finds its basis in its aligned nature with

In addition, an inductive approach is appropriate as social constructionism views

Another important reason for preferring an inductive approach is that it can handle
