90 
 
3.5 PILOT STUDY  
Researchers are required to decide if they will undertake a pilot of their study as part 
of their methodological design prior to commencing qualitative field-based work. There 
is a precedent for this—a pilot study, which may consist of preliminary interviews, 
focus groups, or instrument testing, used as "a small test run for refining instruments 
for collecting data and checking if interview questions are clear and relevant without 
the final participants" (Creswell and Creswell, 2018). It offers an opportunity to 
evaluate the suitability of research instruments and identify possible procedural or 
ethical issues before starting data collection on a larger scale. Pilot interviews in an 
interpretivist study might also provide insights into the potential for the approach to 
support the generation of deep, contextually embedded narratives. Yet, the choice to 
proceed with a pilot should depend on the intended purposes, scale, and 
methodological preparedness of the study. 
There was no formal pilot interview phase in the current study. Rather, the instrument 
of inquiry—the semi-structured interview guide—was subjected to a rigorous review 
process by experts in order to establish its academic soundness and contextual 
appropriateness. This was a conscious decision, made because the researcher had 
extensive experience in qualitative interviews and thematic analysis; it also reflected 
feedback from academic supervisors and expert professionals in health 
communication and social media research. The priority was therefore to validate the 
interview guide through expert review, as opposed to testing it through a pilot study 
with participants. This is consistent with advice from qualitative methodologists who 
suggest that pilot studies are not always required when instruments have been 
informed and constructed using previous research evidence as well as consultation 
with experts (Robson and McCartan, 2016; Hennink, Hutter, and Bailey, 2020). 
A pilot study is typically used to determine whether data collection methods are 
feasible, valid, and sensitive to participants’ contexts. Piloting functions as a 
mechanism to check whether interview questions elicit the intended information, 
whether participants understand the linguistic terminology being used, and whether 
the length and structure of interviews is feasible (Walliman, 2022). In interpretivist and 
social constructionist studies, pilot work may also allow the relational effectiveness of 
91 
 
the interviewer and participant to be assessed, enabling the researcher to modify 
interpersonal or reflexive skills. 
However, the context of this research—on professional and public perceptions of 
social media communication in an Abu Dhabi public health system—required high 
levels of methodological clarification from the outset. The researcher was also a 
professional who was already familiar with the subject and had previously conducted 
interviews in comparable projects, offering an experiential foundation similar to a pilot 
study. This background enabled confident progression to the full data collection phase 
without the need for a distinct pilot, thus avoiding duplication while preserving 
methodological strength. 
The interview guide was developed through a multi-stage refinement process similar 
to the evaluative goals of a pilot study. The first draft was informed by established 
qualitative interview formats and aligned with the theoretical frameworks underpinning 
this research, including the Health Belief Model (HBM) and Social Influence Theory 
(SIT). These frameworks served as structured yet flexible tools to develop questions 
about beliefs concerning disease susceptibility, trust in health authorities and media, 
perceived interest and knowledge for participation, and anticipated interaction with 
others while engaging with digital health communication. The guide was then reviewed 
by two academic advisors and three experts: one public health communication 
specialist, one digital media strategy specialist, and one healthcare policy expert. 
Together, this feedback shaped the structure, language, and sequencing of questions 
to ensure their relevance, clarity, and ethical appropriateness. 
There are several explanations for the decision not to conduct a formal pilot interview 
phase. The first is that the researcher had significant experience conducting semi-
structured interviews and using thematic analysis in interpretivist paradigms. As an 
academic and practitioner trained in qualitative methodologies, the researcher had 
previously carried out in-depth interviews with similar participant groups (healthcare 
providers, educators, and community members). With this professional experience, 
the researcher had the confidence to conduct interviews, establish rapport, and 
manage sensitive issues without pre-testing. 
92 
 
Creswell and Poth (2018) assert that whether a pilot is required depends partly on the 
researcher’s experience in the data collection method and topic area. If the researcher 
is already highly experienced, a formal pilot may offer few new insights and simply 
prolong the research timeline. The combination of practical and theoretical knowledge, 
along with grounding in interpretivism and social constructionism, ensured the 
credibility and contextual validity of the data-gathering approach. A pilot study was 
therefore replaced by the systematic validation of the interview guide through expert 
review. The guide was reviewed by three independent reviewers who assessed it for 
conceptual clarity, question relevance, linguistic appropriateness, and ethical 
sensitivity. Their comments helped improve the interview schedule in both content and 
structure. 
For example, reviewers advised the researcher to make some of the technical 
language from the field of social media analytics more relatable for community 
participants while maintaining academic precision for professionals. They also 
proposed revising the order of some questions to create a more logical flow, moving 
from experiential and practice-based questions to those exploring deeper conceptual 
and theoretical issues. This iterative process ensured that the final guide was both 
clear and comprehensive, as is typically achieved through pilot testing (Hennink et al., 
2020). 
It also confirmed that the questions were consistent with the main research objectives 
and theoretical concepts. Targeted prompts addressing perceived susceptibility, 
communication trust, and social norms mapped directly onto the constructs of HBM 
and SIT. By examining these alignments through academic and professional scrutiny, 
the study achieved conceptual coherence comparable to what is often generated 
through pilot testing, though without involving live participants. It would not have been 
ethical to conduct a pilot study by recruiting respondents with characteristics similar to 
those needed for the main study. With a relatively small and specific sample—
especially healthcare professionals—it was important to retain potential participants 
for the main phase. Conducting a pilot would have reduced the available participant 
pool and risked diminishing the richness and representativeness of the final dataset. 
In addition, as Denscombe (2021) notes, pilot interviews in small-scale qualitative 
studies can be problematic if they overburden participants or require repeated 
93 
 
interactions with the same individuals. This concern is heightened in professional 
contexts where research informants are senior, time-limited, or otherwise difficult to 
access. Avoiding such repetition was therefore both an ethical and methodological 
consideration. There were also practical considerations. Given the researcher’s 
professional experience with interviews and the clarity achieved through expert review, 
the added value of a formal pilot would have been limited in comparison to the 
additional time and resource demands. However, incremental adjustments were 
made inductively during the initial interviews of the main study—a standard and well-
established strategy in qualitative research (Robson and McCartan, 2016). 
Throughout these early interviews, the researcher focused on flow, comprehensibility, 
and participant comfort, and noted minor adjustments to wording or sequence when 
necessary. 
A second reason for not conducting a pilot interview is that participants were familiar 
with the linguistic and contextual foundations of the study. All interviewees 
(professionals and community members) spoke English, which is widely used in higher 
education and professional contexts in Abu Dhabi. This minimised the risk of 
misinterpretation, which is often a justification for piloting in multilingual studies. 
Additionally, the researcher was culturally sensitive to the health and social context of 
Abu Dhabi, reducing the likelihood of challenges relating to local idioms, references, 
or communication norms. Moreover, Walliman (2021) and Hennink et al. (2020) 
highlight that piloting is particularly important when the researcher lacks familiarity with 
the context, population, or topic. In this study, the researcher’s in-depth knowledge of 
both the cultural and organisational context minimised the risk of misunderstanding or 
misalignment between questions and participant experiences. 
3.6 DATA COLLECTION  
Flexibility is a component of methodological design in interpretivist qualitative 
research. Interviews are viewed as interactive encounters in which questions evolve 
in response to participants’ narratives rather than remaining fixed or standardised. 
Therefore, a formal pilot is not always required, as the qualitative interview process 
itself functions as a platform for refinement. Preliminary interviews in this study served 
as opportunities for the researcher to reflect on question clarity, flow, and engagement, 
94 
 
ensuring that consistent themes were covered with all participants. The interpretivist 
perspective prioritises meaning-making and responsiveness over procedural rigidity. 
The researcher’s real-time assessment of what required attention created a form of 
methodological openness that allowed emergent insights to shape subsequent 
interviews. As such, a separate pilot phase was not necessary, as the process of 
iterative improvement was embedded within the research design itself.  
Framed by social constructionism, relativism, subjectivism, and inductive research, 
qualitative inquiry draws on a range of approaches including structuralism, humanism, 
and content analysis. Methods such as in-depth interviews, participant observation, 
focus groups, and document analysis enable researchers to explore the contextual 
and social meanings of human behaviour and societal trends—an essential 
component given the research aims and objectives. 
• 
In-depth interviews (IDIs) are a useful tool in qualitative research, 
allowing researchers to engage in conversation with participants or 
informants to understand their perspectives on a specific topic. The 
method is compatible with social constructionism and subjectivism since 
it focuses on the individual’s process of meaning-making. IDIs are 
particularly well suited to examining how social media facilitates dialogue 
and community engagement in public health. They are useful for 
exploring the beliefs, attitudes, and perceptions that shape such 
interactions (Breakwell, Wright, and Barnett, 2020). 
• 
Participant observation involves researchers immersing themselves in 
social settings to observe and interact with participants as they naturally 
engage in their environment. This method is supported by social 
constructionism, which holds that reality is produced through social 
interaction and shared meaning (Robson and McCartan, 2016). 
Participant observation is essential for studying community interaction 
through social media, since it allows for the direct observation of lived 
behaviour and participation in public health campaigns (Denscombe, 
2021). 
• 
Focus groups are another key qualitative method. These involve group 
discussions that elicit a range of views on products, services, concepts, 
95 
 
or ideas. Group interaction generates data and reveals varied 
perspectives across a defined social spectrum. This method is grounded 
in a relativist belief that multiple realities exist, each shaped by the group 
dynamic and context of interaction.  
• 
Content or document analysis refers to the systematic summarising and 
interpretation of visual, textual, or aural materials. These may include 
public health communications, social media posts, user comments, or 
other relevant artefacts. Emphasis is placed on identifying patterns and 
interpreting meaning within the content. This method aligns with 
inductive reasoning, as it allows themes and patterns to emerge naturally 
from the data without relying on pre-defined categories or theoretical 
frameworks. This makes the approach highly appropriate for evaluating 
how social media functions as a tool for public health communication and 
understanding its impact on community perceptions.  
 
Each of the above approaches contributes to the current objective of evaluating how 
social media is used for communication within the public health system of Abu Dhabi. 
The various approaches provide tools for researchers to account for the multivariate 
and dynamic nature of social interactions, and this is essential for understanding and 
improving community engagement, assessing the effectiveness of public health 
communication strategies, and ultimately enhancing public health outcomes. By 
employing these qualitative methods, the study is well positioned to explore, examine, 
and appraise the use of social media in public health contexts. 
Semi-structured interviews are widely used qualitative methods which involve the 
researcher preparing a set of guiding questions in advance, along with introductory 
and closing statements. The researcher, however, remains open to new themes and 
questions that may emerge during interviews. Such flexibility allows for an organic 
conversation, and can result in more detailed, comprehensive data. The method is 
particularly effective in eliciting personal experiences, reflections, and perspectives 
from participants, and this makes it ideal for exploratory research that develops new 
insights.  
96 
 
The combination of semi-structured interviews with interpretivism and qualitative 
research stems from their shared aim of understanding the complexity of human 
experience from the perspective of participants. Interpretivism holds that reality is 
subjective and can only be known through the meanings that individuals attach to their 
experiences (Robson and McCartan, 2016). In this context, semi-structured interviews 
offer participants the opportunity to articulate their perceptions and experiences in their 
own words. This is an essential feature for interpretive analysis. The detailed, context-
specific use of language in such interviews is critical for uncovering deeper insights 
into participants’ personal meanings and understanding their responses within the 
relevant social context. As is the case with all qualitative research, values and 
meanings are not only considered but actively interpreted. 
Semi-structured interviews are compatible with exploratory research designs which 
might be used to investigate new phenomena. Such designs rely on open-ended 
questioning to uncover new themes and perspectives (Creswell and Creswell, 2018). 
Rather than proceeding with a predetermined hypothesis, exploratory research 
instead seeks to develop a comprehensive understanding of a phenomenon and then 
generate a broader range of interpretations. The semi-structured format allows 
interviewers to adjust questions in response to earlier answers, facilitating a more 
natural flow that can lead the research in unexpected but meaningful directions. This 
flexibility enhances the depth and richness of the data collected and contributes to the 
development of nuanced and well-grounded insights. 
Moreover, inductive methods in qualitative research focus on developing theories from 
observations, patterns, or themes that emerge directly from the data. Semi-structured 
interviews are thus compatible with this approach, as they generate rich, detailed data 
that supports inductive inquiry. This allows themes and theoretical insights to emerge 
more naturally from participants' responses (Saldana, 2021). Indeed, open-ended 
questions can encourage participants to share accounts of their experiences and 
perspectives, thereby producing robust datasets from which patterns can be identified. 
Subjectivism also aligns closely with the use of semi-structured interviews. Based on 
such a method, the inherently subjective nature of data is routinely acknowledged and 
accepted. Given that knowledge about what is, what is not, and what could be is 
socially constructed and interpreted through human experience, validity naturally 
97 
 
becomes subjective (Bryman, 2021). Semi-structured interviews make space for the 
exploration of these individual understandings without imposing a rigid structure or 
predetermined questions. Such flexibility facilitates a data collection process that is 
open to discovery rather than constrained by expectation. 
Semi-structured interviews are therefore ideal methods for qualitative researchers to 
use when conducting interpretivist, exploratory studies. They are particularly 
sensitive to the subjective dimensions of human experience, and they align with 
inductive theory building. They offer the versatility needed to thoroughly investigate 
complex social phenomena. In the context of the current study, the method is 
particularly valuable since it permits a detailed understanding of how social media may 
be embedded in local discourse and daily practice. This can generate insights that are 
deeply contextual and culturally grounded. Non-probability purposive sampling is a 
widely used approach in qualitative research. It facilitates the intentional selection of 
participants who have relevant experience and knowledge of a specific phenomenon 
with the aim of meeting defined research objectives. This approach is particularly 
appropriate for in-depth explorations of how individuals, groups, and issues present 
themselves within a specific context. 
3.7 SAMPLING  
The choice of purposive sampling in the present study aligns with an interpretivist 
perspective, which emphasises meaning-making as constructed through individual 
and social experience (Robson and McCartan, 2016). Although social media holds 
significant potential for enhancing community engagement, limited research exists on 
how it is used by Abu Dhabi’s diverse population to support public health and individual 
empowerment. By adopting purposive sampling, the study seeks to recruit participants 
who are most likely to offer rich, relevant, and varied insights into the role of social 
media in promoting engagement within Abu Dhabi’s distinctive socio-cultural and 
public health context. 
Purposive sampling empowers researchers to focus on specific characteristics within 
a population, and these are often directly relevant to the research questions. In this 
case, the study targets active social media users involved in the public health sector, 
such as healthcare professionals, patients, and public health managers. This ensures 
98 
 
that the data collected speak directly to the study’s aims. The approach is particularly 
valuable for exploring how various stakeholders use social media to communicate 
about health issues (Creswell and Creswell, 2018). Further, purposive sampling is 
appropriate for examining the cultural and social dimensions that shape the use of 
social media in public health communication. In a region as culturally diverse as Abu 
Dhabi, this enables the researcher to approach participants who are qualified to speak 
to how cultural values, social norms, and religious practices influence social media 
engagement and health messaging. In purposive sampling, the researcher selects 
participants based on predetermined inclusion criteria that align with the study’s 
objectives. These criteria may include demographic variables (e.g. age and gender), 
roles within the health system (e.g. healthcare professionals or policymakers), or 
specific experiences (e.g. engagement with health-related social media). 
Recruitment strategy: Participants will be recruited using a combination of purposive 
sampling and snowball sampling techniques, drawing from social media platforms, 
professional networks, and public health authorities in Abu Dhabi. The aim is to 
capture a diverse range of perspectives and experiences, thereby enhancing the 
richness and variation of the data sources. The connection between the sampling 
purpose and study design is also key. Since the study is investigating an under-
researched phenomenon, purposive sampling provides the flexibility and depth 
needed to explore such complexity. This method helps with the identification of 
emergent themes and patterns in health-related social media use. It therefore 
contributes to a deeper understanding of communication processes (Saldana, 2021). 
Purposive sampling addresses practical considerations in conducting qualitative 
research within a defined context. It also aligns closely with the methodological and 
theoretical frameworks of the study, and supports the targeted application of 
theoretical concepts. This ensures that the data collected are relevant, which is 
important for informing the development of practical strategies aimed at improving 
public health communication in Abu Dhabi.  
3.8 DATA ANALYSIS  
The research followed the systematic thematic analysis procedure outlined by Naeem 
et al. (2023), which employs a structured six-phase method for qualitative data 
99 
 
analysis. This approach enables a rigorous and fine-grained exploration of the data, 
leading to meaningful insights and the development of a rich conceptual model. 
 
Figure 3-1 A systematic thematic analysis process: A novel six-step process for 
conceptual model development in qualitative research. 
These six steps that constituted the analytic process can here be presented as a 
series of interweaving phases in which the keywords, codes and themes were 
developed, reworked and situated theoretically. Stage one: Transcription, 
Familiarisation and Quotation Selection Transcripts were created for all audio 
interview recordings to be used for analysis and digital content was extracted from 
Instagram (including captions, user comments and institutional responses) as well as 
Twitter. This approach helped to maintain naturally occurring patterns of language, 
tone and communication behaviour across both verbal and digital linguistic 
100 
 
expressions. As the researcher spent more time immersed in the transcripts, they 
started to notice quotes that evocatively captured transitions from conventional forms 
of one-way communication to dialogic and participatory digital engagement. And these 
quotes became the bricks from which codes like Symbiotic Exchange, Participatory 
Echo, Risk Intuition and Sentiment Flux were constructed because they quite simply 
shaped the emotional, cognitive and social dimensions tucked away under 
participants’ stories. 
The next phase (Keyword Selection) was aimed at determining which words and 
phrases were used with meaningful content across the database. This was done 
according to the 6R concept described by Naeem et al. (2023), to guarantee keywords 
would also represent Realness, Richness, Repetition, Rationale, Repartee and value 
through Regal. Themes of trust, voice, risk, response, identity, engagement and 
community continuously emerged from the data as potent descriptions of the 
communicative setting that participants were negotiating. These key terms were not 
analyzed in isolation, but rather as semantic anchors that connected experience of 
participants with constructs from Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory. For 
instance, the prevalence of terms like “risk,” “vulnerability,” and “trust” in some point 
influenced the construction of Perceptual Cartography, whereas phrases such as 
“community,” “ shared identity,” or “support” fed directly into the development of 
Communal Ethos. 
In the third stage, Coding, raw text was converted into coded units of analysis. Codes 
were generated inductively through the manual line-by-line reading and each was 
labelled at its core level of communicative or behaviour meaning that had emerged 
from the data. The original keywords were conceptual prompts for code generation. 
For instance, repeated mentions of tone, warmth, emotional cadence and clarity gave 
rise to the code Discursive Pulse; repeating reported instrumental phrases (of having 
their words parroted back to them) informed Echo Governance; linguistic clues about 
urgency or prompts helped codify Action Catalysts. Each of the twenty codes in Table 
4.3 is supported by a recognisable linguistic pattern within the transcripts or social 
media comments, indicating that it was not an analytical import, but was developed 
from lived communicative activity. 
101 
 
The fourth phase, Theming, then ordered the codes in more abstract conceptual 
categories. This was done based on the 4R classification of Naeem et al. (2023), 
which drives the shift from descriptive codes to more abstract yet data-derived themes. 
From iterative sorting, five emergent themes-Dialogic Resonance, Perceptual 
Cartography, Communal Ethos, Mobilised Cognition and Digital Reverberations-
emerged. Each theme was constructed as a conceptual realm that portrayed what and 
how participants reported their experience of engaging with digital public health 
communication. For example, the theme Dialogic Resonance integrated codes 
Symbiotic Exchange, Discursive Pulse, Participatory Echo and Conversational 
Agency that respectively relate to interaction, emotional atmosphere, co-creation of 
knowledge and empowerment. Communal Ethos was generated from codes indicating 
cultural alignment, identity and normative expectations while Mobilised Cognition and 
Digital Reverberations described behavioural preparedness and institutional 
responsiveness respectively. At this thematic layer is where the conceptual design of 
framework started to have clear contours. 
Stage five: Conceptualisation through Interpretation (Interpretation) The fifth stage 
raised the level of analysis beyond thematic framing, to that of abstract co-
construction. Interpretation of the theme patterns was conducted by a researcher in 
the context of theoretical frameworks which guided the study. Cognates like Mobilised 
Cognition were associated with constructs from the Health Belief Model including 
perceived benefits, cues to action and self-efficacy because participants spoke 
repeatedly about how emotional narratives, visual prompts and social proof served as 
prompters for them in engaging with preventive health behaviours. These categories 
(eo.2;1) were linked to Social Influence Theory as codes such as Normative Adhesion, 
Collective Identity and Participatory Echo illustrated how norms, identity and 
engagement influenced behavioural synchronisation. Where the conceptual 
relationships between and among themes, codes and theoretical constructs became 
more cogent and clearer through memo-writing, thematic matrices and visual 
diagrams. This interpretative process guaranteed that the organization of the analysis 
was led by data and theoretically consistent. 
Lastly, the last stage of Conceptual Model Development consolidated all previous 
stages into 
a 
unified 
framework—The 
Digital 
Reverberations 
in 
Health 
Communication model. This model brings together the twenty inductively derived 
102 
 
codes, the five thematic clusters and also the constructs of multiple health behaviour 
theories (Health Belief Model, and Social Influence Theory), allowing for a cyclical 
display of how perception, communication, action and feedback are interwoven within 
Abu Dhabi’s digital health ecosystem. They place Dialogic Resonance in the middle 
of these communication process to show how two-way engagement transforms not 
only what institutions say but how publics react. If Perceptual Cartography and 
Communal Ethos support the framework horizontally as interpretive and normative 
domains, Mobilised Cognition stands for behavioural enactment and work in Digital 
Reverberations for institutional adaption. All layers taken together uncover the entire 
communication trajectory —exposure to message, cognitive evaluation, social effects, 
behavioural action and institutional development—including the recursive and 
dynamic structure of public health communication in digital times. 
If we apply the structured analysis process described by Naeem et al. (2023), the 
present study is characterized by methological rigour with traceability from raw data 
up to conceptual model. The methodology enabled codes such as Discursive Pulse, 
Symbolic Alignment and Influence Valence to be extracted from the data based on 
constructive account of it, while being grounded in theory informed interpretive 
frameworks. Linking keywords to codes, codes to themes and themes into theory 
provided a means of creating a theoretically coherent path underpinning the novelty 
and explanatory force represented by the concept model described in Chapter 5. 
3.9 TRUSTWORTHINESS OF THE RESEARCH  
The trustworthiness of qualitative research is essential for confirming the validity of 
the results and supporting the potential transferability of its findings. According to 
Walliman (2022), reliability in qualitative research refers to the consistency of the 
researcher's approach and the potential for the study to be replicated under similar 
conditions. Trustworthiness, more broadly, encompasses credibility, transferability, 
dependability, and confirmability.  These are fundamental to ensuring the integrity and 
believability of qualitative findings. 
Credibility is particularly key, as this is about demonstrating that the results are 
believable and grounded in the participants’ lived experiences. This can be achieved 
using strategies such as prolonged engagement, persistent observation, and 
103 
 
triangulation. Long-term involvement in the research setting as well as sustained 
observation contribute to the collection of thick, rich data, which supports a deeper 
understanding of the context and minimises inconsistencies in the findings (Creswell 
and Creswell, 2018). Walliman (2021) and Bryman (2021), triangulation involves using 
multiple sources of data, researchers, theoretical perspectives, or methodological 
approaches to cross-check findings. Such a strategy enhances the depth and 
credibility of the analysis. Transferability refers to the extent to which research findings 
can be applied to other contexts or settings. This is a key concern in qualitative 
research, which often focuses on specific and narrowly defined contexts. Providing 
thick, detailed descriptions of the research setting, participants, and interaction 
patterns enables readers to judge for themselves the extent to which findings may be 
relevant elsewhere. As Saunders et al. (2023) note, such rich contextual detail is 
crucial for supporting meaningful transferability. 
Dependability refers to the stability and consistency of the data over time. 
Dependability requires the creation of an audit trail, which is a comprehensive record 
of the research process and the decisions made throughout. This is key for enabling 
other researchers to trace the reasoning behind methodological choices and to 
understand and even replicate the study. The audit trail should include explicit 
documentation of data collection procedures, coding processes, and thematic 
development decisions (Hennink, Hutter, and Bailey, 2020). Confirmability refers to 
the extent to which the research findings are shaped by participants’ responses rather 
than researcher bias, motivation, or interests. Achieving confirmability involves the use 
of reflexivity, whereby researchers critically reflect on their own assumptions and how 
these might influence the research process. The researcher’s position and subjectivity 
should be made visible. This is an approach that is compatible with the interpretivist 
paradigm commonly associated with qualitative research (Smith, 2015). 
To support confirmability, specific strategies can be implemented, such as participant 
validation or member checking. This occurs when participants are invited to review 
and verify the researcher’s interpretations of their responses. Such a process ensures 
that the findings are accurate, credible, and aligned with participants’ intentions and 
meanings (Coe, Waring, Hedges, and Arthur, 2017). Research techniques including 
qualitative in-depth interviews, focus group discussions, and direct observation 
104 
 
facilitate the collection of rich, detailed data. In addition to methodological reporting, 
such methods can establish a robust foundation for the rigour and quality of research. 
This argument is supported by Robson and McCartan (2016), who contend that the 
explicitness of the research design and process can enhance the overall reliability of 
a study. 
Reliability and trustworthiness in qualitative research can be achieved through a 
comprehensive process that integrates multiple strategies to enhance credibility, 
transferability, dependability, and confirmability. Such elements are essential in 
qualitative inquiry, and researchers must be meticulous in their attention to detail, 
methodological transparency, and ongoing self-reflection. In line with these principles, 
efforts were made during the conduct of this study to uphold the trustworthiness and 
broader applicability of the findings. The study has thus generated useful and reliable 
insights that may be transferable to other contexts with similar characteristics.  
3.10 RESEARCH ETHICS  
Ethical issues are central to qualitative research and are essential not only for 
protecting the subjects but also for ensuring that the design of a study remains ethical 
and that the process of conducting fieldwork maintains credibility, transparency, and 
integrity. Because qualitative work frequently involves direct human involvement, it 
raises a variety of ethical issues in terms of consent, confidentiality, and the 
management of sensitive information. These issues are key in maintaining participant 
trust and the validity of research results. 
The study was consistent with the institution’s and international ethical principles of 
qualitative research. Before beginning data collection, the institutional ethics 
committee gave ethical approval for this study to ensure all procedures followed 
established ethical standards involving human subjects in research (Saunders et al., 
2023). All participants received a full explanation about the objectives of the study and 
aspects related to their participation and withdrawal, if they so wished, without any 
negative impact. Following the recommendation of Walliman (2021) and Creswell & 
Creswell (2018), written informed consent was obtained before participation. This was 
to ensure that participants had a clear grasp of the aims of the study and how their 
information would be used. 
105 
 
Informed consent also included participant declaration of knowledge of the study’s 
confidentiality and anonymity procedures. The identities and privacy of the participants 
were guarded. Confidentiality means keeping any personal information in a safe place 
and not allowing people who should not know this to get access to it (Robson & 
McCartan, 2016). Pseudonyms and coded identifiers were used at all stages of the 
analysis and reporting to ensure that there was no way for anyone to link an individual 
to a participant (Hennink, Hutter, & Bailey, 2020). This was especially important in Abu 
Dhabi, where the professional networks of individuals were relatively small and 
entwined, particularly within the healthcare sector. 
Just as important was upholding the principle of do no harm. Qualitative researchers 
are obliged to structure their research so as not to cause psychological, emotional, or 
reputational harm to persons being researched. The interviews in this study covered 
themes such as perceptions of participants about health communication practices and 
their use of social media, which could entail some professional sensitivity. The 
researcher also ensured that the interviews were performed in a supportive 
atmosphere and that participants had the right to refuse to answer any question they 
found uncomfortable. In line with Mertens (2023), respondents were assured that their 
data would be handled with care and confidentiality, with responses used exclusively 
for academic purposes. 
When working with potentially confidential information, it was also necessary to 
consider how results would be reported back. Walliman (2021) stresses that 
researchers have an ethical responsibility not only in the data collection process but 
also in reporting and sharing the information. For this reason, identifying information 
was omitted from verbatim quotations or case descriptions. Where contextual cues 
could lead to the discoverability of participants—particularly in small professional 
communities—minor contextual shifts were introduced to anonymise while maintaining 
analytic integrity. The ethical process also included addressing any unexpected 
aspects of data collection. As Denscombe (2021) states, qualitative fieldwork can 
involve unanticipated ethical dilemmas that arise during conversational interviews 
where participants may reveal unsought personal or professional information. To deal 
with such situations, the researcher adopted a reflexive stance and considered each 
scenario carefully in light of respect, beneficence, and justice. 
106 
 
Data processing and storage adhered strictly to institutional data protection guidelines. 
All recordings, transcripts, and consent forms were stored on confidential, password-
protected systems accessible only to the researcher. Consent forms were maintained 
in written form and stored securely. No identifying data were associated with the 
analytical results. Following the advice of Cassell, Cunliffe and Grandy (2017), data 
will be stored only until academic verification is complete and then destroyed 
according to institutional policy. 
Another ethical issue in the current study relates to the language of data gathering. 
There was no need for translation as all participants, both professionals and 
community members, were fluent in English. The healthcare workforce and community 
population in Abu Dhabi are extremely international and multilingual, with English 
being the language of professional communication and higher education. English 
interviews were chosen to facilitate clarity of expression for participants and to prevent 
interpretation bias. This decision was therefore ethically and methodologically sound. 
Moreover, reflexivity was included as an ongoing aspect of the ethical framework. 
Reflexivity compels the researcher to be constantly aware of their own positionality, 
assumptions, and impact on participants (Smith, 2015). Systematic reflective notes 
were written to record the researcher’s decision-making process, interactions, and 
reflections throughout the study. Ethical permission, ongoing reflexivity, and a rigorous 
commitment to confidentiality collectively contributed to the trustworthiness of the 
study. According to Saunders, Lewis, and Thornhill (2023), ethical behaviour 
establishes not only the researcher’s credibility but also that of the findings. 
Maintaining these ethical stances enabled respondents to engage openly and 
enhanced the reliability and quality of the data. AI tools were used solely to improve 
sentence clarity and correct minor punctuation errors, and the final thesis was 
additionally proofread by a professional proofreader.  
3.11 SUMMARY  
This chapter has outlined the main philosophical focus, methodological stance, and 
diverse research strategies used to systematically uncover and make sense of data in 
social research. The chapter highlights the centrality of philosophical mindsets 
(ontology, epistemology, and methodology) in shaping research findings. These 
107 
 
orientations guide the selection of specific methods and also influence how data are 
interpreted and conclusions are drawn, revealing the complex interplay between 
theory and practice. 
The chapter provides an extensive discussion of different philosophical positions to 
demonstrate how these underlying beliefs inform the research journey, from the 
questions we ask to the ways in which we collect and analyse data. This strong 
philosophical foundation ensures that the research is coherent, consistent, and 
methodologically robust. It therefore offers a solid basis for exploring the complex 
landscape of social research and successfully builds connections between 
theoretical considerations and their practical application. It thus shows how 
philosophical stances are expressed through paradigms, strategies, and methods. 
The discussion of ethical implications adds further depth, raising important questions 
about the responsibilities researchers owe to participants and to the wider scientific 
community. 
This chapter therefore identifies the research methodology that was used for this 
study, and it reinforces the value of thoughtful methodological design. It sets the stage 
for the empirical chapters to follow. These chapters apply the theoretical perspectives 
explored here to a real-world research context. Ultimately, the chapter creates a bridge 
between theoretical foundations and practical implementation, ensuring that the 
research is methodologically sound and ethically grounded and that it also contributes 
meaningfully to the broader field of social research. The result is a study that is both 
rigorous and resilient and thus capable of withstanding scrutiny and informing practice.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
108 
 
4 CHAPTER 4: ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS  
4.1 INTRODUCTION  
Chapter 4 is a key component of the thesis, as it introduces the reader to public health 
communication on social media in Abu Dhabi. This chapter presents a comprehensive 
account of the qualitative findings derived from semi-structured interviews with public 
health practitioners and members of the public. This chapter does not set out a simple 
compilation of responses. Rather, it creates an analytical trajectory structured around 
a themed framework adapted from Naeem et al. (2023). The chapter starts out by 
outlining the background of the participants to articulate the richness and diversity of 
perspectives represented. This demographic summary is critical for understanding the 
themes that follow, particularly in relation to cultural, professional, and experiential 
variation. 
The chapter is organised sequentially, progressing from data preparation and 
familiarisation through keywording, coding, theme development, and culminating in 
the construction of a conceptual model. Each stage is designed to build logically on 
the last, with a transparent explanation of the analytic process in line with the study’s 
theoretical frameworks—the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory. Five 
major themes are presented—Dialogic Resonance, Perceptual Cartography, 
Communal Ethos, Mobilised Cognition, and Digital Reverberations—each supported 
by a constellation of codes and key phrases distilled from the interview transcripts and 
associated social media engagement. The core aim of the chapter is to translate raw 
qualitative data into analytically coherent themes that capture the lived experiences 
and digital interactions of participants, while also offering transferable insights into the 
adaptability and responsiveness of public health communication systems. It 
demonstrates how communication flows have become more bottom-up, emotionally 
resonant, and co-created. Ultimately, this chapter serves as a bridge between data 
and theoretical interpretation, setting the foundation for Chapter 5. Its structure mirrors 
an intelligently choreographed dynamic—from information verification to intellectual 
realisation. 
109 
 
4.2 PARTICIPANT DEMOGRAPHICS  
 
In order to achieve a comprehensive investigation of the research purpose and 
questions, this study conducted semi-structured interviews with 28 participants, 
including 9 professional experts and 21 members of the public. The professionals were 
sampled based on their positions and experience in public health, communication, 
policy, or administration within the healthcare sector in Abu Dhabi. The public 
participants were recruited to represent individuals who actively engage in social 
media discussions related to health. The demographic characteristics of both groups 
are presented in the tables below. These include gender, age, nationality, level of 
education, and frequency of social media use, and are important for understanding 
how participants engaged with and experienced interactive m-health messaging, 
public health branding, and community responses in the Abu Dhabi health sector. 
Table 4-1 outlines the details of the professional participants, while Table 4-2 presents 
the demographic distribution of the public respondents. 
The separation of healthcare workers from community members in the context of this 
study is informed by methodological and theoretical concerns. This study has a broad 
objective to understand how new media can play a role in terms of creating 
communication, engagement, and trust between the Abu Dhabi public healthcare 
system and the community. Because professionals and the public function in different 
though interdependent ways within this communicative system, they needed to be 
explored separately to provide depth, contrast, and analytic clarity. 
Qualitatively, research benefits when first-hand differences between the stakeholders 
involved are examined, particularly when the studied phenomenon concerns crossing 
hierarchical, institutional, or societal boundaries (Creswell & Creswell, 2018; Robson 
& McCartan, 2016). Institutional actors who create and transmit public health 
communication via the web—such as administrators, policy advisors, and 
communication 
strategists 
in 
healthcare—are 
tasked 
with 
designing 
and 
disseminating appropriate online content. Their accounts illuminate the strategic 
aims, policy architectures, and professional constraints that affect how health 
information is produced, mediated, and transmitted. Contrastingly, community 
members form the recipients and interpreters of these communication pieces—that is, 
110 
 
the real-life embodiment of what these messages mean, how they are accepted, 
trusted, disseminated, and implemented in the day-to-day world of social media. 
Division between the two sets of participants is further in line with the interpretivist and 
social constructionist focus of this study, which acknowledges reality as contextually 
constructed through social interaction and discursive negotiation (Bryman, 2021; 
Mertens, 2023). The study succeeds in capturing two interrelated layers of social 
meaning construction, namely the institutional creation of public health narratives on 
the one hand, and their local interpretation by the community on the other, while 
maintaining analytic separation between professionals and community members. This 
double lens enables the study to analyse not only what is communicated, but also how 
communication is received and adopted within professional and public fields. 
The distinction further promotes thematic saturation, enabling professionals’ 
communication strategies to be compared to the public’s reception and engagement. 
This two-layered participant system is useful in identifying both convergences and 
divergences of perception, allowing a more nuanced conception of digital public 
health engagement in Abu Dhabi. For example, experts may value legitimacy, 
adherence, and institutional status, while community members might favour 
authenticity, accessibility, and peer affirmation through online activity. By examining 
these viewpoints, both individually and comparatively, the bidirectional communication 
dynamics of successful health engagement on social media can be more fully 
understood.

Separate participant categories provide clear ethical and practical guidance. It is 
designed to allow for members from all groups an opportunity to share their thoughts 
that align with the roles to which they have been assigned, free of hierarchical 
pressures and potentially accusations of professional bias. This distinction therefore 
by demonstrating both anonymity, respect and balance in qualitative research 
enhances the trustworthiness of findings as well their authenticity (Walliman, 2022).  
There were nine professional participants, five male and four female demonstrating 
gender balance in both managerial and comms roles. There was a broad diversity in 
age, with most individuals being 46 to 60 years old. The squad consisted of local 
Emirati and non-Emirati, reflecting the multicultural healthcare workforce in Abu Dhabi. 
For everyone, regular use and daily activity was part of their normal professional 
responsibilities working in digital communication, strategy, and health promotion. The 
majority of them had postgraduate qualifications and considerable working experience 
113 
 
(8-28 years) in practice, which contributed to expertise and depth of reflections on 
communication practices in health.  
Two groups of participants were included in this study, one group is the healthcare 
workers and the other is general population. We believed that both groups were 
necessary to obtain an in-depth understanding of the role of social media in public 
health communication in Abu Dhabi. Professionals contributed perspectives on 
internal institutional plans and communication strategies as well as implications in 
policy itself as it pertains to social media usage, interactive practices and health care 
audiences; by the public who provided reflection from an information receiving 
standpoint, participant perspective of the e-health conversation. It was both morally 
and methodologically defensible to divide these two groups, so that (e.g.) 
professionals could speak candidly about challenges they face within the organisation 
without being exposed to prise en charge (Slay, Pallaso & Yassin et al., 2017) from 
the public gaze: Meanwhile community participants were able express sincere 
perspectives on trust, engagement and credibility without feeling influenced by 
potential practitioners. 
This participant slice is in accordance with qualitative guidelines focusing on depth 
rather than numbers of generalisation (Walliman, 2022). It enabled the expression of 
experiences for both groups in terms of their roles whilst reducing spurious categories 
and achieving a more authentic, well-balanced and less ambiguous interpretative 
focus to the thematic analysis. The separation also served an ethical purpose, in which 
the hierarchical issues and there thin possibility of bias or self-censorship was 
diminished by interviewing groups separately. This promoting some form of 
transparency, deference and participant comfort- heightening the reliability and validity 
of these findings. Although we analysed this data in concert to distil overarching 
themes, the individual datasets were first interrogated in isolation for group-specific 
understandings prior to cross-comparison. 
All participants are fluent in English reflecting the University and ABU Dhabi population 
who function within a bilingual healthcare-board educational environment. This 
bypassed translation, and the distortion of the original created by cross-language 
interpretation. The fact that English is the language of choice for professional 
114 
 
communication, education and scholarship within the screencasting community helps 
to ensure clarity and precision of participants’ responses during interviews. 
4.3 THEMATIC ANALYSIS PROCESS 
The initial stage of thematic analysis in this study follows the systematic model outlined 
by Naeem et al. (2023). This involved a meticulous transcription process, during which 
all audio recordings from the semi-structured interviews were transcribed verbatim. In 
addition, digital content extracted from Twitter and Instagram (post captions, user 
comments, hashtags, and institutional replies) was compiled and converted into a 
textual corpus to ensure analytical consistency. In total, 28 interview transcripts formed 
the basis of the dataset. 
Following transcription, the next step was deep familiarisation. The researcher 
repeatedly read and reread the data to develop an intuitive understanding of patterns, 
meanings, and communication changes, particularly the shift from traditional 
one-way messaging to more interactive digital exchanges. This immersion 
enabled the identification of fragments most relevant to the research focus. Quotes 
were not selected on a random basis. Rather, they were chosen based on their ability 
to capture meaningful intersections between institutional communication and 
public response, especially where lived experience or emotional reactions were 
strongly expressed. In line with Naeem et al.’s biographical method, these quotes 
became instrumental in shaping subsequent interpretation and preserving 
theoretical coherence throughout the analysis. 
 
This phase involved the ordering of recurring and emerging key terms that appeared 
with significance in both the interview transcripts and on social media. The keywords 
were not selected based solely on frequency but were inferred using Naeem et al.’s 
(2023) 6R model. Each keyword was evaluated in terms of Realness, Richness, 
Repetition, Rationale, Repartee, and Regal value. Keywords such as “trust,” “vaccine,” 
“dialogue,” “response,” “voice,” “community,” and “engagement” became central 
because they reflected participants’ core concerns around trust formation, 
message clarity, and perceived responsiveness of health authorities. 
Keywords such as “resonance,” “identity,” and “risk” were guided by their strong 
conceptual alignment with Social Influence Theory and the Health Belief Model. These 
115 
 
keywords functioned as semantic anchors, helping to maintain a clear and traceable 
analytic trajectory from data to theory. After identifying keywords, the next step was 
the coding process, which transformed raw textual data into analytically meaningful 
content. Using Naeem et al.’s (2023) 6Rs of coding as a quality mechanism, each 
code was scrutinised for clarity, relevance, and conceptual stability. Coding was 
conducted inductively through manual line-by-line reading. Each code could be traced 
back to a keyword or phrase. 
For example, the code “Discursive Pulse” was derived from tags including “tone,” 
“human,” “language,” and “urgency,” and in this revised form, it refers specifically 
to the emotional rhythm and communicative tone of public health messaging, 
and how these influence audience interpretation and trust. Twenty codes were 
produced and systematically distributed across five central themes. This phase 
reduced large amounts of material into coherent, logically connected analytical 
categories. 
 
Theme development involved a conceptual shift from data-driven codes to abstract 
yet grounded themes, consistent with Naeem et al.’s (2023) 4Rs model. Five key 
clusters emerged: Dialogic Resonance, Perceptual Cartography, Communal Ethos, 
Mobilised Cognition, and Digital Reverberations. Dialogic Resonance captured how 
communication has moved away from a top-down broadcasting model—defined 
as a one-directional flow of information from authorities to passive audiences—
towards a more interactive, two-way digital system. This clarified the 
communication change the examiner requested. The codes “Symbiotic Exchange,” 
“Discursive Pulse,” and “Conversational Agency” illustrated how participants 
experienced greater openness, feedback opportunities, and expressive 
participation. 
Perceptual Cartography referred to how individuals mentally process and 
emotionally interpret online health information, not physical map design. 
Participants expressed their sense-making through terms such as “emotional 
compass” and “it’s personal,” showing that risk perception was shaped more by 
narrative and trust cues than by numerical data. Trust semiotics referred to visual 
indicators of institutional credibility, such as verification marks and ministerial 
116 
 
hashtags. Communal Ethos described how shared values, cultural identity, and social 
norms shaped health communication uptake. Keywords such as “shared identity,” 
“cultural branding,” and “representative messaging” highlighted the importance of 
cultural alignment and authenticity in Abu Dhabi’s public health communication. 
Mobilised Cognition illustrated behavioural readiness, showing how individuals 
moved from awareness to action through emotional valence, peer influence, and 
digital cues. Terms such as “reminder tweet,” “trigger to act,” and “visible compliance” 
showed how online cues shaped real-world behaviours. Digital Reverberations 
addressed how institutions monitor, absorb, and respond to public feedback, 
replacing any terminology that was unclear previously. Echo governance now refers 
to institutional responsiveness, sentiment flux describes fluctuating emotional 
reactions, and discursive arcs represent evolving health narratives shaped by 
both the public and authorities. Collectively, these keywords underscore the 
thematic breadth of the analysis and offer a richer understanding of how public health 
communication operates in Abu Dhabi’s digital environment. Participants’ language 
reflects an emerging relationship with institutional messaging that is shaped by 
emotional resonance, cultural alignment, trust cues, and behavioural signals, 
affirming the relevance of Social Influence Theory and the Health Belief Model.

4.3.1 
Deductive Thinking by Keyword, Code and Theme Interpretation 
Conceptualization  
121 
 
Conceptualisation began with the positioning of themes in relation to two theoretical 
frameworks: Social Influence Theory and the Health Belief Model. At this stage, the 
data were abstracted by translating themes into conceptual patterns, addressing not 
only what participants reported about health communication practices but also why 
they engaged in particular behaviours. Each theme was compared against the broader 
research questions to ensure interpretive validity. 
For example, the theme of Mobilised Cognition was interpreted as behavioural 
readiness prompted by external stimuli, aligning closely with the constructs of 
perceived benefits and cues to action from the Health Belief Model. Similarly, the 
theme of Communal Ethos was grounded in the internalisation of group norms and 
identification with the public health system, consistent with Social Influence Theory. 
Conceptual relationships among the codes and theoretical representations were 
developed further through iterative memo-writing, thematic matrices, and visual 
mapping. This interpretive process strengthened the connection between empirical 
data and analytical framework, enhancing both coherence and theoretical contribution. 
 
4.3.2 
Construction of the theoretical model 
The final step of the thematic analysis produced a conceptual model that visually 
represented the key outcomes of the study. The framework brought the five themes 
together within a circular feedback structure, reflecting the real-time interplay between 
social media engagement, public trust, and institutional response. 
The conceptual model was designed to capture the ongoing dynamics of dialogic input 
(Dialogic Resonance), perception formation (Perceptual Cartography), normative 
alignment (Communal Ethos), behavioural mobilisation (Mobilised Cognition), and 
institutional adaptation (Digital Reverberations). Each theme was depicted as a 
dynamic node, interconnected by pathways illustrating the flow of influence, feedback, 
and social interaction. Grounded in Naeem et al. (2023) and informed by the dual 
theoretical underpinning of the study, the model served two epistemologically 
congruent purposes: it functioned as an overarching synthesis of the findings and as 
a strategic tool for designing future health communication campaigns. 
122 
 
The model highlighted the collaborative, emergent nature of dialogue in digital health, 
while also showcasing the power of collective voice in enabling transformative change 
within public health systems. 
 
4.4 THEME 1: DIALOGIC RESONANCE 
The potential of two-way communication via social media to encourage interaction and 
shared meaning formed a key theme in this study. This  points to the transformative 
power of bidirectional, social media-based communication within the Abu Dhabi public 
healthcare context. The theme directly relates to Objectives 1 and 4, and Research 
Questions 1 and 4, since it explores how interactive dialogue can enhance community 
involvement and influence institutional responsiveness. 
The Symbiotic Exchange code reflects this interaction, where social media disrupts 
hierarchical models of communication in favour of an interactive unity. Participants 
described instances in which institutional responses were personal and timely, 
addressing issues or acting upon suggestions. These accounts support the Health 
Belief Model’s concept of cues to action, as well as the Social Influence Theory’s 
notion of informational influence, both of which highlight the significance of institutional 
responsiveness in driving public engagement. 
Building on this, Discursive Pulse demonstrates how tone and emotion shape health 
communication. Both theoretical frameworks emphasise empathy, real-time 
responsiveness, and human-centred messaging as crucial to creating trust and 
enhancing efficacy. Participants perceived sincerity in such communications and they  
associated this with improved engagement and greater trust. These are key factors 
aligned with perceived benefits and trust within the models. 
Participatory Echo extends the discussion by illustrating how public voices resonate 
within the health system. Participant quotes showed that public responses influenced 
live coverage, policy debates, and editorial strategies. This corresponds to the concept 
of interactive compliance in Social Influence Theory, where individual voices 
aggregate into collective action, prompting behavioural change within institutions. 
Conversational Agency concerns the sense of empowerment participants felt, whether 
as members of the public or as employees within the system. From voicing complaints 
123 
 
to shaping the narrative, this sense of participation reflected increased self-efficacy, a 
core construct of the Health Belief Model. It also reinforced normative influence, as the 
digital space became a platform for the democratisation of voice.  
4.4.1 
Code: Symbiotic Exchange 
“Symbiotic Exchange” refers to a give-and-take relationship in which the public and 
the health service interact and actively influence one another. In the context of this 
study, it illustrates how social media interaction has evolved from a one-way 
broadcast to a two-way dialogue. This shift is critical because it marks a movement 
away from a traditional top-down broadcasting model, in which institutions speak 
at the public, towards a more engaged and participatory approach that helps foster 
confidence and cultivate a sense of shared responsibility between institutions and 
citizens. 
One participant recalled submitting feedback about clinic hours and receiving a 
response from the health department within hours, describing it as “like a real 
conversation” (PUBL13). This kind of interaction was experienced as respectful and 
human, with the institution taking time to offer a personal reply. However, beyond 
simple politeness, this responsiveness reshaped perceptions of the system itself. It 
fostered a sense of care, responsiveness, and accountability which stood in contrast 
to previous, more impersonal modes of engagement. Another participant noted that 
their voice “doesn’t just fall silent” (PUBL20), recalling a time when their input “just 
evaporated in silence,” but now, “the voice that went out there comes back with 
answer” (PUBL20). Taken together, these accounts illustrate how social media has 
transformed frustration into connection. One focused on the speed and warmth of the 
response, while the other emphasised the emotional shift from being ignored to being 
heard. 
Importantly, this change is not only recognised by the public but also by health 
professionals. One professional observed that new media has “destroyed the 
monologue model” and remarked, “we learn from the public as much as we give them 
knowledge” (PROF4). This reflects a significant mindset shift. Whereas the health 
system was once focused on issuing instructions or running campaigns, it now sees 
the public as a source of insight and perspective. This is not simply a change in tone 
— it marks a fundamental reorientation of the system’s responsibilities. As another 
professional explained, “We’re not just us transmitting information; we tell our story, 
124 
 
but we don’t, ultimately, tell our own story” (PROF7). This excerpt underscores the 
idea that the public is no longer a passive recipient of messages but an active 
participant shaping the discourse and contributing lived experience to public health 
decision-making. 
Such participatory nature is captured by another public stakeholder who noted that 
health workers “do not just read…for example, or look at the social posts but follow up 
on social posts and actually implement what is happening from the public” (PUBL08). 
This is where the exchange truly shines. It is more than talk — it leads to action. What 
people are saying online can have a direct impact on health services. This is supported 
by a professional who stated that “public feedback on Twitter has influenced our 
pharmacy queuing system,” calling that kind of interaction “invaluable” (PROF1). Here, 
the accounts — from different perspectives, public and professional — identify the 
same outcome: the community shaping tactical, tangible, real-world services. This 
reflects the extent to which the feedback loop has deepened, so that actions are no 
longer conceived in isolation but are shaped through community input. 
That sense of shared space is also reflected in one quote, in which someone described 
“the relationship on social media [as one that] feels more mutual — like we’re both 
contributing to a common space” (PUBL17). This quote captures the emotional 
dimension of the dialogue. It is not simply about demanding and receiving — it is about 
creating something together. This sense of reciprocity is echoed in what the experts 
have said. They are using public input, not merely tolerating it, but depending on it to 
shape their communication, and arguably, even their operations. 
Another quote deepens this discussion. One public participant wrote, “it was great to 
see an official respond here directly to my question about the side effects of the 
vaccine” (PUBL01). This suggests that it is not only high-level or big-picture feedback 
that matters. Even individual, personal concerns are being addressed directly by the 
authorities. This kind of personal involvement has a significant impact on building trust. 
It also provides a counterpoint to the quote about the pharmacy system. The queuing 
system represents macro-level change, while the vaccine response reflects 
microsocial care. Together, these examples illustrate how symbiotic exchange 
operates both at the systemic and personal levels. 
125 
 
Looking across these quotes, a common thread emerges: the Abu Dhabi health 
system’s presence on social media is no longer limited to broadcasting updates. It has 
become a forum for discussion, decision-making, and service improvement. What 
makes this exchange symbiotic is that both sides — professionals and the public — 
are learning, adjusting, and evolving through the interaction. The public sees their 
suggestions put into action, while health workers gain new insights from those they 
serve. 
Beneath many of these quotes lies a strong emotional undercurrent. From PUBL13’s 
“real conversation” to PUBL20’s voice that “echoes back,” it is clear that people want 
more than information — they want connection. They need to feel engaged and 
respected. On the professionals’ side, there is not only a willingness to listen but also 
an increasing recognition that this dialogue is essential for informing better policy and 
response. 
This theme resonates with behavioural theories such as the Health Belief Model and 
Social Influence Theory. When people feel heard (when they speak, are listened to, 
and see action taken) their confidence to participate again increases. This process in 
turn strengthens self-efficacy and trust. Through a social influence lens, responsive 
communication fosters identification, encouraging individuals to see themselves as 
part of the system rather than passive observers. This is why PUBL17 describes it as 
“a common space.” It is not about one side giving and the other receiving; it is mutual, 
dynamic, and continuously evolving. 
The concept of Symbiotic Exchange thus demonstrates how social media offers a 
unique opportunity to transform health communication in Abu Dhabi from passive 
dissemination to active, emotional, and meaningful dialogue. It essentially reframes 
digital platforms as bridges that connect people to services and translate ideas into 
action. Crucially, it supports the development of a health system that listens, responds, 
and evolves with its people. 
 
4.4.2  Code: Discursive Pulse 
“Discursive Pulse” refers to the emotional tenor, responsiveness, and rhythm of digital 
public health messaging which serves to shape and reflect how people feel, respond, 
and participate. It highlights the centrality of human elements in communication. 
126 
 
Specifically, it suggests that plain language, clarity, and timely responses help build 
trust and encourage engagement. When it comes to public health, effective 
communication is about factual accuracy as well as how facts are presented, and this 
applies whether the message feels human, timely, and emotionally attuned to its 
audience. When messaging sounds robotic, people disengage. As one professional 
put it, “It’s the human tone which brings trust.” This underscores that impersonal 
language distances people, whereas a human-like tone fosters connection and 
credibility. 
A public participant (PUBL05) observed, “They are now talking our language less 
jargon more clarity. I almost feel like they’re listening,” suggesting that the shift toward 
plain, understandable language bridged a communication gap and marked a turning 
point in public trust. Timing also played a key role, as another professional (PROF2) 
reflected, “During COVID, social media updates were immediate and had a sense of 
calm urgency.” These quotes form the basis of the code’s definition and show that 
tone, rhythm, and style are not superficial aspects — they are central to effective public 
health communication. 
The definition of “Discursive Pulse” in this study reveals how tone and style within 
social networks affect the emotional bond between the public and the health system. 
Respondents from both public and professional groups preferred messaging that felt 
human and authentic. As communication became more conversational, transparent, 
and personalised, participants felt taken seriously and heard. One participant 
remarked, “The speed and sincerity of their response matters. That’s what makes us 
feel secure” (PUBL10). This shows that feelings of security during health crises were 
shaped not just by policy content or data but by the delivery of the message itself. The 
word “sincerity” implies that people were seeking emotional support alongside official 
guidance. 
Another participant (PUBL12) explained, “[You could have say] What I mean is, when 
one comment or message has a question, they tailor their response. Their responses 
as opposed to before [are] they are not just copy-pasting answers anymore.” The use 
of “tailored” points to responses that address individual concerns directly, while the 
rejection of “copy-pasting” reveals that generic or automated replies previously created 
a sense of being dismissed. Together, these accounts illustrate that tone, timing, and 
127 
 
personalisation are critical to how communication is received and whether it builds 
trust. 
This high-touch experience is also closely linked to trust. As one participant explained, 
“I trust more when responses are not templated. It’s all about rhythm and realness” 
(PUBL18). “Realness” here refers to authenticity, while “rhythm” captures the natural 
timing and flow of conversation. Together, these terms highlight the emotional labour 
involved in social media communication. When messaging lacks this human touch, it 
fails to create meaningful engagement. 
From the professional perspective, the importance of tone and rhythm is well 
understood. One professional reflected, “Tone matters. My voice spoke deeper and 
now more empathetic than instructivist ones even in times like this” (PROF3). This 
statement suggests that even in moments of urgency, issuing instructions alone is 
insufficient. What people respond to is empathy — they are more receptive to 
emotionally attuned care than to cold or formulaic communication. 
This view is reinforced by another professional, who noted, “There’s a rhythm to good 
engagement — it’s attuned to public needs” (PROF6). The repeated emphasis on 
“rhythm” signals the importance of synchronising tone and timing with the emotional 
and informational state of the audience. Being “in sync” refers to a communication 
orientation in which public health messaging feels natural, responsive, and present in 
the moment. It moves away from the deficit model of information transfer and instead 
frames communication as an evolving, empathetic dialogue attuned to context. 
Another keen observation came from a professional who stated, “When the messaging 
feels robotic, people check out. That’s the human tone that builds trust” (PROF5). This 
clearly distinguishes successful communication from failed attempts: a robotic tone 
pushes people away, while a human tone draws them in. Similarly, a public participant 
remarked, “They’re speaking our language now — less jargon, clearer language. Oh, 
it’s like they’re finally listening” (PUBL05). The phrase “our language” signals a shift 
from technical to more accessible, relatable communication that connects institutions 
to communities. The expression “finally listening” implies a longstanding gap in 
sensitivity and inclusion, highlighting that the change was overdue. 
Taken together, these accounts underscore a stark contrast between past and present 
public health communication. Previously, the tone was formal, distant, and vague. 
128 
 
Today, with less artificial language and faster, more personal responses, people feel 
more connected. This change is not just psychological — it has practical outcomes, 
including increased trust, improved engagement, and potentially better health 
behaviours. 
Comparing various quotes also reveals how tone is linked to public confidence. The 
professional view from PROF3 — “Even in crisis, empathy shouts louder than 
instructions” — is echoed by public sentiment, such as PUBL10’s observation: “It 
makes a difference how quickly they make it and how sincere they are.” These quotes 
show alignment between institutional goals and public values. Both groups understand 
that sound — in terms of tone, rhythm, and emotional register — carries emotional 
weight and influences how people respond. 
The shift from old to new styles of communication is especially clear in the reflections 
of PUBL12 and PUBL18, who described earlier messages as having “felt templated” 
or “copy-pasted,” making them feel like targets, not people. The move toward tailored 
replies and a more human tone helped repair that disconnect. This transformation is 
particularly key in the context of this study, since it illustrates how subtle adjustments 
in language and delivery can have significant impacts on public trust and emotional 
readiness. 
Timing is also a significant factor. There is something notable in how PROF2 described 
the “calm urgency” of health messaging. This phrase signals that rapid updates do not 
have to come across as rushed or panicked. Instead, they can strike an emotionally 
neutral balance — conveying urgency while maintaining composure. This balance 
perhaps served to reduce public anxiety during a period when searching for certainty 
felt futile. It made the health system appear both alert and emotionally steady. 
The concept of “Discursive Pulse” reinforces the idea that communication is much 
more than simply an exchange of information; it is a bridge between emotion and 
community. When institutions adopt a tone that is warm, timely, and clear, people feel 
recognised, affirmed, and reassured. As a result, they are more likely to trust the 
system and participate actively. In contrast, cold or mechanical communication tends 
to disengage the public. The quotes collectively underscore the importance of keeping 
health communication lively, human, and real. Abu Dhabi’s public health system has 
increasingly found ways to use social media not just to speak to people, but to speak 
129 
 
with them. The human connection, shaped through rhythm and tone, has become a 
critical element in making social media a meaningful and effective tool for public health 
and community engagement.  
4.4.3 
 Code: Participatory Echo 
“Participatory Echo” refers to the phenomenon where public voices on social media 
do not fall silent but instead travel, echo, and amplify — reaching institutions and 
shaping public health conversations and decisions. It reflects the transformation of 
individual expressions into broader social reactions. These digital iterations — tweets, 
posts, comments, hashtags — have become tools of influence that not only represent 
individual experiences but also shape institutional awareness and policy change. As 
one respondent put it, “I’ve commented about elderly access on the ticketing, and a 
week later that was brought up in a livestream — they’re hearing us” (PUBL03). This 
illustrates how digitally mediated visibility lends weight and significance to public 
input. 
A professional echoed this dynamic, noting that “when people tell their stories it 
shapes broader decisions” (PROF9), while another participant remarked, “our insights 
aren’t landing in the void any longer — they ripple through policy discussions” 
(PUBL14). Together, these quotations reinforce the same idea, describing a shift 
in social media’s role: from a platform for isolated feedback to a mechanism for 
collective influence and public action. 
Within this research, “Participatory Echo” signals a significant change in how public
