health systems treat community feedback. The public no longer appears to be ignored; 
instead, participants observe that online complaints can generate momentum and 
institutional accountability. The testimonies of PUBL03, PUBL14, and PROF9 
collectively illustrate this shift. The fact that concerns about elderly access were raised 
in a livestream within a week signals a different type of communication channel — one 
that is fast, open, and responsive. 
Rather than being stalled in bureaucratic processes, public input now features in live 
policy discussions. This responsiveness also lends legitimacy to institutional actors. 
Participants no longer feel that they are speaking into a void. Instead, they see 
responses and actions that affirm their contributions, reinforcing trust in the system 
130 
 
and encouraging continued engagement. “Participatory Echo” thus reveals how 
personal stories can evolve into community-wide concerns, and how institutions have 
entered the same digital space where worries are shared, repeated, and amplified. 
The effects of new media devices such as hashtags further amplify the dynamics of 
participatory echo. As PUBL06 observed, “Hashtags seem innocuous, but they serve 
as rallying points — social-media bullhorns for real issues” (PUBL06), highlighting that 
even simple digital tools can draw attention to pressing public health concerns. This is 
echoed by PUBL09: “the more we share, the more the system learns. It’s feedback at 
scale” (PUBL09), and supported by PROF8, who remarked, “The real power is when 
a single post catalyses collective change” (PROF8). Read together, these statements 
suggest that the public health system is no longer the sole producer of knowledge or 
action. In this evolving exchange, the public has come to function as instructor, mirror, 
and guide. 
Collectively, these voices illustrate that public expression now moves beyond 
awareness — it has become a fundamental mechanism through which institutions 
detect needs and shift direction. A single hashtagged post can spotlight an issue, 
shared stories can validate it, and ultimately, the institution responds. This represents 
a digitally enabled form of participatory democracy — continuous, visible, and 
emotionally resonant — embedded within the health system. 
This process is also empowering for the community. When PROF2 stated, “A patient’s 
story, once in the shadows, now sparks public conversations and awareness” 
(PROF2), it spoke to a cultural shift in which everyday voices are now visible. When 
considered alongside PUBL21’s comment, “We don't just hear each other — we 
amplify one another” (PUBL21), and PUBL06’s earlier point about hashtags, the 
message becomes clearer. These participants highlight that social media gives 
individuals a voice and connects them with others who share their concerns. Their 
words are thus not isolated cries, but are echoed, affirmed, and magnified by others. 
This amplification creates a shared sense of influence and power. Personal narratives 
become public ones, and public narratives, in turn, shape the priorities of the health 
system. Through this echo chamber effect, public health is no longer something done 
to people — it becomes something created with them. This is the spirit of 
131 
 
participatory echo: the community in close contact with the system, and the system in 
close contact with the community. 
4.4.4  Code: Conversational Agency 
“Conversational agency” speaks to the capacity of individuals to speak up, challenge 
authority, raise concerns, and engage with health communication on social media on 
their own terms. It is about the power of being heard, of joining the conversation, and 
of seeing one’s input shape real decisions or public messaging. This form of agency 
is particularly significant for those who previously lacked a platform or a safe space to 
express themselves (Sommer, 2460). As one participant reflected, “I can at least ask, 
or challenge, or criticize, we can do all kinds of things that we never could before” 
(PUBL16), pointing to the new expressive space that the digital world has created. 
Another participant put it more starkly: “For a lot of us where I'm from, social media is 
the only safe place to talk that shit without fear” (PUBL07), suggesting that social 
media transcends social and institutional boundaries and offers a form of protection. 
A professional echoed this by stating, “Empowerment starts when people feel listened 
to and social media is that microphone” (PROF3). Across these quotations, the 
essence of this code emerges: empowerment, expression, and the expectation of 
response. 
The “Conversational Agency” code in this study captures a broader shift in how people 
understand their role in public health communication. Individuals are thus no longer 
simply passive recipients of health information. Rather, they are contributors, critics, 
and co-creators. Social media offers a space where people can speak out without 
being silenced by fear, authority, or institutional filtering. The perspectives of PUBL16, 
PUBL07, and PROF3 illustrate this transition. Their comments suggest that expression 
is no longer the exclusive domain of professionals or experts. Members of the public 
including those who were previously marginalised — now feel that they have a voice. 
The phrase “agency we never had before” speaks directly to a history of exclusion. In 
earlier forms of public health communication, information flowed one way — from 
authority to audience, representing a classic top-down broadcasting model in 
which institutions transmitted messages without inviting dialogue. That flow has now 
shifted. Social media platforms like Twitter and Instagram enable the public to 
132 
 
challenge, question, and even redirect official messaging. The metaphor of social 
media as a “microphone” reveals how once-muted voices are now amplified rather 
than suppressed. This development has major implications for community organising, 
as it signals that individuals who once had no traditional power are now participating 
meaningfully in the system. 
This new power is not only about speaking. Rather, it is about witnessing what 
happens when you speak. PUBL11 described “The ability to speak, be heard, and see 
and what will help the body that society has” (PUBL11), while PUBL15 noted, “We 
went from recipient to participant in our own health conversations” (PUBL15), and 
PROF6 reflected, “We’re not just reaching the people; we’re letting the people reach 
us” (PROF6). Together, these voices illuminate the same underlying shift: the public 
is no longer passively receiving information, but is actively contributing to the formation 
of public health messaging. 
People are now part of the process in the sense that they are not just being addressed, 
but are helping to shape what gets said and done. The system therefore listens, 
responds, and adapts. These quotes suggest that conversational agency becomes 
real when people speak and when institutions respond meaningfully. This reciprocal 
exchange enhances the public’s sense of ownership and value. It encourages 
individuals to see the health system not only as something that serves them but 
as something that represents them. 
The strength of this code lies in its universality. As PUBL04 noted, “I’ve seen people 
with no formal education be health advocates, the whole nine yards, on Instagram” 
(PUBL04), illustrating how digital technology can transcend traditional barriers. When 
read 
alongside 
PROF7’s 
statement, 
“Digital 
platforms 
have 
redistributed 
communicative power in healthcare” (PROF7), and PUBL19’s observation, “People 
now shape the discourse, not just consume it” (PUBL19), a clear narrative emerges: 
social media has reshaped who gets to speak, who gets to be heard, and who gets to 
lead. Public health dialogue is no longer confined to experts — it now includes people 
from all walks of life. Regardless of formal education, individuals can spark stories, 
respond to others, and drive change. 
133 
 
Redressing this imbalance in communicative power acknowledges that health is a 
shared, collective endeavour. This suggests that everyone has the right to participate 
in shaping how it is spoken about. Ultimately, these perspectives reveal that 
“Conversational Agency” is not simply about having a voice. It is about understanding 
the impact of that voice — about freedom, equality, and power. In Abu Dhabi’s 
healthcare landscape, this agency is helping shift communication from a one-way 
broadcast to a two-way dialogue in which people feel seen, heard, and empowered to 
effect change. 
4.5 THEME 2: PERCEPTUAL CARTOGRAPHY 
This section sets out how people perceive COVID-19 messages from both cognitive 
and emotional standpoints. It corresponds to Objective 2 and Research Question 2, 
which focus on how individuals make sense of, interpret, and personally process public 
health news through social media. The emphasis here is therefore on the 
psychological and affective dimensions of communicating health risks and benefits. 
The code Risk Intuition illustrates that health threats are not primarily understood 
through scientific facts — like the sight of a microbe — but through how health officials 
frame conditions such as flu, SARS, or monkeypox. These perceptions are shaped 
through narrative cues, with users describing heightened feelings of vulnerability 
when people they knew, or who lived nearby, were affected. This maps onto the 
susceptibility and severity constructs in the Health Belief Model (HBM) and also aligns 
with informational social influence in Social Influence Theory — where individuals 
adjust their health perceptions based on the shared experiences of their community. 
Trust Semiotics shows that credibility is not solely about content but is symbolically 
conveyed. Features such as verified identities, professional presentation, and familiar, 
clear language serve as visual and linguistic signals of trust. This code underlines the 
idea that public evaluation of authority is increasingly based on aesthetics and 
communicative cues — design and presentation serve as social proof, reinforcing the 
authenticity of messages and building confidence in their source. 
Benefit Horizon centres on users’ anticipation of positive outcomes. Participants spoke 
of taking action, such as scheduling health screenings, after seeing content that 
134 
 
communicated health gains. This resonates with perceived benefits and cues to action 
in the HBM, as well as with the role of internalised social norms in Social Influence 
Theory, where the anticipation of future rewards shapes current decisions. 
However, this optimism often collided with Cognitive Dissonance. Conflicting advice 
and mixed messages triggered internal conflict and a kind of “what the hell” reaction, 
which eroded trust. Information overload led some users to disengage from health 
discussions entirely. This response represents a significant perceived barrier and 
underscores the fragile nature of trust when public communication lacks clarity, 
coherence, and synchrony. 
4.5.1  Code: Risk Intuition 
“Risk Intuition” is the name for the personal, emotional way people understand and 
feel health risks, especially in times of crisis like the COVID-19 pandemic. It is not 
only in the data or the official announcements; it is in the way people experience 
vulnerability through others’ stories, posts, and social cues on social media. This code 
demonstrates that numbers and graphs are insufficient, in many cases, to raise 
awareness, prompt action, or mobilise support. What engages people instead are 
personal stories that make the risk seem real and feel close. 
As one public participant shared, “I didn’t need no graph to feel the risk‐ them social 
posts about young victims made it real” (PUBL02). “It felt like each tweet was ringing 
a warning bell in my gut” (PUBL05) also demonstrates that emotional responses may 
be more powerful than rational ones. “It’s not the stats that sell us; it’s the scary stories 
that make the threat feel real” (PROF1). Even health experts acknowledge the 
persuasive power of emotional storytelling. 
These observations clarify how “Risk Intuition” arises from emotional cues and past 
experience instead of mere facts. The interpretation of “Risk Intuition” in this study 
suggests that people’s interpretation of danger in the context of a health crisis is 
powerfully emotional, and often dictated by what they see online, rather than through 
formal health campaigns. A broad range of participants described how their personal 
sense of risk was heightened more by personal stories than by government reports or 
statistics. 
135 
 
As PUBL02 said, “I didn’t need a chart to feel the risk — those social posts about 
young victims made it real” (PUBL02). This could be because stories from one’s own 
life on social media can evoke emotional responses more than numbers can. PUBL18 
also added, “When I read that a neighbour had it I felt quite safe suddenly” (PUBL18). 
Here, knowing someone in person, or even seeing someone nearby, made the threat 
feel immediate. The threat was no longer remote or speculative. It felt close, personal 
and suddenly deadly serious. 
This affective proximity is also audible in the words of PUBL07: “I didn’t think I was at 
risk until I saw the symptoms of someone my age… till then, I was takin it lightly. That 
hit me” (PUBL07). These stories illustrate that people judge their own vulnerability not 
based on national headlines or expert-prepared graphs, but by comparison with those 
they feel personally connected to. Once they know that someone their age, or 
someone nearby, is sick, it becomes easier to imagine they could be next. This creates 
a strong emotional bond that motivates people to take action, or at least be more 
cautious. 
Intuitively, this view is well supported by the professional perspective in a quote from 
PROF1: “It is not statistics that persuade us — it is stories that make the threat 
personal” (PROF1). This observation bridges the gap between expert knowledge and 
public feeling, suggesting that both groups recognise the persuasive power of 
narrative. Some participants also noted that local context and emotional signals 
mattered more than abstract data. “I also get the feeling that when the results of local 
tests are being announced, I felt more awake. It made danger tangible.” The word 
“tangible” is crucial here, because it suggests that risk — once invisible — became 
something people could physically or emotionally perceive. Not general reports, but 
posts that hit close to home. 
PUBL05 said similarly, “When the numbers were high, every tweet felt like a warning 
bell in my gut” (PUBL05). This quote perfectly underscores how emotion, not logic, 
can drive comprehension. Instead of absorbing what the daily reports had to say, 
people followed the lead of their feelings, shaped by what they were seeing posted 
and shared in personal stories. 
136 
 
There is also a telling distinction between the psychology of formal media and social 
media when it comes to emotional sway. PUBL11 said, “What the media didn’t do, one 
post from a nurse did — it changed the way I saw my own risk” (PUBL11). This 
suggests that traditional news may not have made the risk feel personal, while a single 
post from a real person had an outsized impact. This distinction highlights the 
particular relevance of social media for public health communication. Because 
messages often come from like-minded individuals — usually average people posting 
on platforms like Twitter or Instagram — it becomes easier for others to feel the 
emotion and internalise the message as something that also belongs to them. It is not 
about reading something — it is about feeling something. 
PROF4 describes this emotional connection clearly: “There's an emotional compass 
we all use and that social media guides more than official bulletins” (PROF4). The 
phrase “emotional compass” suggests that individuals rely on emotion to assess what 
is risky and what is not. In such cases, social media takes the lead by providing 
emotional cues in the form of stories, videos, and comments. Official bulletins may 
offer precise information — the facts — but a truth devoid of sensibility may not 
resonate as deeply. 
It is also interesting to note the disparity between official sources and social media. 
Numbers and bulletins may discuss percentages and infection rates, but those figures 
do not always bring a sense of personal relevance. Conversely, if you see a post from 
a nurse, a neighbour, or someone your own age, it becomes easier to picture yourself 
in that situation. As reflected in the quote from PUBL07, the transition from “I didn’t 
think” to “That hit me” signifies a sudden identification with an issue — one that arises 
not from a cognitive understanding of data, but through emotional reflection. 
Employing stories, particularly from laypeople, alters how data is received and 
processed. This emotional processing is central to Risk Intuition. When people prefer 
stories to data, this is not because they are uninformed or irrational, but more likely 
because they are seeking meaning through narrative. This is where social media plays 
a crucial role. It helps people understand not just intellectually but emotionally — and 
this emotionally charged understanding leads to behavioural change. 
137 
 
It may be the reason someone starts wearing a mask, avoiding crowds, or getting 
vaccinated not because of a chart, but because they heard a story that felt too real to 
ignore. These voices are collective, and the message they share is this: in a climate 
of risk, feelings of risk are constructed gradually, through small, emotional encounters 
on social media. People do not wake up and feel fearful because a report told them 
to. They feel panicked after reading a singular, personal account, seeing a case in 
their town, or encountering a relatable post. 
This emotional response is intense and has a profound effect on public behaviour. It 
is also tied to trust. People are more likely to believe in, and act on information when 
it emanates from someone they perceive as one of their own. “Risk Intuition” also 
demonstrates the influence of emotional stories on social media in terms of shaping 
how people perceive and react to health threats. It suggests that facts alone are not 
sufficient, and what truly resonates with people is feeling. They want to see themselves 
in the story, and social media ostensibly makes that possible. It helps turn a distant, 
abstract risk into something that feels close, relevant, and personal. This emotional 
process is fundamental to how social media facilitates public health communication. 
In that sense, social media is not only about disseminating information but about 
shaping understanding and motivating people to care and to act. Through emotional 
storytelling, social media becomes a powerful tool to help people recognise risk and 
make healthier decisions. 
4.5.2 Code: Trust Semiotics 
On social media, people judge credibility based on visual cues, symbols, and 
presentation styles — a concept referred to as Trust Semiotics. These are not merely 
decorative features, but meaningful ones. They are designed to help people decide 
whether to trust a message or disregard it. “Things like the blue verification check, 
health authority logos, official photographs or language selection can also contribute 
to [the illusion of legitimacy].” If a doctor is shown in scrubs or a post features an official 
ministry logo, people are more likely to trust the content. “It shows more than titles; 
what it communicates is validity,” said one public participant (PUBL12). This is 
because symbols operate as signals of authority in the digital space. 
138 
 
Likewise, one respondent noted: “Picture of a doctor in scrubs with so-called real name 
tag says more than any press release” (PROF5), showing that visual trust signals are 
often more compelling than formal announcements. These are the small details upon 
which Trust Semiotics is built — simple, powerful signs that inspire confidence in the 
message being delivered. 
The concept of Trust Semiotics, as constructed in this study, emphasises that 
individuals rely on visual and symbolic signs when evaluating online health 
information. This is particularly the case when it comes to making quick decisions 
about whether to trust or reject such information. These signals become especially 
crucial during health crises, when people are inundated with information and need fast 
ways to distinguish truth from misinformation. Several participants described how 
small but significant cues shape their trust. 
PUBL12 said that “The blue tick means more than any title — it means legitimacy” 
(PUBL12), highlighting that online, a small icon can carry more authority than a 
person’s real-world job title. This blue tick, used by verified accounts, creates the 
impression that the person or organisation is real, official, and accountable. For users 
trying to navigate constant health updates, this kind of symbol acts as a shortcut for 
identifying trustworthy information. 
This perception of visual trust is supported by PUBL06, who said, “What I noticed is 
that I trusted posts that had a logo and a ministry hashtag. They feel more grounded” 
(PUBL06). The word “grounded” here refers to a sense of stability — something 
backed by institutional force and structure. This introduces another layer: trust is not 
just about who posts the content, but how it is framed. Thus, a single hashtag or official 
government logo can make an otherwise mundane post seem credible. PUBL15 
added, “Whenever they are speaking in Arabic and looking at real people instead of 
the stock image, I feel as though I can believe them a bit more” (PUBL15). This 
highlights how authenticity also plays a role — not only through the visibility of real 
people but also by using the audience’s language. When health messages align with 
the culture and lived reality of their audience, trust increases. It is not just about visual 
representation — it is also about emotional and cultural validation. 
139 
 
In juxtaposition with professional perspectives, the insights gain depth. According to 
PROF5, “A picture of a doctor in scrubs with a real name tag is worth a thousand press 
releases” (PROF5), since even professionals recognise the power of simple visuals. 
This relates directly to PUBL21’s view: “If it’s from someone in a lab coat, I listen. 
Presentation matters” (PUBL21). Together, these examples show that a lab coat or 
name tag is more than just a garment — it is a visual code that signals expertise and 
reliability. 
Even design choices such as colour, font, and layout influenced how people 
interpreted health messages. PUBL04 wrote, “If I see casual fonts and emojis I scroll 
past, whereas clean design and data visuals stop me” (PUBL04). This indicates that 
people judged content based on its professionalism. When a message seemed casual 
or disorganised, it lost credibility. When it was clean, polished, and structured, it gained 
authority. PROF6 elaborated: “There is power in visual cues — uniforms, badges, 
even the inflections of language. It shapes belief” (PROF6). Taken together, these 
quotes show that trust is not only about information, but also about how the messenger 
presents it — visually and emotionally. 
There was also a more profound observation from PROF2: “Symbolism matters more 
than we’d like to think — it helps strain out what’s noise and what’s truth” (PROF2). 
This suggested that such signs often operate subconsciously. In a fast-paced digital 
ecosystem, users do not analyse every post; instead, they look for cues that help them 
quickly categorise information. When the combined voices were considered, a clear 
pattern emerged. People relied on visual trust cues as emotional footholds. They were 
not purely searching for facts. Rather, they were looking for signals that the information 
was secure, reliable, and familiar. Whether this was a blue tick, a photo of a nurse in 
uniform, or the use of local language and imagery, such signals conveyed the 
consistent message: “This is trustworthy.” 
And yet, such symbols had to be used with caution. If they appeared contrived or 
forced — like stock images or overly casual emojis — they could backfire. PUBL04 
made this clear by noting that people tended to skip posts with casual fonts or emoji-
laden designs. This shows that audiences sensed authenticity, and reacted 
accordingly. 
140 
 
The code Trust Semiotics therefore uncovered that people depended on visual and 
symbolic cues to guide their beliefs and health-related decisions online. These 
symbols — a logo, a uniform, a photograph, a verified icon — helped build fast and 
powerful trust. They allowed users to cut through the complexity of the digital 
environment and quickly determine what to believe. When deployed consciously, 
these trust signals reassured, respected, and informed the public. At a time when 
public health systems needed to reach out quickly and effectively, such symbols were 
not optional — they were essential. Social media became more than a message 
carrier; it became a space where trust was constructed through small but powerful 
visual gestures. 
4.5.2.1 Code: Benefit Horizon 
Benefit Horizon refers to the way people perceived and imagined the positive 
consequences of engaging with public health messages on social media platforms. 
Participants reported that when users were exposed to messages promising accurate, 
real-world solutions and meaningful action, they were more likely to engage and take 
concrete, health-related steps. These messages shifted the focus from fear or risk to 
a vision of a better future. 
“I followed a campaign online and booked my screening — social media opened that 
door” (PUBL10). This response indicated that online content could lead directly to 
action. Another participant said, “When we engage, it’s not just noise — it leads to 
resources and reassurance” (PUBL01), illustrating how digital engagement offered 
value — not just information. PROF3 added, “There is hope in these posts — they are 
imagining the results prevention can produce” (PROF3), underlining the potential of 
social media to frame achievable health outcomes. Together, these voices articulate 
Benefit Horizon as a vision for reframing health communication around opportunity 
and possibility. 
Significant to the interpretation of Benefit Horizon in this study was that people were 
most likely to engage with online health content they perceived as moving them toward 
something useful or hopeful. Unlike messages focused solely on danger or statistics, 
those that offered tangible benefits and emotional meaning were more likely to inspire 
action. 
141 
 
PUBL10 captured this clearly: “I followed a campaign online and ultimately booked my 
screening — social media made it happen” (PUBL10). This demonstrated how an 
effective campaign could lay out a path that felt open and achievable. This echoed 
PUBL01’s comment: “If we engage, it’s not just noise — it leads to resources and 
reassurance” (PUBL01). In both cases, engagement created direction and purpose. 
As PUBL14 reflected, “And sometimes people think they’re just scrolling, but one 
message like plants a seed for better decisions” (PUBL14). This metaphor aligns 
perfectly with the concept of a horizon — something ahead, gradually unfolding. It 
suggests that change may begin with something small, simple, and emotionally 
resonant. The emotional connection here is central. PUBL17 wrote, “It wasn’t the data  
it was that the things I did mattered, that I could create my own narrative in a way” 
(PUBL17). This illustrates that people are motivated not just by information, but by a 
sense of meaning. When messages help individuals feel that their actions matter, they 
become more committed. This is reinforced by PROF7: “Our campaigns aren’t about 
fear — they’re about envisioning a better public health future” (PROF7). This bridges 
the emotional with the strategic. When messages highlight what can go right — 
prevention, safety, community protection — they create hope rather than anxiety. 
PUBL03 captured this vividly: “I seen one post about early testing saves a life  I shared 
it right away!!” (PUBL03). This shows how a single hopeful message can trigger further 
engagement and create a wider social ripple. Another important concept in this motif 
is empowerment. PUBL09 stated, “I kept seeing that same message — ‘Protect your 
community.’ It felt empowering” (PUBL09). This demonstrates that repeated, 
purposeful messages can instil confidence and responsibility. When individuals feel 
part of something larger, they shift from passive observers to active participants. 
These different voices — public and professional — all point to one main conclusion: 
when health communication emphasises potential benefits and enables people to 
envision success, it becomes more effective. It moves from warning to possibility. 
Unlike fear-based messages or heavy statistics, messages aligned with the Benefit 
Horizon are uplifting and motivating. They bring people hope, empowerment, and 
purpose. They encourage engagement and behavioural action. People pause, think, 
and act. Benefit Horizon demonstrates the capability of social media to show people 
that their involvement counts and their engagement is meaningful. 
142 
 
4.5.3 
Code: Cognitive Dissonance 
“Cognitive Dissonance” describes the emotional and psychological distress people felt 
when they received conflicting messages about public health, particularly during a 
crisis. It demonstrated how mixed signals from different sources, such as social media, 
group chats, official health agencies, or news outlets, generated uncertainty and 
diminished trust in the information people had. Individuals began to feel disoriented, 
angry, and helpless rather than informed and reassured. PUBL13 articulated their 
frustration crisply: “One post said masks were crucial, another said they are not. Who 
do I believe” (PUBL13). This kind of inconsistency resulted in doubt and hesitancy, or 
even withdrawal from engagement in health matters. A more emotional take came 
from another participant, PUBL16: “I wanted to trust the updates, but they would 
change the rules. It felt confusing” (PUBL16). Together, these quotes demonstrate 
that even when people tried to believe and follow health advice, they felt trapped 
between contradictory messages. Where transparency was missing, the 
communication failed. 
4.6 THEME 3: COGNITIVE DISSONANCE 
The theme of “Cognitive Dissonance” in this study revealed the persistent struggle 
people faced when health communication was inconsistent or unstable. Rather than 
establishing a clear course of action, conflicting messages nudged those trying to stay 
informed into a state of continuous doubt. PUBL13’s comment, “One post said 
masks were a must, another said no to them. Who do I believe” (PUBL13), exemplified 
this struggle. People were attempting to make the right decisions for their health, and 
when advice shifted or clashed from one source to another, they began to lose faith in 
all of it. This erosion of stability was not purely about facts. Instead, it created a 
significant emotional burden. PUBL16 echoed this: “I wanted to trust the updates, but 
they were always changing the rules. It felt confusing” (PUBL16). These changes 
generated frustration, especially for those attempting to protect themselves and their 
families. It was not only about the content of the message but also about trust, and 
how inconsistency made that trust increasingly difficult to sustain. 
This emotional pressure was aptly described by PROF9, who observed, “Mixed 
messaging leads to internal chaos. It just makes you rethink everything” (PROF9). The 
143 
 
term “internal chaos” implies that it was not simply the external environment that 
became disorderly, but that confusion and disorientation were also felt within. When 
people received contradictory messages about what was or was not healthy, from 
experts, peers, or influencers, they began to mistrust both the individual message and 
the system delivering it. Such instability disrupted the sense of order the public 
typically expected from health authorities. That form of chaos became particularly 
dangerous during a health crisis, when people sought clarity and guidance, especially 
in an oversaturated information environment. 
PUBL06 remarked, “So much social media inundates you with opinions. That sound 
dilutes the substance of facts” (PUBL06). This suggests that the issue was not only 
conflicting messages but also the sheer volume of content with which people were 
bombarded each day. Within that flood of scientific updates, personal opinions, and 
misinformation, it became increasingly difficult to distinguish fact from noise. People 
reported feeling overwhelmed, which made facts harder to see, trust, and act upon. 
As a result, platforms intended to inform risked becoming sources of stress, further 
eroding public trust. 
The emotional and rational aspects of this confusion often came into direct conflict. 
PUBL20 stated, “I was emotionally with vaccines, but rationally I was in doubt after 
reading too many conflicting comments” (PUBL20). This highlighted how people could 
feel committed emotionally, yet intellectually unsettled by the digital conversation 
surrounding it. This contest between emotion and logic created psychological 
discomfort, particularly when individuals began questioning whether they had made 
the right choice. Such dissonance could delay action or halt it entirely. Even when 
people believed in the importance of getting vaccinated, the surrounding confusion 
stopped them from following through. 
Another issue was the pace of updates. PROF8 noted, “The speed of the updates 
collided with the depth of understanding, too much, too fast” (PROF8). Although rapid 
updates are typically seen as beneficial, in this case they backfired. When health 
guidance changed faster than the public could process it, people were left behind. 
They had insufficient time to absorb one version of the message before a new one 
emerged. In this context, the constant churn contributed to mistrust, particularly when 
individuals no longer knew which version of the advice they were meant to follow. 
144 
 
Here, velocity itself became counterproductive, undermining the clarity and 
effectiveness of public health communication. 
Personal communication channels such as group chats and private messages added 
further layers of confusion. As PUBL19 reflected, “It’s difficult to digest official advice 
when your group chats are telling you the opposite” (PUBL19). This demonstrated how 
trusted social spaces, such as family threads, friendship groups, or neighbourhood 
networks, could counteract public health messaging. Faced with contradictions 
between their private circles and official guidance, individuals were forced to decide 
whom to trust. Often, emotional attachment to one's social group caused people to 
favour unofficial advice, even when it lacked credibility. This personal influence, 
layered on top of wider confusion, deepened uncertainty. 
PROF1 added another perspective: “Clashing sources undermine our intuition related 
to health. Even when people have the best intentions, it blows trust” (PROF1). The 
implication was clear: even if authorities acted in good faith, trust still eroded when 
messaging collided. When a second wave emerged or guidance shifted again, people 
no longer trusted themselves to discern what was right. Their “health intuition” was 
dulled. They began to doubt not only the system but their own judgment. Thus, both 
the sender and receiver suffered in this collapse of confidence. Well intentioned 
campaigns launched into a noisy, disorienting environment often lost impact. 
Taken together, the quotes painted a clear picture. People were willing to believe 
public health messages but only if they were delivered with clarity and consistency. 
When the messages became mixed, trust began to erode, and emotional fatigue, 
digital noise, and the relentless pace of updates combined to overwhelm audiences. 
Some grew weary of trying to understand. Some stopped reading, others stopped 
listening. Crucially, some stopped believing. 
In the Cognitive Dissonance motif, the study illustrated how contradictory or muddled 
communication could create emotional distress and undermine trust. Even those who 
were keen to do the right thing found themselves paralysed by contradictions. This 
confusion went beyond simple misunderstanding, as it actively blocked action. The 
analysis suggests that clear and consistent communication is not optional but 
145 
 
essential, especially in fast moving digital environments where trust can be built 
quickly but lost even faster.  
4.6.1 
Code: Normative adhesion  
“Normative Adhesion” describes the way individuals adopt public health behaviours 
not through explicit instruction but by observing others within their community, 
particularly through social media interactions. It reflects an invisible form of pressure 
shaped by emotional contagion and group persuasion. When people witness their 
friends, family, or community members complying with health guidelines, they often 
feel compelled to follow suit, even in the absence of direct messaging. This means 
that behaviour spreads not by rules, but through repeated social cues. 
This dynamic is especially pronounced in online environments, where public acts like 
sharing health campaigns, wearing masks, or posting vaccination updates are easily 
observed and amplified. As PUBL10 noted, “Everyone in my circle began to post about 
vaccinations, eventually I felt I had to as well” (PUBL10). This illustrates how personal 
decisions are frequently shaped by visible peer behaviour. PUBL06 offered a similar 
view: “When your whole community is wearing masks and talking about safety, you 
naturally go with the flow” (PUBL06). Taken together, these views show that 
individual choices often follow the dominant mood of one’s digital environment. 
The implication is that health behaviours are anchored in collective momentum. 
PROF2 summarised the phenomenon succinctly: “There is unspoken pressure to 
support public health, on social media, it’s visual” (PROF2). The tension lies in its 
visibility and subtlety. This pressure is ambient yet overt, constant yet unspoken. 
Together, these statements characterise Normative Adhesion as a process of 
behavioural mirroring, enabled and reinforced by digital presence. 
Under the title Normative Adhesion, this study identifies how individuals are drawn into 
public health practices through the behaviour of their social contacts, particularly in 
online spaces where norms are performed, exchanged, and adopted. Several 
interviewees described how adherence often followed a pattern of social observation 
rather than deliberate research or official instruction. As PUBL10 reflected, “Suddenly 
everyone in my circle was posting about their vaccinations, after a while, I just felt like 
146 
 
I had to also” (PUBL10). This links directly to the idea that seeing others take 
action creates an implicit expectation that one should do the same. 
This subtle pressure, not enforced but constantly visible, created a sense of necessity. 
Not participating risked social exclusion. As PUBL06 put it, “When your entire 
community is wearing masks and promoting safety, you naturally go with the flow” 
(PUBL06). The emphasis on “naturally” suggests authenticity. It was not about 
coercion but about belonging. It was not compliance imposed from above; it was 
shared meaning created horizontally across peers. 
This pressure becomes even more visible in digital environments. As PROF2 
observed earlier, “There’s this unspoken kind of pressure to support public health, on 
social media, it becomes seen” (PROF2). Private behaviours, such as deciding to wear 
a mask or get vaccinated, are no longer entirely private. Once posted or shared, they 
become open to social observation. Visibility converts personal decisions into 
public actions, turning implicit expectations into digital norms. Individuals start 
to feel accountable not only to institutions but also to their peers. 
While this emotional pressure can motivate positive health behaviours, it can also 
foster a sense of performance, in which people act not solely out of personal conviction 
but to demonstrate responsibility to others. The influence is amplified within close-knit 
networks. PUBL17 stated, “On top of that, my family WhatsApp group had been 
constantly going on about it, constantly. I wouldn’t have even thought about doing 
some of the measures if my family WhatsApp group hadn’t” (PUBL17). This shows 
how family communication intensifies normative pressure in quieter but more 
intimate ways. Advice from trusted circles carries more weight than official messages. 
Several participants noted how online behaviours set offline precedents. As PUBL03 
noted, “Even the reserved folks in my network have shared those Ministry videos. It 
turned into a communal standard” (PUBL03). Once a behaviour gained sufficient 
traction, it ceased to feel optional. Even those who usually stayed silent were 
compelled to participate. Norms were solidified not through explicit debate but through 
repeated, unspoken agreement. 
147 
 
PUBL19 similarly observed, “You don’t always ask the question, you especially do it if 
your whole digital community is doing it” (PUBL19). This “mute agreement” illustrates 
how the perceived consensus of one’s digital peer group can ease behavioural uptake. 
People comply not because they understand the underlying science or policy rationale, 
but because the behaviour feels correct within their social context. This makes social 
influence more powerful than any single campaign message. 
Social media thus becomes a space where norms are both shaped and displayed. As 
PROF5 stated, “Health norms are living online now. It’s not in private any more, it’s 
performative, in a good sense” (PROF5). Here, performance is interpreted positively. 
Sharing safety messages, posting about vaccinations, or engaging with government 
content serves as a digital signal of care and social alignment. These acts multiply; 
people see them, replicate them, and reinforce them. Behaviour spreads through 
observation, imitation, and affirmation, creating a cycle of visibility and validation. 
This is also evident in PUBL08’s remark that online behaviour shapes offline decisions: 
“The group behaviour online sets the tone for what’s acceptable offline” (PUBL08). 
This indicates that the influence of digital platforms extends beyond the screen. What 
people observe online strongly informs their real-world conduct. When individuals see 
others adhering to public health behaviours, they gain reassurance and normalisation. 
Conversely, if safety behaviours are absent in their digital circles, they may begin 
questioning the necessity of such actions. 
These participant voices collectively illustrate how social norms are constructed, 
circulated, and internalised in digital environments. People rarely act in isolation; their 
behaviours are shaped by what they witness around them. In the digital age, that 
means what they see online. From family WhatsApp threads to Instagram stories, 
individuals are regularly exposed to cues about what is appropriate, expected, or 
socially endorsed. These cues guide both thought and action. 
Even those uncertain about the guidance are more likely to comply when they see 
their digital communities doing so. This aligns strongly with social influence 
theory, which demonstrates that people conform because they want to belong. 
Compliance becomes a sign of inclusion. People follow public health behaviours not 
simply for themselves but as a visible expression of collective responsibility. 
148 
 
This form of social contagion allows health-promoting behaviours to spread quickly 
and widely. Digital platforms become spaces where such behaviours are modelled, 
reinforced, and legitimised. If health systems can harness this dynamic, they may 
amplify engagement and strengthen public trust. By fostering constructive digital 
norms, they can guide communities toward healthier collective choices. 
4.6.2 
Code: Collective Identity 
‘Collective Identity’ refers to the degree to which people emotionally and socially 
identify with public health messaging and see it as part of their identity and shared 
national or community ideals. It signals that public health is no longer something 
remote or driven by government fiat, but something actively owned by those who 
embrace it with pride, something they can work for and stand publicly behind. Via 
social media, health messages are transformed not into instructions so much as signs 
of unity, belonging, and care for the community. PUBL15 articulated this sentiment 
when they said: “I see a post about the Ministry, and I don’t see it as these aloof 
people. They are one of us” (PUBL15). This crystallises how government health 
communication is no longer seen as coming from a distant, removed place of state 
control. PUBL11 added, “The health department’s logo feels like a symbol of 
reassurance now” (PUBL11), indicating how what was once perceived as official is 
now regarded with personal significance. PROF4 extended this, saying, “Our brand is 
not healthcare alone, it's really our national resilience” (PROF4), emphasising that the 
public health identity is now associated with national strength and pride. These 
reflections describe Collective Identity as the emotional and symbolic relationship that 
people have with the public health system, communicated through language, imagery, 
and values circulated online. 
In this study, Collective Identity illustrates how social media has changed the public’s 
relationship to the public health system of Abu Dhabi. What was once regarded as a 
formal authority now feels like a collective mission. Participants explained that they no 
longer see the Ministry or health department as another organisation outside of them, 
but rather as a part of their own community and values. PUBL15 wrote, “I don’t feel 
that the guy who records in the Ministry is another world, he’s one of us” (PUBL15). 
This quotation is indicative of an emotional connection established by communicating 
in people’s language, valuing their culture, and respecting their fears. Instead of feeling 
149 
 
like passive recipients of public health messaging, people now feel a sentiment of 
partnership. This transformation is also visual. “It feels now like the health 
department’s logo is a badge of reassurance” (PUBL11). A symbol that once stood for 
authority now offers relief and recognition. It becomes a sign not of power but of care. 
This shift in emotion proves that branding is about more than design; it has meaning. 
It signals that someone is looking out for you. When people feel this kind of sentiment, 
it fosters trust and engagement. 
It also allows people to share and shape the message in ways that feel personal, just 
as PUBL02 states: “I love sharing their posts because they are really the affirmation 
of our society” (PUBL02). Sharing in this sense is about more than passing along 
information. It is a way of engaging and expressing ownership. You feel like you are 
doing something for the greater good. 
Professionals in the research observed this change as well. “There’s a kind of unifying 
in the way the public health messages are framed, it binds people” (PROF1). The word 
“binds” is crucial because it implies a strong emotional connection built on steady and 
respectful communication. The sense of unity comes from messages that 
communicate shared values such as protection, care, and community. PUBL13 stated 
this plainly: “We aligned with mission, protection health, extra care health, unity. That’s 
our identity too” (PUBL13). When public health goals align with public values, people 
do not feel like they are obeying rules; they feel like they are living their beliefs. This 
association reinforces the message and encourages people to take action. 
This emotional connection is further enriched by the fact that messages via social 
media can feel intimate. As PUBL18 put it: “Social media made public health personal, 
that matters” (PUBL18). This quote demonstrates how the style and tone of digital 
communication converted distant instructions into meaningful conversation. The more 
people identify with the messages, the more they see themselves reflected in the 
health narrative, and the greater the engagement. The more they see themselves in 
the message, the more they believe it, trust it, and share it. This feeling of personal 
connection makes public health feel real and relevant, not imposed or abstract. 
PROF8 gave further insight into that shift, noting, “Branding has moved above 
corporate, it’s become cultural. We relate to it now” (PROF8). This suggests that the 
150 
 
system’s identity has travelled beyond its official image and now mirrors the 
community’s own culture, language, and emotional landscape. The messages are 
more than just information; they represent shared hopes, struggles, and beliefs. This 
cultural alignment means the public health brand is something people feel proud to 
support. It is not viewed as foreign or formal. It is part of who they are. 
When we compare these quotes, a common theme emerges people want to connect 
with public health not because they are being told to, but because they feel emotionally 
and socially connected to each other. From PUBL02 taking pride in sharing posts, to 
PUBL13 seeing their values reflected in the mission, to PROF4 calling it “our national 
resilience,” the human connection to the public health system has never felt more 
personal and more collective at the same time. The system is no longer external to the 
people; it is among them, even within them. 
The experience of community encourages greater involvement. People are not only 
listening; they are participating and standing with the health system. There are no 
longer outsiders to the message; they are co-owners of it. Such co ownership renders 
public health initiatives more effective, supported by a shared sense of responsibility 
and pride. The message becomes a mirror reflecting the society’s values. Collective 
Identity offers a snapshot of how public health communication on social media in Abu 
Dhabi has harnessed a strong emotional and cultural bond between the people and 
the system. Through visuals, language, and shared values, people have come to view 
the health system as something to which they belong, not merely something to which 
they adhere. This feeling of identification motivates people to participate, share, and 
champion messages for health with pride. It transforms health behaviour from an 
assignment into a common cause, and institutions from remote authorities into reliable 
teammates. In this way, Collective Identity can act as a social foundation for broad 
based community health engagement.  
4.6.3 
Code: Symbolic Alignment 
“Symbolic Alignment” describes the degree to which individuals resonate with public 
health messages that mirror their own personal, cultural, and national values. It is 
about emotional identification, where people do not only understand the message 
intellectually but feel that it speaks for them. When the tone, narratives, imagery, and 
151 
 
language of health campaigns align with people’s beliefs and aspirations, a message 
becomes more than a message; it becomes part of one’s sense of self and shared 
belonging. 
“I repost messages not only to share information but because I support what they 
stand for” (PUBL04). This illustrates that sharing health messages is about more than 
transmitting information; it is an expression of belief and identity. Similarly, PUBL12 
stated, “Those health campaigns are often about values that we personally believe in, 
in family, safety, community” (PUBL12). The message is more deeply felt when it 
represents something people are emotionally connected to. PROF9 added, “If the 
messaging is consistent with our values, it is reflected back on us” (PROF9). Taken 
together, these voices show that symbolic alignment occurs when messages 
feel like an extension of one’s own values, rather than external mandates. 
When public health communication succeeds, it often does so by resonating on an 
emotional and cultural level with the community. Messages are not only accepted as 
factually true but experienced as emotionally right, consistent with people’s beliefs, 
pride, and their understanding of what it means to care for others. PUBL04 reiterated: 
“I reshare messages not only to communicate but to show that I support what it 
communicates” (PUBL04). This demonstrates how resharing becomes a symbolic 
act, signalling affiliation with what the message represents. It does not feel like 
an obligation; it becomes a way to express one’s commitment to values such as 
protection, unity, and care. 
That emotional connection strengthens when campaigns speak directly to what 
matters most in people’s lives. As PUBL12 noted, “Those health campaigns are often 
a mirror of values we strongly believe in, family, safety, healthy community” (PUBL12). 
In this sense, alignment transforms messaging from instruction into affirmation, 
reminding people of the ideals they already hold. This is also reflected in PROF9’s 
observation: “When the messaging mirrors our values, it becomes a reflection of 
ourselves” (PROF9). When people see their own beliefs and aspirations reflected 
back, they are more likely to trust, share, and act on the message. The message 
becomes part of their identity, not an external directive. 
152 
 
Tone and storytelling further strengthen this alignment. PUBL07 shared, “I can identify 
with the tone and stories that they use. They are speaking our emotional language” 
(PUBL07). This suggests that people respond as much to how a message feels as to 
what it says. A warm, empathetic tone can generate emotional closeness and 
encourage people to see themselves inside the narrative. When stories resemble 
lived experience, the message becomes relatable and fosters empathy, making 
it more persuasive. 
Symbolic words and images deepen this connection. “There’s symbolic power in 
phrases like ‘Together for Health’” (PROF5). “They unite us” (PROF5). The power of 
such language lies in its ability to mobilise emotion and convey collective strength. As 
PUBL14 reflected, “I follow their page not because I feel obliged to do so, but because 
I identify with their message” (PUBL14). This indicates that genuine engagement 
develops when a message resonates with a person’s emotional and cultural 
reality. 
Health campaign aesthetics also matter. PUBL01 said, “The imagery, the language, 
it’s all consistent with my notion of what public service should be” (PUBL01). Visuals 
that include national colours, familiar symbols, or culturally relevant landmarks make 
people feel acknowledged. This recognition strengthens trust and motivates 
engagement, because the message appears tailored rather than generic. 
PROF2 expanded on this: “Their campaigns are successful to the extent that they 
combine national pride and public duty” (PROF2). This convergence makes public 
health messaging feel not only informative but meaningful, allowing people to express 
patriotism, solidarity, and moral care through engagement. 
When taking all these voices together, a clear pattern emerges: symbolic alignment is 
the emotional thread in public health communication. It transforms words and images 
into meaningful reflections of identity. It allows people to feel not only informed but 
seen, respected, and included. That sense of inclusion drives loyalty, engagement, 
and trust. Health communication becomes most effective not simply when it 
encourages behaviour, but when it affirms belonging. 
153 
 
Importantly, symbolic alignment also empowers collective behaviour. When people 
believe that a message represents them, they are more likely to act together. Acts 
such as vaccination, mask-wearing, or supporting community initiatives become 
expressions of shared purpose. Symbolic alignment shows that the most impactful 
public health messages are those that reflect emotional, cultural, and national values. 
These messages succeed not only because they are well crafted, but because they 
speak directly to the heart of the community. People do not merely listen; they see 
themselves in the message. When this recognition occurs, they are not only willing to 
act, but proud to do so. Aligned symbolically, public health becomes more than a 
directive; it becomes a shared mission shaped by identity, values, and collective 
care.  
4.7 THEME 4: MOBILIZED COGNITION  
 
Social influence mechanisms can induce both the intention and the behavioural 
readiness to act via social media. The Mobilized Cognition perspective connects the 
way social media shapes people's cognitive readiness and willingness to engage in 
health-related action. 
 
The foundation of all the codes is the recognition that behaviour is not transformed by 
information alone. Behaviours are shaped by triggers, emotional engagement, social 
support, and the rehearsal of future behaviour. Social media enables this 
transformation by making health behaviours visible, emotionally resonant, and easy to 
follow. Action catalysts thus provoke people in the real world to turn desires into action 
and thoughts into words. These triggers, such as reminders in tweets or posts by 
influential users, support health initiatives by making them easily understood and 
compelling to act upon immediately. However, such interventions are effective only 
when combined with emotional depth, as illustrated by Influence Valence. Narratives 
of hope, empathy and shared humanity are more powerful than statistics. Storytelling 
builds 
belief, 
restores 
trust, 
and 
inspires 
action 
among 
viewers. 
In addition to emotion and urgency, Compliance Drift shows how social cues and 
conformity shape behaviour. People are more likely to adopt visible health actions, 
154 
 
such as vaccination or mask-wearing, when they observe such behaviours within 
digital networks. This tendency to conform is driven by a sense of emotional safety 
and belonging, and when people see others behaving in certain ways, that behaviour 
becomes more normal, accepted, and expected. This creates a soft nudge that moves 
people from hesitation to activation by simply reflecting the expected norm and what 
is considered good in their community. 
 
Completing this behavioural loop is another reinforcing factor. Indeed, the Self-Efficacy 
Loop shows that once people are emotionally stirred and socially guided, they still 
seek clarity before taking the final step. Practical cues, how-to guides, stories of 
learning experiences, and even humorous mishaps can help move audiences from 
confusion towards confidence. Social media content that tells people why to act and 
how strengthens the sense of efficacy they need to follow through. The more people 
engage, succeed, and see results, the more they place their trust in the system, 
reinforcing 
the 
loop 
and 
encouraging 
continued 
participation. 
Taken together, these codes suggest that mobilizing cognition is not a single mental 
event but a complex orchestration of thoughts and emotions that emerges over time 
in response to social cues. Mobilized Cognition involves feeling that health behaviour 
is urgent, supported, visible, and achievable. Such a process is about changing minds, 
preparing the heart, guiding the hands, and instilling belief that one’s actions matter. 
Within this framework, social media is both a channel for information and a space for 
action. 
4.7.1 
Code: Action Catalysts 
 
“Action Catalysts” are digital calls to action that prompt people to take immediate steps 
related to health behaviours, such as getting tested, vaccinated, or seeking further 
information. These cues are not simply informational, they are motivational, 
leveraging desirability, urgency, or convenience. They appear in many forms, including 
tweets, WhatsApp broadcasts, influencer content, countdowns, and visual alerts. 
What makes them surprisingly effective is their ability to turn mindless scrolling into 
active participation. As PUBL08 explained, “Once I saw that reminder tweet from the 
health authority, I immediately booked my check-up” (PUBL08). This quote captures 
155 
 
the immediacy of a well-timed digital message. PUBL12 echoed this sentiment, noting 
that “The WhatsApp broadcast from the Ministry was not just information, it was a 
trigger to act” (PUBL12). These examples show that digital messages go beyond 
raising awareness, since they activate decisions. As PROF7 observed, “Seeing 
countdowns and alerts on Instagram made me feel like I need to catch up” (PROF7).  
Collectively, these reflections describe Action Catalysts as brief, emotionally potent, 
strategically 
crafted 
digital 
cues 
that 
bridge 
awareness 
and 
action. 
In this study, Action Catalysts illustrate how social media communication can go 
beyond simply sharing information to actively motivating people to take specific health-
related steps. Survey responses reveal that digital messages can act as switches, 
moving someone from passive understanding to active engagement. PUBL08 
illustrated this clearly: “When I saw that reminder tweet from the health authority, I 
made an appointment” (PUBL08). This kind of instant response is a reminder that 
digital messages are most effective when they arrive at the right time and offer a clear 
next step. It was not just the content of the tweet that mattered, but its timing and 
clarity, which made it a true catalyst. This sense of immediate inspiration is also 
highlighted in PUBL12’s comment: “The bulletin by WhatsApp from the Ministry was 
not only information, it was a catalyst for action” (PUBL12). Here, the word “trigger” is 
key. The message carried emotional or psychological weight. It was not just processed 
intellectually, it was felt and acted upon. 
 
PUBL16 described a behavioural shift that was driven not by data, but by emotional 
resonance: “I wasn’t going to take the booster until the story they posted of a recovered 
patient convinced me” (PUBL16). This shows that narrative, when grounded in 
empathy and connection, has a powerful effect. An emotional link to someone’s 
recovery can transform hesitancy into action. 
 
Timing and visual urgency are also key components of action catalysts. “Did people 
really think viewing countdowns and getting alerts on Instagram would make me 
anxious and stressed?” asked PROF7 (PROF7). The visual style including 
countdowns, timers, and flashing posts can create a sense of urgency, making people 
feel they need to act now. This helps reduce processing time and indecision. PUBL14 
156 
 
affirmed the effectiveness of visual urgency, stating, “The flashing graphics and live 
announcements really work, they push you to decide now as opposed to later” 
(PUBL14). These comments show that urgency does not have to arise from fear, it 
can 
be 
evoked 
through 
visual 
design 
and 
strategic 
timing. 
That urgency and accessibility make these cues actionable. Influencers and 
community leaders also play a critical role in driving action. As PUBL11 noted, “Once 
influencers started posting their vaccine cards, it became a social cue for many” 
(PUBL11). This underscores the power of social proof. Seeing others take action 
makes it feel safe, normal, and encouraged.  
In this way, personal influence reinforces institutional messaging, creating a 
powerful 
convergence 
between 
peer 
behaviour 
and 
official 
guidance. 
There is also a sense of effortlessness and instinctive response in PUBL19’s 
comment: “I remember clicking on the testing location post without even thinking about 
it. It was so intuitive” (PUBL19). This suggests that the most effective action catalysts 
are those that feel simple and obvious. When a message is easy to understand, the 
design is clear, and the next step is evident, people often respond instinctively. It does 
not feel like a task, it feels like a natural click. That frictionless experience leads to 
participation. 
 
This immediate reaction is also shaped by how social media generates urgency 
through exposure and silence. “Social media makes you feel like you are going to miss 
out if you are not echoing this health call,” PROF6 observed (PROF6). The fear of 
missing out, or FOMO, a term more often seen in commercial contexts, is increasingly 
relevant in public health communication. The sense that everyone else is participating 
and that opportunities may be limited motivates people to act. This emotional 
momentum, rather than fear alone, supports quick decision-making and coordinated 
responses, especially during health emergencies. 
 
Taken together, these accounts show that well designed external prompts can 
significantly influence public health behaviour. Whether it is a quick tweet or a 
compelling graphic or a full influencer campaign, such messages resonate because 
they counter hesitation, spark motivation, and offer a clear path to the next step. They 
157 
 
carry emotional pull, social currency, visual urgency, and accessibility. Whether it is a 
countdown, a personal story, or a one-click link to a health service, the most effective 
action catalysts do not wait for the user to make a decision, they gently guide it. 
Action Catalysts further highlight the importance of urgency, clarity, and emotionally 
intelligent design in digital health communication. People are more likely to act when 
messages reach them quickly, speak to them directly, and carry subjectively 
meaningful impact. Digital posts, when combined with a prompt through stories, 
visuals, or social proof, can transform ordinary content into moments of action. Such 
catalysts are essential if engagement is to be converted into something of value. In 
public health systems, particularly during fast-moving situations like a pandemic, such 
rapid, intuitive, emotionally resonant communications can be the difference between 
delay and decision, between merely seeing a message and actually responding to it. 
4.7.2 
Code: Influence Valence 
 
‘Influence Valence’ refers to the emotional charge in public health communication 
within digital spaces that makes people feel something strong enough to act. It 
captures how the emotions expressed in digital messages, whether hope, sadness, or 
even neutral facts, are converted into broader emotional responses, influencing 
how the message is received, how far it is shared, and how likely it is to prompt action. 
When a post conveys genuine feeling or a video displays raw emotion, people do not 
just read or watch it, they connect with it, remember it, and in some cases act on it. 
PUBL01 illustrated this powerfully: “I cried watching that video of a doctor hugging a 
recovered patient. It made me believe again” (PUBL01). Human experience like this 
carries more weight than any chart or statistic. PUBL21 echoed this, adding, “The 
health videos that showed real stories, particularly losses, captured emotional choices” 
(PUBL21). These are not just touching moments, they become emotional prompts 
that rebuild confidence, renew motivation, and foster personal investment in public 
health. As PROF1 put it, “When a post is emotional, it is spreading faster, and it is 
sticking deeper” (PROF1). These reflections show that the emotional tone of a 
message, its valence, is just as important as the content. It is often the difference 
between a message that resonates and one that is quickly forgotten. 
The Influence Valence construct in this study shows that emotional connection plays 
a central role in driving public health engagement. Participants consistently reported 
158 
 
that emotional messages, not statistical or fact-heavy ones, influenced their trust and 
behaviour. PUBL10 drew a clear distinction: “I didn’t see what the percentages 
showed, but one story about a mother scared me into getting the vaccine” (PUBL10). 
This implies that data alone is not persuasive. What drives action is emotion, 
especially when people can see the lived reality behind a public health issue.  
A mother’s story is not just relatable, it generates empathy and creates a sense of 
personal 
connection 
to 
the 
wider 
health 
challenge. 
This emotional connection can help restore trust and belief in the health system. 
PUBL01 said, “I wept watching that video of the doctor embracing a recovered patient. 
It made me believe again” (PUBL01). This quote reminds us how emotional exhaustion 
can be repaired. The hug itself is simple, yet it symbolises solidarity, recovery, and 
hope. In a cynical or disconnected era, a moment of emotional truth can shift both 
hearts and minds. It reintroduces humanity into public health messaging and gives it 
warmth. 
 
PROF9 explored this emotional impact in more detail: “[There’s a mix of empathy and 
urgency] in the Ministry’s posts, [the Ministry doesn’t] just inform [us], it makes [us] 
feel” (PROF9). This blend is crucial. Posts that merely instruct may not cut through, 
but when empathy is added, the message becomes easier to understand and more 
persuasive. It is not just about telling people what to do, it is about showing them why 
the message matters emotionally. PROF1 reinforced this by saying, “When a post 
is emotionally charged, it spreads faster and sticks deeper” (PROF1). Emotion drives 
engagement. When content truly moves people, they are more likely to share it, 
comment on it, and act on it. 
 
This emotional immersion also supports message recall. PUBL21 noted, “The real 
health videos, stories mainly of losses, enhanced emotional persuasion” (PUBL21). 
The key idea here is that these narratives instil lasting conviction, not just fleeting 
sympathy. People who may have been hesitant or afraid of something like vaccination 
begin to feel more confident simply because the emotional weight of a story helped 
them grasp the deeper meaning of their choices more effectively than data ever could. 
Tone matters as well. “The tone does matter,” wrote PUBL06. “There’s warmth and 
159 
 
care in the messaging and it elicits bigger responses” (PUBL06). This observation 
highlights that the way in which something is said can be as impactful as what is said. 
Cold, technical language may not be trusted, but when the message sounds caring 
and sincere, it feels more authentic. It humanises the content and shows that the 
person behind the message understands the emotional gravity of the situation. Tone 
creates 
emotional 
bridges 
that 
link 
the 
health 
system 
to 
the 
public. 
PROF5 captured this balance well: “Fear is effective but hope is more effective. The 
best posts balance both” (PROF5). This implies that emotional encouragement does 
not need to be overwhelming or manipulative. Fear can generate urgency, but hope 
fuels motivation. Posts that are too negative risk producing anxiety or resistance. 
However, when messages include positive emotions, such as stories of recovery, 
community strength, or resilience, they help people feel empowered and inspired. This 
dynamic between urgency and optimism is what makes emotional content not only 
effective, but ethically grounded. 
 
The secret to effective health communication is not neutrality but intentional 
emotional framing. Whether it is presented as a tearjerking video, a powerful story, 
or a passionate message of support, emotional content resonates most deeply with 
people and prompts the strongest responses. It creates a shared experience and 
builds a connection between the public and the health system. Emotional messages 
are also more likely to be shared on social media, amplifying their impact. 
“Influence Valence” implies that the emotional quality of a message matters. 
Messages that trigger empathy, hope, or even despair are not only informative, they 
mobilise and motivate, shifting public attitudes. Ultimately, they turn passive readers 
into active participants. These are emotional stories that give meaning to statistics and 
substance to public health guidance. 
 
By carefully calibrating tone, narrative, and visual content, public health campaigns 
can build trust, inspire action, and help people feel seen, heard, and supported. 
Emotion is therefore at the core of what makes messages meaningful and impactful. 
It transforms public health communication from simple information delivery into 
emotionally resonant engagement. 
160 
 
4.7.3 
Code: Compliance Drift 
“Compliance Drift” explains how people come to emulate health behaviours simply by 
seeing others do the same, especially in digital environments like social media. It is a 
subtle, often unconscious shift toward adherence, not prompted by direct pressure but 
because visible actions from peers and networks make the behaviour appear normal. 
People begin adopting health behaviours such as vaccination, mask-wearing or 
attending check-ups not only in response to official guidance but also because these 
actions are presented as standard practice within their social circles. PUBL02 said, 
“After everyone was posting shots of their vaccine, a selfie felt normal to post.” This 
sense of organic involvement is reinforced by the community dynamic of online 
compliance. As PROF3 noted, “When compliance becomes visible, it’s contagious,” 
illustrating how the visibility of prosocial behaviours encourages others to follow suit. 
The central idea in Compliance Drift is that social cues from others act as emotional 
and behavioural anchors, nudging individuals to align with what appears typical or 
expected. In this study, Compliance Drift demonstrates how social media platforms, 
by making behaviour visible, can strongly influence what people perceive as normal 
or acceptable in the context of public health. People’s decisions are shaped not only 
by personal judgement or scientific evidence but also by what others around them are 
doing. PUBL18 explained, “You wouldn't want to be the stick in the mud, if your clique 
behaves, you do too.” This insight highlights how the desire to belong can be a 
powerful motivator for health-related action. Rather than positioning themselves 
outside the group, people tend to follow the norm that emerges within it. 
This alignment is not necessarily formal. It stems from observing and responding to 
social cues. PUBL03 said, “Health behaviour went viral, literally. It was all over my 
feed.” The term “viral” reflects the speed and scale with which these behaviours spread 
once they are replicated and shared online. Vaccine selfies, hashtags and stories 
appear repeatedly and this repetition transforms compliance into something that feels 
not only correct but expected. The behaviour takes on a life of its own, not through 
instruction but through example. This is a form of soft power that makes health 
decisions feel socially endorsed. 
This influence can be powerful even without deep understanding. PUBL07 added, “I 
didn’t feel like looking into it, I trusted who others were going with.” This reflects how 
people often replace introspective analysis with social observation. When enough 
161 
 
people in one’s immediate circle project confidence and positivity, it fosters a sense of 
security in imitating them. It is not blind following but a form of listening to the crowd, 
which serves as a shortcut to truth and safety during times of uncertainty. 
The emotional safety tied to compliance is also central. PUBL20 stated, “It sure is safe 
to be following the herd during troubled times.” This illustrates how following others is 
not just about agreement. It is about seeking security. When the surrounding world 
feels unstable and people are unsure of what to believe, watching others act provides 
direction and steadiness. It replaces fear with confidence, not through persuasion but 
through modelling. Observing others sends a silent message that a behaviour is safe 
and acceptable. 
PROF2 observed, “It’s not peer pressure exactly, it is more like social permission to 
join the group.” This shows that Compliance Drift is not driven by guilt or force. It is 
about having a door opened. When people see others joining public health campaigns, 
they receive an emotional signal that it is acceptable to do the same. They feel invited 
into the behaviour rather than coerced. The action becomes socially approved and 
psychologically easy. This ease is exactly what makes Compliance Drift effective. The 
visibility of others practising health behaviours provides silent encouragement. 
PUBL15 explained, “The group dynamic on Instagram made me feel so confident that 
it was the right thing to do.” This captures how digital environments circulate not only 
information but also confidence. Every time someone shares a post about getting 
tested, wearing a mask or getting vaccinated, it contributes to the idea that responsible 
people take these actions. That emotional reassurance acts as a small nudge toward 
compliance. These voices form a strong picture of how social media does not merely 
reflect behaviour but shapes it. Once people see health behaviours being displayed 
and repeated, they are likely to adopt them too, often without conscious deliberation. 
This drift toward compliance is not driven by policy or fear but by the quiet logic of 
belonging. As more people take part, the momentum builds. What begins as individual 
choices gradually becomes group identity. 
Compliance Drift shows how social pressure operates subtly but powerfully within 
digital spaces. It makes health behaviours visible, repeatable and emotionally safe. 
Instead of instructing people directly, it shows them what others are doing and creates 
an invitation to join in. This is a mechanism for achieving widespread participation 
162 
 
without conflict, which is vital for effective public health communication. When 
compliance feels not only like the right thing but also like the normal thing, people are 
far more inclined to participate and to motivate others to do the same. 
4.7.4 Code: Self Efficacy Loop  
“Self Efficacy Loop” describes the pathway through which people build confidence, or 
self efficacy, to carry out health behaviours once they receive clear, supportive and 
actionable information, particularly through digital platforms. It shows that 
empowerment is not just about motivation, but critically about having enough clarity 
to know what to do. When guidance is delivered through simple steps, relatable 
stories and straightforward instructions, people’s belief in their own ability increases. 
As PUBL04 put it, “When the post tells me not just why, but how to do it, then I feel 
like I can” (PUBL04). This shows that explanation alone is not enough, because 
practical support is essential for confidence. PUBL05 echoed this point, recalling, 
“The visual step by step guides on social media gave me confidence to take action” 
(PUBL05). These examples demonstrate that belief is fostered when health systems
