move beyond simply telling people what is important, and instead show them how to 
act in response. PROF8 captured this well: “The clearer the instructions, the better, 
clear instructions help make a lower mental barrier” (PROF8). The theme is therefore 
no longer simply about confusion, but about clarity and inclusion. This reinforces the 
idea that anyone can take part in health decisions when they are given the right tools 
and messages. 
The Self Efficacy Loop identified in this study illustrates how social media plays a 
central role in empowering individuals to take health related action. Participants 
consistently reported that clear, supportive digital content helped them to feel more 
confident, more prepared and more willing to practice health behaviours. PUBL04 
expressed this plainly: “When the post shows me how to do, instead of just why, I feel 
like I can” (PUBL04). This highlights a key lesson, which is that motivation alone is not 
enough. People need direction, and when they receive it, they are empowered to act. 
Motivation combined with clarity creates readiness. 
This emotional strength of guidance is reiterated by PUBL05, who explained, “Step by 
step visual guides by social media gave me the confidence to do something” 
163 
 
(PUBL05). Here, the video is just as important as the message itself. An image or 
infographic that walks someone through complex actions makes those actions feel 
manageable. This removes fear, reluctance and doubt, and instead of asking “What if 
I mess it up” people start thinking “I know what to do.” These small enabling nudges 
help people move from thinking to doing. 
Professionals recognised the value of this too. Clear instructions make all the 
difference because they lower the mental barrier, as PROF8 pointed out (PROF8). 
That barrier may be invisible, but it is very real. It is the hesitation that comes from 
unclear steps. Even when people want to follow health recommendations, they may 
hold back if the steps feel too complex or unclear. But once uncertainty is removed 
through effective communication, action becomes easier and more natural. 
This cycle of support building confidence is echoed by PUBL13, who stated, “I’m more 
likely to act when I feel I know what I’m doing, and social media helped with that” 
(PUBL13). This shows that effective communication does not only shift attitudes, it 
also builds capability. Social media networks that provide timely, clear and repeated 
instructions help people move from doubt to confidence. It is not just about telling 
people what matters, but about showing them how to act without fear of failure. 
Real life stories also play a key role in this process. PUBL09 shared, “The pictures of 
people doing awesome health routines online inspired me to think that I could do so 
as well” (PUBL09). This illustrates how seeing others succeed can nurture belief in 
one’s own ability. It is a quiet form of encouragement, a form of social proof that fosters 
self belief. When someone sees an ordinary person, someone who looks like them, 
taking action, the behaviour becomes more real and more attainable. These stories 
transform abstract health messages into relatable and doable scenarios. 
PUBL06 offered a strong example of this transition: “I saw a post explaining home 
testing, and that put me over the edge in terms of feeling empowered” (PUBL06). It is 
a statement with both practical and emotional clarity. One well crafted post, if clear 
and informative, can close the gap between intention and action. It offers what people 
need most, reassuring direction that reduces doubt. It shows how digital content 
can turn hesitation into readiness simply by clearly and warmly showing the way 
forward. 
164 
 
Another professional perspective, from PROF4, reinforces this idea: “When they post 
FAQs and visual explainers, it takes out the guesswork” (PROF4). Guesswork is often 
what stands between intention and action. People hesitate when they are uncertain. 
But when that uncertainty is replaced with clear answers, diagrams or checklists, 
confidence grows and action becomes more feasible. This process creates a 
reinforcing loop. Clarity builds confidence, confidence leads to action and action 
strengthens trust in the system. This reinforcing cycle was captured by PUBL20, who 
said, “You just grow more confident when the system stands by your side with clarity 
and consistency” (PUBL20). This sentiment pulls the point together. One off 
information is not enough. The system must offer guidance that is consistent, reliable 
and emotionally reassuring. 
Taken together, these voices suggest that the Self Efficacy Loop is about emotional 
and psychological readiness. The more people feel supported, informed and prepared, 
the more likely they are to access health systems, follow guidance and support others 
in doing the same. When a social media post is designed to inform and teach, it 
becomes a tool of empowerment rather than a simple announcement. The 
outcome is not only increased engagement, but deeper community confidence. To be 
effective, health communication should be clear, relevant and consistent. The Self 
Efficacy Loop shows that people are more likely to act when they are given information 
that is clear and meaningful to them. Fundamentally, people act when they feel ready 
to act, and that preparedness develops step by step through visual guides, simple 
instructions, familiar stories and shared social cues. When media empowers people, 
they act with confidence, independence, and a sense of contribution to a healthier 
society. 
4.8 THEME 5: DIGITAL PRESERVATION  
Responses at both system and individual levels to themes and concerns on social 
media reveal important dynamics. This theme focuses on the reflexivity of public health 
agencies as they engage with social media dialogue. It contributes to Objectives 1 and 
4 and supports Research Questions 1 and 4 by showing how institutions evolve by 
responding to community priorities. 
165 
 
Echo Governance illustrates that institutions are willing to listen and adapt. From 
introducing new languages to hosting live sessions, participants observed that 
public feedback directly shaped system actions, reflecting forms of dynamic 
governance informed by ongoing feedback loops, social proof, and iterative learning. 
Sentiment Flux captures the fluctuations of public emotion. Institutions monitored 
citizen attitudes and adjusted both tone and communication style to maintain trust, 
aligning with the accumulation of normative feedback and emotional tuning as 
mechanisms for engagement and trust-building. The study shows that public 
narratives, rather than institutions alone, frequently drive conversations. Topics such 
as mental health and transparency advanced through public discussion, following the 
narrative flow of user-centred leadership outlined in Social Influence models. Spoken 
feedback is part of the co-creative nature of messaging. Participant comments, 
critiques, and suggestions were not overlooked but actively incorporated into 
messaging strategies, strengthening institutional communication. This adaptive 
communication model highlights the system’s capacity to evolve in response to real-
time public engagement. 
4.8.1 
Code: Echo Governance  
Echo Governance refers to how community voices are heard and acted upon by public 
health institutions, especially through social media. It captures the shift from one way 
governance to a feedback loop where community input shapes institutional actions, 
language, tone and services. This code marks a transition in public health 
communication from static top down approaches to adaptive and dialogic ones. As 
PUBL14 noted, “Once we shared apprehensions about the vaccine slots, it took only 
24 hours for them to update the app. That felt heard” (PUBL14). This highlights the 
value of community input leading to visible system level changes. PROF3 also 
observed, “I saw the Ministry change their messaging on a campaign following 
backlash. That was a pivot in response” (PROF3). These examples illustrate how Echo 
Governance involves institutions filtering and reflecting digital feedback to build a more 
human and responsive relationship between systems and people. 
Research findings clearly show Echo Governance emerging as a dominant force in 
health communication. Abu Dhabi’s public health authorities no longer make decisions 
166 
 
in isolation; they are increasingly influenced by the voice of the community, amplified 
by social media. PUBL14 explained this clearly: “The same day we called out the 
vaccine slots, the app was different. It was reassuring” (PUBL14). This responsiveness 
builds confidence. People feel validated when their opinions lead to real changes, such 
as vaccine slots opening following spontaneous demand. The government appears 
more accessible and willing to listen, embracing a more participatory form of 
governance. PROF3 further described, “I saw the Ministry change the campaign 
message after a strong response and apology backlash. That was a quick recovery” 
(PROF3). This rapid response shows strategic flexibility in policymaking, revealing 
institutions that are adaptable rather than rigid. Such adaptability signals an 
evolving communication culture, where health guidance increasingly involves the 
public in managing emotions and shaping future decisions. 
Public impact is also evident in the tone of communication. PUBL07 wrote, “The tone 
in official sources tends to soften and become ‘less official, more human like’ in general 
as community input surges” (PUBL07). This marks a shift from distant, bureaucratic 
language to more personal and conversational styles. When officials adjust their tone, 
they recognise the public’s emotional state and try to meet people where they are. 
This creates trust and makes institutions appear more sympathetic. Content formats 
and communication channels are similarly shaped by public demand. PUBL13 noted, 
“The real time Q and A feature on Instagram became a thing due to popular demand” 
(PUBL13). This demonstrates that the communication process is increasingly co 
created with the audience. People do not only want information, they also want to 
participate, ask questions and receive answers in real time. Institutions that meet this 
demand show respect and transparency, reinforcing that governance is a dialogue 
rather than a one way broadcast. 
This shift is recognised within institutions as well. PROF2 explained, “We repurpose 
our content calendars according to what gets emotional or informational traction” 
(PROF2). This shows content is no longer planned in isolation, but created based on 
audience preferences. Emotional traction refers to posts that provoke strong reactions 
such as joy, outrage or sadness, while informational traction refers to posts that are 
clear and useful. Both factors influence what content is disseminated. The evolving 
landscape of public health communication is captured by PROF6: “Public health isn’t 
167 
 
static anymore, it’s constantly morphing to fit the social echo” (PROF6). The metaphor 
of a “social echo” highlights how public voices on platforms like Instagram or Twitter 
send ripples that institutions must respond to. Monitoring what content gains 
engagement and what messages evoke emotional responses guides the next round 
of communication. This ongoing recalibration is not optional; it has become essential 
for maintaining trust and relevance. 
Critically, Echo Governance also involves emotional intelligence in responding to 
criticism. PUBL18 observed, “The government’s response to negative comments was 
measured, they listened and did not become defensive” (PUBL18). This highlights a 
level of maturity and confidence in the system. Instead of suppressing criticism, the 
government welcomed it as an opportunity to improve, listening without 
defensiveness. This creates emotional safety and signals to the public that their input 
matters, even when critical. Inclusiveness is another vital aspect of responsive 
governance. PUBL09 noted, “They started including several languages in their 
releases after people complained about accessibility” (PUBL09). This reflects how 
institutions adapt to the diversity of society. Language becomes not only a 
communication tool but a symbol of recognition. Multilingual public health 
messaging makes information more accessible, respectful and effective across 
communities. 
Together, these insights illustrate how institutions are becoming more agile in learning 
from and reflecting their audience in real time. Echo Governance transforms public 
health messaging into a living conversation, where every interaction, comment, repost 
and reaction feeds back into the system and shapes what comes next. This creates a 
health system that is not only reactive but increasingly introspective, responding to 
public needs as soon as they surface. Echo Governance therefore represents an 
emerging era of public health systems and communities working in tandem. By 
adopting multilingual communication, offering real time engagement and reshaping 
posts based on public sentiment, Abu Dhabi’s institutions demonstrate that they are 
speaking as well as listening, hearing and acting. Social media becomes not simply a 
platform but a mirror, reflecting public needs and guiding institutional behaviour. Such 
an adaptive feedback loop strengthens relationships between people and systems, 
168 
 
deepens trust and helps ensure public health communication is timely, respectful and 
grounded in real experiences. 
4.8.2 Code: Sentiment Flux  
“Sentiment Flux” refers to the rapid and often unpredictable shifts in public emotion on 
social media during health crises or periods of heightened concern. The concept 
illustrates how public sentiment, which ranges from optimism and trust to fear, doubt 
or frustration, can change suddenly as a consequence of events, tone or even a single 
post. The digital community becomes emotionally reactive, with platforms such as 
Instagram, Twitter and WhatsApp acting as mirrors for collective feelings. PUBL04 
noted, “One week it’s optimism, another it’s scepticism, it all depends on tone and 
timing” (PUBL04), highlighting the volatile nature of digital mood. 
This emotional noise is not merely background; it directly influences how messages 
are received and what actions people take. PROF7 reinforced this by saying, “Public 
mood can flip overnight, especially immediately following a major event or 
announcement” (PROF7). Sentiment Flux therefore reflects the emotional climate in 
which public health communication occurs and reminds us that timing, tone and 
contextual sensitivity are as critical as the information itself. 
In this study, Sentiment Flux demonstrates that emotional responses online are never 
neutral but constantly shifting, particularly during public health crises. People react 
strongly to announcements, tone, visuals and even institutional silence. These 
emotions spread digitally, generating echo chambers that ripple through social 
networks and escalate into waves of reaction that institutions must learn to monitor 
and navigate. 
PUBL04 captured this well: “One week there’s optimism, the next, scepticism, it just 
depends on tone and timing” (PUBL04). This reinforces the idea that even well crafted 
messages can fail if the surrounding emotional environment is unreceptive. A 
message that offers hope one day may provoke doubt the next, depending on 
prevailing feelings and how events unfold. PROF7 added nuance to this by explaining, 
“Public mood can flip overnight, especially following a significant event or 
announcement” (PROF7). This indicates that external events such as shifts in 
169 
 
regulations, changes in case numbers or political announcements can rapidly reshape 
public emotions. Social media accelerates these shifts by providing real time platforms 
for expressing and amplifying feelings, allowing moods to escalate within hours. 
In such a fluid landscape, health communication must be flexible and responsive 
rather than fixed. The full range of emotions, including fear, joy, frustration and relief, 
resonates throughout online spaces. PUBL20 observed, “Fear, relief and even joy, 
social media mirrors the emotional turbulence of a health crisis” (PUBL20). These 
emotions can be irrational yet remain genuine. A single post or story can spread hope, 
sadness or anxiety to thousands, highlighting the need for institutions to communicate 
facts with emotional awareness. People want to feel understood, not only informed. 
During moments of intensity, this emotional tension becomes even more visible. 
PROF5 observed, “The collective anxiety of people during lockdowns was evident 
online, it informed our digital strategy” (PROF5). This demonstrates how institutions 
had to adjust their tone, shifting from rigid updates to calmer, clearer and more 
empathetic messages. If communication is too vague or too harsh, it risks amplifying 
fear rather than reducing it. 
PUBL03 illustrated how fragile communication can be: “An off key campaign or hitting 
a wrong note, and the community responds with frustration lightning quick” (PUBL03). 
This shows that poorly phrased or ill timed messages can provoke strong backlash, 
undermining trust and damaging credibility. Public health messages must therefore be 
tested not only for accuracy but also for emotional resonance, since misjudged 
communication can spread faster than corrections. This emotional rhythm is visible 
even in routine social media exchanges. PUBL06 commented, “There’s a rhythm to 
the emotional waves, you can feel it in comment sections” (PUBL06). These comment 
sections provide real time emotional cues and immediate feedback. By observing 
these cues, institutions can gauge the community’s pulse and adapt their 
communication strategies. The speed and openness of these signals give health 
authorities insight into how messages are landing at any moment. 
Acknowledging these emotions with empathy is essential. PUBL11 said, “I have more 
faith in the system when it shows some empathy, particularly when people are venting” 
(PUBL11). When people feel distressed or overwhelmed, they are not only seeking 
170 
 
information; they are seeking recognition. Institutions that respond with warmth rather 
than authority build trust and reduce emotional tension. Even individual voices can 
shape collective feeling. PROF8 explained, “Every once in a while, one influencer post 
is going to create a mood swing across an entire digital community” (PROF8). Key 
figures such as celebrities, affected individuals or respected professionals often hold 
emotional weight and can sway public sentiment quickly. Public health systems must 
therefore monitor these influential voices, both for collaboration and for early detection 
of mood shifts. 
Sentiment Flux captures this rapid and potent emotional change transmitted through 
social media and reveals its long lasting impact on public reactions to health 
messaging. It also shows that communication must be emotionally intelligent, timely 
and sensitive to shifting public moods. Messages should be carefully phrased and 
timed to align with public feelings; otherwise they risk being misunderstood or 
dismissed. Institutions that track emotional highs and lows in real time, listen to 
feedback and respond with empathy are more likely to maintain connection and trust. 
By doing so, they can better manage public sentiment even in emotionally charged 
and uncertain times. 
4.8.3 Code: Discourse Contours 
“Discourse Contours” describes how themes, ideas and conversations evolve 
naturally over time in public digital spaces, especially within user generated content 
on social media. These contours show that the public is not merely a passive recipient 
of official messages but an active co creator of the public health narrative. Instead of 
institutions solely driving the agenda, people contribute through stories, hashtags, 
discussions and reactions, shaping new focal points for health conversations that 
institutions must recognise and adapt to. 
As PUBL10 observed, “Mental health was hardly talked about until the public kept 
making it, and now it’s a focus” (PUBL10). This illustrates how certain issues gain 
importance only after the community decides they matter, and it links directly to the 
way digital spaces elevate repeated concerns. PROF4 echoed this, noting, “Nutrition, 
vaccine safety and misinformation naturally emerged as dominant themes” (PROF4), 
meaning these topics gained prominence because they resonated with people rather 
171 
 
than being pre determined by institutions. Together, these examples show that 
Discourse Contours are shaped by emotional, cultural and societal forces grounded in 
the public rather than the system. 
In this study, Discourse Contours highlight the fluid and crowd driven nature of public 
health discussions in the digital age. Participants noted that “when people become 
aware of issues, that’s when change is possible, it all starts on social media” 
(participant). PUBL10 provided a clear illustration: “Mental health was hardly 
mentioned until the public raised it, now it’s a focus” (PUBL10). This demonstrates that 
ordinary users can reshape the health agenda through repetition and persistence. 
Once enough people cared and “shared their outrage on social media,” as one 
participant explained, scattered concerns began to converge and gain traction. 
This natural emergence of key themes is reflected in PROF4’s observation that 
“nutrition, vaccine safety and misinformation became the prevalent themes 
organically” (PROF4). The word “organically” is therefore significant because it shows 
that the evolution of topics was not orchestrated from above but grew from lived 
experiences, everyday concerns and shared stories. These bottom up patterns push 
institutions to adjust their priorities and communications in response to the public 
mood. 
Shifting public discourse also unfolds in stages. PUBL15 explained, “It’s moved on, 
from access, to transparency, to trust” (PUBL15). This shows how the nature of health 
conversations evolves with time, beginning with practical concerns, then transitioning 
to emotional needs such as honesty, reassurance and institutional accountability. 
These shifts reveal underlying changes in how the community interprets public health 
messages. The rise of storytelling is one key driver of these shifts. PUBL02 noted, 
“Stories, not stats, began to be shared. It was a complete narrative change” (PUBL02). 
This indicates that personal stories carry more persuasive force than graphs or data. 
When many individuals share similar narratives, they form a collective emotional 
chorus that pushes particular themes forward. The direction of discourse becomes 
people led rather than institution led. 
Institutions are increasingly learning to monitor this evolution. PROF1 said that 
hashtags allow them to observe recurring concerns, adding, “It’s incredibly 
172 
 
enlightening” (PROF1). Hashtags act as digital markers of collective sentiment, 
helping institutions trace what issues are gaining attention and why. This transforms 
communication from a one way broadcast into an interactive environment where 
listening becomes just as important as speaking. 
Listening also means accepting that the public can set the agenda. PUBL17 explained 
this clearly: “The public picked the topics, institutions simply rode the tide” (PUBL17). 
This reflects a reversal of traditional communication roles. Public health institutions 
are now required to follow the direction set by public discourse if they want to remain 
relevant and trusted. PUBL19 added, “The story arc is a constantly evolving one, user 
generated content is constantly influencing that direction” (PUBL19). This underscores 
the fluidity of digital communication. Themes move, shift and expand depending on 
what people feel, share or debate. Institutions may begin with one message, yet find 
it reshaped by the public as the conversation continues. 
This dynamic is further illustrated by PROF9, who said, “It used to be a fear driven 
narrative, now it’s all about recovery and resilience” (PROF9). This tonal shift aligns 
with broader social changes. Fear may dominate the beginning of a crisis, but as 
people gain more understanding and support one another, the narrative gravitates 
toward hope and resilience. Institutions must adapt their communication or risk 
appearing disconnected. 
Taken together, these reflections show that Discourse Contours are shaped by the 
ongoing negotiation between public voices and institutional responses. Social media 
empowers people to raise issues, share experiences and reshape public 
conversations, and institutions that track these patterns, recognise emerging themes 
and adjust their messages accordingly can build trust and strengthen community 
engagement. Discourse Contours demonstrate that today, health communication is 
shaped as much by public discourse as by official announcements. The community, 
through emotional storytelling, recurring themes and shared experiences, guides the 
tone and agenda. Institutions that understand and adapt to these shifting contours 
remain more relevant, more trusted and more effective in guiding public health 
behaviour. In a digital landscape, the conversation becomes the compass, and 
institutions that read its direction can respond with clarity, empathy and strategic 
awareness.  
173 
 
4.8.4 Code: Narrative Feedback 
“Narrative Feedback” is the process by which public health messages are continuously 
refined and adjusted based on how the public actually responds to them online. This 
highlights the dynamic and iterative nature of digital communication, where comments, 
reactions and engagement levels are treated as essential inputs that shape the next 
round of messaging. This approach reveals a collaborative form of communication in 
which the audience actively helps shape and reshape the story. 
PROF6 summed it up simply: “We use tiny online sites to test messages then scale 
depending on reaction” (PROF6), showing that institutions now view public platforms 
as living laboratories. PUBL12 reinforced this by stating, “Comments are like real time 
focus groups, we tweak messaging almost instantly” (PUBL12), illustrating the rapid 
integration of feedback to adjust tone, visuals and copy. Together, these descriptions 
define Narrative Feedback as an adaptive and audience driven rhythm that builds trust 
and effectiveness by making messages more empathetic, emotionally attuned and 
respectful. 
In this article, Narrative Feedback is one of the clearest examples of how social media 
has transformed health communication from a one way broadcast into interactive 
dialogue. Health officials in Abu Dhabi do not merely observe public response, they 
refine their communication based on what they see in real time. This process values 
the audience’s voice and recognises communication as a two way street. 
PROF6’s statement, “we test messages in small online spaces and then scale based 
on reactions” (PROF6), captures this well. Before launching major campaigns, 
organisations use digital platforms to gauge audience interest and then tweak 
messages for tone, clarity and resonance. PUBL12 compares public comments to 
“real time focus groups” and notes, “we tweak messaging almost instantly” (PUBL12). 
This shows how feedback has become central to communication strategy. Rather than 
following fixed plans, communicators now adapt in response to digital reactions such 
as likes, shares, silence or criticism. This approach keeps health messaging aligned 
with how people actually think, feel and live. 
174 
 
The shift in tone driven by this feedback loop is noted by PROF2, who explained, “At 
first no posts were too clinical. Now they are more empathetic because of feedback 
loops” (PROF2). This highlights a crucial point: factual accuracy alone does not ensure 
engagement. When people say messages feel cold or robotic, institutions adjust the 
tone as a sign of respect and responsiveness. PUBL08 described this emotional 
recalibration clearly: “We chased vaccine fatigue by seeing people disengage, and 
shifted to uplifting stories” (PUBL08). Here, the public’s declining engagement 
signalled the need for a more positive, hopeful narrative. The change was not 
guesswork, but grounded in reactions from the audience. These adjustments helped 
reconnect people who had emotionally withdrawn. 
Over time, active listening and iteration produce more sophisticated communication 
strategies. PROF5 observed, “Our communications have matured as we have learned 
from our digital audience” (PROF5). The word “matured” emphasises that institutions 
have moved beyond basic information delivery to focus on relationship building. They 
no longer speak at people, they speak with them. Each round of engagement becomes 
an opportunity to refine approaches and build credibility. 
One of the most insightful perspectives comes from PUBL16, who said, “The 
community essentially co authors the health narrative through reaction” (PUBL16). 
This reframes the communication dynamic entirely. Public discourse is no longer 
dominated by institutions, but co created by those who interact with it. Health 
messaging is therefore fluid and collaborative, reflecting the lived experience of the 
community. 
PUBL21 added an important dimension: “Feedback is more than criticism, it is design 
input” (PUBL21). This shows a shift in how institutions interpret complaints and 
suggestions. Instead of seeing negative reactions as obstacles, they now view them 
as opportunities. Each comment helps refine language, imagery, structure and 
emotional impact. PROF1 summarised the importance of this cycle: “We learned it the 
hard way, without closing the feedback loop trust breaks down” (PROF1). This means 
that simply listening is not enough. Institutions must respond, adjust and demonstrate 
that public input leads to change. When people see their voices reflected in later 
messages, trust deepens. When they feel ignored, trust erodes. 
175 
 
Taken together, these voices demonstrate that Narrative Feedback is not a secondary 
task, but central to how digital public health systems operate today. The story is no 
longer top down. It is co created, continuously revised and shaped by emotion, 
engagement and interaction. This makes health organisations appear more flexible, 
more approachable and more trustworthy. 
Narrative Feedback shows that public health communication has become a 
collaborative and cyclical process. Institutions must listen attentively, adapt quickly 
and speak with empathy. Each message becomes part of a conversation, and every 
response becomes an opportunity to learn. This two way engagement creates a 
synergy in which people and systems become more connected and communication 
remains aligned with public needs. When health institutions treat feedback not as data 
but as co authorship, they appear more human, more responsible and more effective 
at guiding public health behaviour. 
 
 
4.9 DISCUSSION  
 
The next section builds on theoretical foundations from the Health Belief Model and 
Social Influence Theory to explain the implications of the findings within frameworks 
of interactive, emotional, and co-constructed public health discourse. Focusing on the 
mechanics of interactive communication and participatory engagement, this section 
situates the findings within existing scholarship on behaviour change, trust dynamics, 
and institutional messaging practices. Drawing on extensive literature, it describes 
how digital platforms have transformed public health communication in Abu Dhabi from 
a one-way broadcast of information into a mutual, empowered engagement. The 
section conceptualises the interplay between individual agency, community norms, 
emotional resonance, and institutional legitimacy in shaping public health at a micro 
level through social media. In this sense, the analysis demonstrates how 
communication has shifted from a top-down model to a reciprocally shaped digital 
dialogue, where meaning is influenced jointly by institutions and the public. 
176 
 
4.9.1 
Interactive Communication and Community Participation 
 
The subtheme Dialogic Resonance highlights the interactive nature of social media 
and shows how two-way communication has shifted public health messaging in Abu 
Dhabi from a traditional top-down transmission model to a more dialogic and 
participatory form of engagement. Respondents consistently characterised this 
transition as a movement from broadcasting to conversation. As one public participant 
(PUBL13) stated, “I posted on waiting times and hours later the health department 
replied directly – you felt they were listening.” This interaction demonstrates the 
connection between institutional responsiveness, relational trust, and perceptions of 
inclusivity within governance. 
This finding mirrors an international shift in digital healthcare communication. Burgess 
et al. (2021) emphasise the importance of participatory community engagement as a 
critical component of effective public health response. Azer and Alexander (2022) 
similarly note that bidirectional communication grounded in empathy and 
timeliness elicits stronger behavioural responses than one-way information 
delivery. These principles reflect the emerging Abu Dhabi approach, which appears 
increasingly human-centred. 
Experienced professionals also recognised this transformation. As PROF4 observed, 
“We learn from the public just as much as we educate.” This demonstrates what 
Fedorowicz et al. (2022) describe as the integration of “citizen voice” into health 
decision-making through digital dialogue. The consultative feedback loop described 
here aligns with Campbell and Rudan’s (2020) argument that meaningful engagement 
turns public communication from persuasive into collaborative. 
The theme Discursive Pulse also appeared frequently, referring to the tone and 
rhythm of communication. PROF5 noted, “If the message is robotic, we lose them; 
empathy keeps people reading.” PUBL05 similarly reflected that official posts now use 
“simpler, kinder language — less jargon, more clarity.” These statements reflect 
Chaudhri et al. (2021), who argue that reputational trust in healthcare communication 
is strengthened when messages are accessible and emotionally warm. Likewise, 
177 
 
Eghtesadi and Florea (2020) report that empathetic, accommodating online tone 
enhances compliance and trust during crises. 
These narratives indicate that word choice and emotional tone are not stylistic 
additions but critical indicators of authenticity and credibility. As PUBL10 
explained, “When you see them respond fast, what it does it builds up trust in them — 
this is a live thing happening; it’s not automated.” These findings affirm Li et al. (2022), 
whose work suggests that timeliness and information richness directly support public 
participation during health emergencies. In this way, Abu Dhabi’s communication 
approach can be seen as symbiotic, with institutions and publics co-generating 
communicative meaning. 
The second subtheme, Participatory Echo, demonstrates how online participation 
influences institutional behaviour. PUBL03 stated, “We raised the issue of older people 
not being able to get to vaccine centres. And a week later… it was taken up in a 
livestream — that’s not much but it is proof our voice matters.” This reflects the 
democratising capacity identified by Duku et al. (2022), who found that structured 
community engagement enhances institutional responsiveness and policy legitimacy. 
PROF8 added that “a single post can incite collective change,” mirroring Ohmer et al. 
(2022) who observed that interactive digital platforms allow communities to co-produce 
solutions rather than merely consume them. 
Participants described a new sense of conversational power. PUBL16 observed, “I 
feel like finally I can say something and be heard — agency we’ve never had before.” 
PROF3 echoed this sentiment: “Empowerment starts when a person feels they have 
been heard.” These experiences are consistent with Alicea-Alvarez et al. (2016), who 
found that participatory discussion fosters empowerment by helping individuals see 
themselves as contributors to social change. Naeem and Ozuem (2021) similarly 
argue that social media dissolves hierarchical boundaries, creating “horizontal 
engagement spaces.” 
Perceived institutional responsiveness strengthened this sense of agency. PUBL11 
said, “The ability to speak, be heard and see change — that’s agency in practice.” This 
resonates with Farley et al. (2021), who show that visible feedback mechanisms 
maintain confidence and build trust, particularly among marginalised groups. PROF6 
178 
 
added, “It’s not only one way, it’s being accessible to people, and letting people access 
us,” echoing Massey et al. (2021), who report that reciprocal communication enhances 
engagement and sustained compliance with public health guidance. 
These digital exchanges also reflect components of the Health Belief Model (HBM). 
Participants’ experiences indicate strengthened perceived benefits and cues to action 
when messages were empathetic and interactive. As PUBL04 explained, “If they 
respond in my language or with empathy then I want to listen.” This aligns with 
Alhaimer (2022), who found that culturally framed, emotionally grounded messages 
increase public self-efficacy and comprehension. Alejandro et al. (2022) also show 
that HBM-based communication increases behavioural intention when delivered 
through relatable social media content. 
Social Influence Theory (SIT) offers further insight. PROF2 reflected, “When people 
see that their post is shared by the health department, they feel as though they are 
contributing.” This interactional validation mirrors the internalisation mechanisms 
identified by Tian et al. (2023), where recognition of user participation strengthens trust 
and engagement. Thus, media culture shifts from broadcast to what can be 
understood as a conversational commons, shaped by trust, empathy, and co-
creation. 
References to rhythm and timing indicate that emotional cadence sustains 
engagement (“the right tone keeps engagement alive,” as PROF5 noted). Chaudhri et 
al. (2021) show that tone and accessibility build authenticity and relational capital. 
Jeyaraman et al. (2023) similarly argue that social-media storytelling allows 
professionals and communities to co-produce narratives of care that reinforce 
relationships. Respondents also observed a redistribution of communicative power. 
As PROF7 put it, “They have changed the winners of who can speak. It’s no longer 
top-down — it’s shared.” This aligns with Pangborn et al. (2023), who documented 
how social-media storytelling allowed frontline workers to reshape health narratives 
and foster emotional support. Collectively, these findings show a dual paradigm shift: 
from hierarchical circulation to dialogic co-creation, and from institution-dominated 
messaging to mutually constructed communication shaped by real-time 
interaction. This maturation aligns with global evidence for participatory digital health 
engagement (Mao et al., 2023; Vatcharavongvan et al., 2023).  
179 
 
4.9.2 The Effect of Social Media on Consumer Confidence and the Public 
Perception  
The growing importance of social media as a communication channel in the public 
health arena provides an active space to build trust, influence perception, and engage 
the public in informed, participatory discourse. This section examines how people 
construe digital health messages and how these interpretations shape, and are 
shaped by, perceptions of institutional credibility and public trust. Drawing on 
qualitative interview data and thematic constructs, including perceptual cartography, 
dialogic resonance, and communal ethos on one hand, and mobilised cognition and 
digital reverberation on the other, the data reveal the cognitive and emotional 
landscape through which health narratives circulate across digital communication 
spaces. 
Respondents emphasised that health communication via social media is particularly 
effective when it follows a dialogic approach, and where meaning is co-constructed 
through mutual exchange between institutions and the public. The concept of mutual 
respect was clearly illustrated when participants described how direct responses to 
their queries made them feel both empowered and acknowledged. This resonates 
with earlier work by Alamri et al. (2019), who contend that social media enables more 
direct interaction between the public and health authorities, fostering immediacy, trust, 
and transparency. Similarly, Heldman et al. (2013) highlight the shift from 
unidirectional messaging to dialogic communication, wherein the public becomes an 
active co-producer of discourse rather than a passive recipient of information. These 
discursive rhythms inform the tone, energy, and compassion of communication. When 
communication becomes human, timely, and relevant, rather than distant or overly 
technical, it ceases to function as generic messaging and becomes a connection that 
resonates with people.  
The idea of a participatory echo, where public discourse refracts back through 
institutional channels, further complicates the conventional divide between citizen and 
sovereign. Multiple interviewees provided examples where social media activity such 
as comments, stories, or hashtags precipitated policy responses or institutional 
recognition. This finding aligns with work by Belt et al. (2013), who found that patients 
are more likely to trust health systems when their feedback is publicly acknowledged. 
180 
 
The reciprocity of such interaction fosters what can be described as conversational 
agency, enabling individuals to experience agency through the expression of concerns 
and the visible impact of their contributions. This aligns with Graffigna et al. (2021), 
who identify empowerment as a key mediator between digital engagement and 
adherence to health behaviours. 
The emotional and cognitive rendering of messages, categorised here under the 
umbrella term perceptual cartography, reveals that people navigate public health 
communication using intuitive heuristics rather than statistical logic. Codes such as 
risk intuition show that emotional proximity to health events, for example hearing about 
peers who are unwell, often triggers stronger feelings of vulnerability than impersonal 
demographic data. Similar findings are evident in Health Belief Model studies by Al-
Metwali et al. (2021), which show that perceived susceptibility is heightened by 
anecdotal evidence rather than formal recommendations. 
The semiotics of trust, another key code, demonstrates how visual cues such as status 
markers, language, uniforms, official logos, or even typographic choices contribute to 
perceptions of legitimacy. These symbolic elements act as heuristics for distinguishing 
credible from non-credible information, a phenomenon also reported by Hita et al. 
(2023) in their study of trust mechanisms during pandemic communication. Anticipated 
benefits, or what participants referred to as the horizon of benefits, emerged as key 
motivators for action. Members of the public engaged in order to access information, 
seek reassurance, and identify clear routes to health-promoting behaviours. This 
finding corresponds with Davies and Mann (2023), who observed that social media 
campaigns were more effective when they conveyed a clear vision of the health gains 
associated with preventative action. 
However, cognitive dissonance was a recurring theme, and participants frequently 
expressed confusion and frustration when credible sources delivered inconsistent 
information. Such inconsistencies undermined trust and led to disengagement. This 
was also observed by Gesser-Edelsburg (2021), who found that when messaging 
across platforms lacked coherence, public confidence was eroded. The collective 
belief in society theme demonstrated the impact of shared norms and social identity 
on community engagement. Normative adherence occurred when users adopted 
181 
 
health behaviours because such behaviours were visible and part of their network’s 
norms.  
Beyond behaviour, collective identity was constructed through symbolic interaction 
with health organisations. Individuals shared public health content not merely to 
inform, but to signal group membership — to say, “I’m here, I support this, these values 
speak to me.” This transliteration of public health branding into civic identity is explored 
by Sharp (2011), who argues that when public health branding reflects communal 
ethos and culture, it becomes tied to broader identity structures. One sub-theme of 
trust, relational trust, concerned the presence of relatable messengers. When viewers 
saw individuals who resembled them in language, ethnicity, or social context, they 
reported higher levels of trust in health content. This supports findings by Alhaimer 
(2022) and Vatcharavongvan et al. (2023), who emphasise that culturally grounded 
and demographically specific communication strategies foster greater receptivity and 
trust. Additionally, when the tone of messages was culturally meaningful, users 
perceived that their identity was respected, leading to symbolic alignment between 
institutional narratives and user worldviews. 
Social media also generated a variety of cognitive and behavioural effects — 
collectively mobilized cognition. Action-staged reminders, stories, and visual cues had 
immediate impact on prompting actions such as booking appointments or attending 
check-ups. This is consistent with Yoo, Kim, and Lee (2018), who demonstrated that 
campaigns with strong cues to action significantly increase behavioural intention. It 
was also a matter of affect, meaning the emotional charge of the message. Emotional 
stories, especially about recovery or loss, inspired action more than statistics. This 
aligns with Alejandro et al. (2022), who contend that emotional appeals improve 
memory and have persuasive effects in health communication. 
There was also evidence of behavioural contagion, with some participants becoming 
more motivated simply by observing others. Their responses showed that sharing was 
intentional due to common practice and peer behaviour, aligning with social learning 
theory and studies such as Azer and Alexander (2022). Self-efficacy acted as the 
bridge connecting intention with action. Visual graphics, FAQs, and peer testimony 
helped individuals feel capable of acting by reducing cognitive burden and increasing 
confidence. The role of instructional clarity in enhancing self-efficacy is highlighted by 
182 
 
Ayaz-Alkaya et al. (2020), who show that structured message content supports action 
in health settings. 
At a macro level, this analysis highlights how institutions process and respond to public 
sentiment online. A crucial enabler of trust was echo governance, where feedback 
loops informed adjustments to policy or communication. When individuals saw that the 
system responded to their voices, for example by changing apps or refining messages, 
they perceived it as responsive and human. This form of adaptive governing is 
supported by Campbell and Rudan (2020), who argue that flexibility is vital for 
credibility during health crises. Sentiment flux also illustrated how emotionally volatile 
digital spaces can be, with moods shifting in response to message tone, timing, and 
context. This requires communication plans that are adaptable, as recommended 
by Landi et al. (2021). 
Discourse contours illuminated the organic evolution of health themes on social media, 
as issues such as mental health or vaccine safety gained traction through persistent 
public discussion. This bottom-up thematic shaping aligns with Ni et al. (2021), who 
highlight the importance of grassroots discursive influence. Narrative feedback loops 
then enable messages to be refined in real time based on audience reaction. 
Institutions that succeeded were those that listened, adjusted tone, and co-authored 
public health narratives with their audiences. This iterative co-creation reflects the 
participatory models advanced by contemporary scholarship. 
Taken together, this thematic synthesis demonstrates that social media is not merely 
a transmission platform but a site of participatory work where meaning, trust, and 
action are constantly co-produced. Institutional legitimacy is not imposed from above, 
but earned through dialogic atonement, emotional intelligence, and adaptive 
responsiveness. Public trust in health systems is built not just through information, but 
through shared identity, responsiveness, and affective engagement, a concept well 
anchored in contemporary theories of digital health communication.  
4.9.3 Social Media Use and Public Health Results  
The public health implications of social media engagement in Abu Dhabi are evident 
in the cognitive, emotional, peer, and design dimensions of digital messaging. The 
183 
 
findings show that when platforms stimulate both reasoning and feeling, they do not 
simply raise awareness but drive concrete health action. Mobilized Cognition captures 
this movement from passive reception to active participation through Action Catalysts, 
Influence Valence, Compliance Drift, and the Self-Efficacy Loop. 
Action Catalysts were reflected in participants’ accounts of timely reminders and visual 
cues that acted as behavioural prompts. PUBL08 described booking a check-up after 
a single tweet, and PROF6 noted that urgency in digital design produces a “drive to 
decide now.” These findings align with Mao et al. (2023) and Li et al. (2022), who 
demonstrated that timely crisis communication increases public responsiveness. In 
Abu Dhabi, daily digital engagement provided fertile ground for countdowns, 
reminders, and interactive notices that translated directly into preventive behaviour. 
Affective content, captured through Influence Valence, strongly predicted engagement 
and behavioural intention. Participants such as PUBL01 and PUBL10 highlighted how 
personal stories of illness or recovery inspired action, and PROF9 emphasised that 
messages balanced “urgency and empathy.” These insights correspond with 
Pangborn et al. (2023) and Hita et al. (2023), who found that affectively encoded 
narratives are more likely to be internalised and shared, aligning with the Health Belief 
Model (HBM), which posits that emotional cues heighten perceived susceptibility and 
motivate action (Alejandro et al., 2022). 
Social influence processes were equally important. Compliance Drift demonstrated 
that visible norms normalise compliance. Seeing vaccine selfies, check-ins, or shared 
routines influenced behaviour. PUBL02 noted that “it’s a nature that’s easy to follow,” 
and PROF2 described this not as pressure but “social permission.” This mirrors 
reference group behaviour (Kelley, 1955) and the collectivist, digitally literate social 
environment of Abu Dhabi. Studies such as Graffigna et al. (2021) similarly argue that 
visible community endorsement enhances behavioural uptake. 
Yet motivation must be paired with capability. The Self-Efficacy Loop emerged when 
participants described how step-by-step guides, live demonstrations, and infographics 
reduced cognitive barriers. PUBL13 said, “If I feel like I know it, I’m more likely to do 
it,” and PROF8 stressed that clear instructions “lower the mental barrier.” This aligns 
with HBM constructs regarding the centrality of self-efficacy (Ayaz-Alkaya et al., 2020). 
184 
 
For a diverse population, simplified visuals and accessible formats ensured that 
people not only understood what to do but felt able to do it. 
Public absorption of health messages is also participatory. Respondents framed their 
digital habits as socially grounded, shaped by observing others and recognising the 
cost of inaction. This reflects Dunn et al. (2018), who showed that youth engagement 
with prevention campaigns was driven by emotional resonance and peer 
endorsement, and Azer and Alexander (2022), who identified similar dynamics during 
COVID-19. The Abu Dhabi context stands out because institutions repeatedly adapted 
tone and content in line with digital feedback, generating a sense of co-authorship that 
strengthened trust. Cultural responsiveness further amplified trust. Participants noted 
higher confidence when messages included multilingual content, culturally familiar 
imagery, and national symbols. This supports Burgess et al. (2021), who emphasise 
that culturally attuned engagement enhances intervention success. Abu Dhabi’s 
demographic diversity required a broad communicative repertoire capable of speaking 
to Emiratis and expatriates alike. 
Sustained engagement was also important. Feedback loops allowed institutions to 
remain attuned to public sentiment, with repeated exposure to reminders, stories, 
norms, and guidance transforming momentary engagement into ongoing behavioural 
reinforcement. Duku et al. (2022) similarly argue that continuous community 
engagement is essential for influencing behaviour. HBM constructs such as perceived 
threat, benefits, and efficacy were shown to be dynamic, continually recalibrated 
through digital interaction. Mao et al. (2023) found that behavioural cues delivered 
through relatable narratives reinforce perceptions of risk and benefit—patterns 
strongly reflected in Abu Dhabi. 
The convergence of immediacy, emotion, peer influence, and empowerment forms an 
ecosystem where digital engagement becomes behavioural architecture. Action 
Catalysts trigger behaviour; Influence Valence deepens emotional meaning; 
Compliance Drift normalises behaviour through visibility; and the Self-Efficacy Loop 
builds capability. Together, these mechanisms explain how digital communication 
shaped real-world action. Health behaviour emerged from an interdependent system 
of sense-making and value alignment. Normative adhesion was visible as individuals 
adopted health behaviours to integrate within their digital community, echoing 
185 
 
institutional isomorphism (DiMaggio and Powell, 1983). Social media made norms 
visible and transformed preventive practices into forms of social currency, consistent 
with Davies and Mann (2023). 
Trust functioned as a critical compliance moderator. PUBL05 felt reassured when 
Emiratis featured in campaigns, reflecting findings by Freire et al. (2023), who argue 
that messages resonate more strongly when delivered by culturally proximate 
messengers. In a multicultural environment, authenticity and representational 
closeness were essential. Eghtesadi and Florea (2020) highlight the importance of 
culturally embedded crisis communication, a dynamic clearly present in Abu Dhabi. 
Echo Governance was central to building trust. Participants described visible policy 
changes following online feedback, an example of institutions demonstrating respect 
and inclusion. This is consistent with Franceschini and Marletto (2019), who argue that 
iterative institutional responsiveness strengthens trust. In Abu Dhabi, adaptations in 
language, app design, and campaign formats reinforced the perception of a listening 
system. 
Sentiment shifts further influenced institutional decision-making. Rapid emotional 
fluctuations in comment sections, hashtags, and influencer posts shaped 
communication strategy. Pangborn et al. (2023) describe such narratives as 
barometers of collective affect, requiring institutions to recalibrate tone accordingly. 
Participants described how issues raised online were later addressed in livestreams 
or policy updates, reinforcing civic ownership (Duku et al., 2022). Cognitive 
dissonance emerged when conflicting messages circulated. Participants like PUBL13 
described confusion when guidelines contradicted each other, echoing Hita et al. 
(2023), who linked inconsistent information to anxiety and erosion of trust. Institutions 
that maintained clear, consistent, emotionally intelligent messaging—such as those in 
Park et al. (2016)—secured higher engagement. 
Symbolic alignment and Benefit Horizon showed that messaging must be aspirational 
as well as informational. Campaigns invoking themes of unity and resilience resonated 
deeply, positioning compliance within a shared cultural narrative. As Bornkessel et al. 
(2014) and Navarro et al. (2020) suggest, storytelling transforms public health 
messages into shared human truths. Ultimately, social media is a dynamic space 
where norms, emotions, and identities are continuously negotiated. In Abu Dhabi, 
186 
 
digital engagement contributed directly to higher adherence to vaccination, screening, 
and preventive behaviour. Public trust emerged through responsiveness, emotional 
resonance, and cultural attunement. These findings show that when used 
authentically, social media becomes not only a communication tool but a mechanism 
for behavioural change and health citizenship. 
4.10 SUMMARY  
Chapter 5 also elaborates on how public health communication in Abu Dhabi has been 
reframed through its convergence with social media, transitioning from a one-way 
apparatus of information distribution to a participatory model that is enacted 
collaboratively by institutions and the public. The chapter synthesizes five major 
themes in order to clarify how digital environments generate the emotional, cognitive, 
and relational conditions necessary for eudaimonic public health behavior. The 
chapter shows that on the basis of these findings public health communication has 
emerged as a dialogical communicative event resonating emotionally and culturally, 
reflecting shared values and beliefs in local communities. 
The first important observation is that digital communication has redefined how 
people live and understand public health messages. Hearing participants consistently 
reported 
how 
responsiveness, 
tone 
and 
accessibility 
made 
institutional 
communication feel more human-like and trusting. Messages were no longer viewed 
as distant or administrative, but as conversations that took into consideration people 
and their emotions and cultural contexts. Trust was established, the message 
understood better and a sense of joint ownership developed through this relational 
process. The public’s responses were observed to impact greatly on the way that 
institutional posts are drafted, phrased and delivered thus pointing towards a co-
constructed communication model in which social voices genuinely influence how 
discussions lead. 
A second key result pertains to the emotional infrastructure of digital public health 
communication. Content that had high emotional valance was consistently cited as 
being more memorable and impactful than numerical data. Recovery, loss or 
community solidarity stories increased perceived susceptibility to and inspired 
empathy for disease consequences, suggesting an urgency to act. Participants 
187 
 
explained how emotions of messages were either more or less congruent with the 
emotions of the public mood and that this strengthened or weakened trust as a result. 
This indicates that emotion attunement is not a matter of aesthetic preference but 
essentially a strategic necessity in how it shapes behavior and institution regulation. 
Simultaneously, social signals and peer effects were observed to be strong drivers of 
behaviour. Observability of others behaviours – for example vaccine selfies, check-ins 
or shared testimonies – normalised adherence and generated a perception of social 
permission. Health behaviours were frequently discussed by participants as being 
undertaken because they are what other people do or socialll practised within their 
network of society. This process of normative modelling was reinforced by cultural 
affinity and identification, particularly when communications used linguistically or 
culturally coherent communicators. 
The cognitive aspect of communication also proved to be important. When messages 
provided clear instructions, step-by-step guides and practical reassurance people 
were more likely to act. Self efficacy was raised when uncertainty decreased and an 
act became doable by visual demonstrations, FAQs, or simple language. Participants 
viewed these types of direction as instrumental in translating intention to action and 
emphasized the necessity for clarity, smart design, and ongoing encouragement. 
The chapter demonstrates that public health communication is not static but subject 
to feedback loops, emotional sw troughs and discursive fashion. Public opinion shaped 
campaign shifts, as organizations harnessed online reaction to tweak their approaches 
in real time. This reflective system generated an experience of civic engagement and 
reinforced the relational tie between the government and community, so that health 
was accepted as having more common ground; health for all became ideally.  
 
 
  
188 
 
5 CHAPTER 5: CONCEPTUAL FRAMEWORK  
5.1 INTRODUCTION 
The rise of social media as a disruptive influence on healthcare communication has 
reframed the way public health systems interact with communities, nudge behaviour 
change, and build trust. In Chapter 5, the “Digital Reverberations in Health 
Communication” framework is introduced and explained as an analytical tool, nested 
on multiple levels and conceived precisely to analyse the interconnectedness of health 
communication, community engagement, and public health branding from ADTDAD’s 
point of view. This framework is based on the constructs of the Health Belief Model 
(HBM) and Social Influence Theory (SIT), but provides a synthesised view that 
includes the psychological, social, and technological aspects of digital engagement. 
The model represents communication not as a linear process, but as a feedback loop 
— a reverberating system in which digital interactions perpetually mould and are 
moulded by public opinion, institutional narratives, and behavioural reactions. In doing 
so, it lays a solid groundwork for examining the ways in which social media functions 
as a trigger and facilitator of participatory public health discourse. This chapter 
describes the theoretical foundation, profile of constructs, and structure of the 
framework, and locates its application within the context of a multicultural, digitised 
public health system in Abu Dhabi. Through a combination of empirical research and 
theory-building, the chapter situates this framework as a pragmatic tool for 
policymakers and health communicators seeking to optimise the role that digital 
platforms can play in promoting public trust, increasing engagement, and improving 
health outcomes. 
5.1 THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK INTERACTION WITH THE CONCEPTUAL FRAMEWORK  
Figure 5.1, Digital Reverberations in Health Communication, is the product of, and 
a direct response to, the themes identified — a distillation of this study’s processual 
chapter in visual form. Chapter Five 5.1  Thematic Analysis set forth in Chapter 4 
marks that it is not a conceptual model, but an empirically based representation in 
which every element, link, and direction of effect stems from codes/themes identified 
during systematic thematic analysis. The model illustrates the circular and 
multifaceted forms through which communication takes place among Abu Dhabi 
189 
 
public health agencies and communities through social media. It models a 
conversational environment that develops meaning, trust, and interaction through 
dynamic dialogic exchange, emotional resonance, and behavioural feedback. This 
figure is developed in the interpretivist logic described by the methodological 
framework, within which theory arises inductively from data rather than being imposed 
a priori (Naeem et al., 2023). 
In the middle of the figure, Dialogic Resonance, which encapsulates the interpretive 
centre of the data, typifies dynamic reciprocity between health authorities and the 
community, traversing back and forth prominently in participant narratives. The 
dialogic core is encompassed by the concentric territories of Symbolic Exchange, 
Discursive Pulse, Participatory Echo, and Conversational Agency. These layers 
directly parallel the primary and sub-themes of Chapter 4. For example, Symbolic 
Exchange accounts for participants’ descriptions of mediated meaning-making and 
emotional attunement to institutional health discourse, and Discursive Pulse measures 
the pulse or flow of online conversation as detected through sub-themed sentiment 
and tone. So too do Participatory Echo and Conversational Agency spring from 
participant accounts of the rejection of viewing communication as passive 
reception, in which participants actively engage in shaping and redistributing 
digital material. Together, these elements constitute the communicative life cycle of 
public health messages on social media — from institutional dissemination to public 
response, interpretation, and behaviour change. 
The top part of the framework, Digital Reverberations, is the source of discourse and 
its propagation through social media. Such behaviour is linked directly to the code 
Narrative Feedback from Chapter 4, in which participants reflected on how posts, 
replies, and shares shaped the reach and meaning of a message. The paired outflows 
of Narrative Feedback and Discourse Contours give a visual sense of the feedback 
loops shaping broadcast into conversation. This top-down and bottom-up dynamic 
represents the dialogic structure of digital communication implicated in the data, 
whereby social media functions both as a transmitter of information (distribution 
channel) and a locus of participative reflection. 
The left of the figure is Communal Ethos, the community sense of social norms, moral 
codes, and shared values among Abu Dhabi’s multicultural public. It is based on the 
190 
 
conceptual evidence that public trust, cultural match, and emotional tone underpin 
engagement. When we respond to the cultural and moral dimensions of people’s lives, 
then codes like Relational Trust, Symbolic Alignment, and Normative Adhesion are 
found here. It is in line with findings from Alhaimer (2022) and Burgess et al. (2021), 
suggesting that public health communication is effective only when it becomes 
culturally credible and empathetic. Conversely, the right-hand panel displays 
Perceptual Cartography, and it illustrates how people navigate and interpret whatever 
health cues they receive. It encompasses such elements as Benefit Horizon, Cognitive 
Dissonance, Risk Intuition, and Trust Semiotics, all of which were observable in 
participants’ stories, where they articulated how their beliefs interacted with issues 
around credibility and risk when consuming online health information. The reciprocity 
of Communal Ethos and Perceptual Cartography, on one hand, and the balance 
between collective identity and cognitive self on the other, constitute, in turn, a 
horizontal axis of this model. 
Mobilised Cognition is the lowest-level component of the framework, which reflects the 
conversion of digital context to real-world behaviour. It is also related to other sub-
themes such as Influence Valence, Action Catalysts, Compliance Drift, and the Self-
Efficacy Loop, which altogether provide a structure describing how taking part, 
trusting, and emotionally resonating translate into intention or action. This aspect of 
the framework captures the end point of the social media–based communication 
process, in which perception is translated into performance and understanding 
translates into health practice. 
The form-and-content unity is reflected in the theoretical combination of HBM-SIT as 
a basis for interpreting the findings. The HBM provides the cognitive and motivational 
element of health behaviour, while SIT adds the social and normative processes that 
show how collective influence affects individual choices. The concepts of perceived 
susceptibility, perceived severity, benefits, and barriers are positioned most clearly in 
the P1 (Perceptual Cartography) space. Statements made by participants suggested 
that online forums, infographics, and shared posts served as cognitive maps through 
which they evaluated their susceptibility, the severity of disease, and the effectiveness 
of protective measures. This is in accordance with the study of Vatcharavongvan et al. 
(2023) and Youssef et al. (2022), in which online engagement increases risk 
191 
 
perceptions and leads to protective health behaviours. Risk Intuition and Cognitive 
Dissonance in the model therefore map directly to the relationship between perceived 
susceptibility and barriers that is, a psychological conflict between awareness and 
resistance. 
Benefit Horizon also records the perceived benefits and cues to action, given that 
messages on social media such as stories with positive emotional content, visual 
information, and testimonial evidence — encourage health behaviours. This is similar 
to the results of Alhaimer (2022) and Mao et al. (2023), who showed the effectiveness 
of affectively laden health messages across social media, particularly ones couched 
in culturally appropriate and outcome-focused language as digital cues to action for 
boosting efficacy beliefs and normalising target behaviours. HBM’s emphasis on 
confidence and agency  individuals’ belief in personal capabilities — is illustrated 
through the Self-Efficacy Loop (lower panel of the model), which is in line with HBM’s 
focus on “confidence” and shows how successful exposure to others’ beliefs and 
perceived responsiveness of institutions can strengthen or weaken these beliefs in 
their own personal capability. In doing so, the framework illustrates how social media 
conceptualises each HBM component, transforming intangible cognitive constructs 
into tangible communicative exchanges. 
Social Influence Theory is the thread that runs through the model’s left-hand-side 
constructs: namely, Communal Ethos, Symbolic Exchange, and Participatory Echo. 
The phenomena of compliance, identification, and internalisation, as characterised in 
SIT, are again echoed in the present focus group participants’ discussions of social 
norms, peer acceptance, and image credibility. Finally, Normative Adhesion and 
Collective Ideals describe the social underpinning of behaviour, where individuals 
perform in congruence with the perceived norms of their group. This is supported by 
Naeem and Ozuem (2021), who examined how professionals digitally negotiate their 
identities in public health. Analogously, Symbolic Alignment relates to the SIT concept 
of identification people taking on the attitudes or behaviours of others whom they 
admire or consider high-status. Participants commonly recounted replying to 
messages shared by their revered doctors, religious authorities, or Emirati influencers, 
highlighting how messengers’ attributed credibility facilitated adherence to 
recommended behaviours. This is consistent with Odunsi and Farris (2023), who 
192 
 
recently discovered that message source credibility significantly enhances behavioural 
intention when used in pandemic-related health campaigns. 
The idea of Participatory Echo illustrates how social inspiration and community 
dialogue are interdependent. When participants like, comment on, or re-create 
content, they are affirming collectively and reinforcing socially both normative 
expectations and individual stake/commitment. This interactional feedback is the 
implemented version of SIT's internalisation: repeated practice turns external 
persuasion into inner conviction. In this sense, the arrows connecting Participatory 
Echo with Conversational Agency represent the transition from social reinforcement 
to independent action. Trust, situated at the interface of these two domains, becomes 
a mediating mechanism that links HBM and SIT. Studies such as Tetteh et al. (2022) 
and Alhaimer (2022) verify that trust functions as a simultaneous antecedent and 
consequence of health communication: it improves message receptivity while being 
enforced by regular, transparent engagement. The model captures this by showing 
that Relational Trust feeds into both Dialogic Resonance and Mobilised Cognition; 
trust accounts for the fact that dialogue is sustained (rather than switching off when a 
conversation is over) and also that someone might so wholeheartedly believe in what 
they have been convinced of through discourse that it affects their behaviour. 
The diagram in Figure 5.1 is also a conscious interpretation of the original model 
introduced in Figure 2.1. While the original design of health communication in Figure 
2.1 presents this process in a linear fashion linking government institutional messaging 
to public response, our revised model now encompasses circularity, reflexivity, and 
emotional resonance. The first developed model built on existing literature to posit a 
relationship between social media use and community engagement in public health. 
But empirical evidence revealed that the interactions are not unidirectional; they 
constitute a dialogic spectrum in which institutions and members of the public co-
construct meaning through ongoing digital feedback. Figure 5.1 thus reframes 
communication away from transmission and towards a dynamic ecology of resonance, 
participation, and co-production. As opposed to the static relationship between 
variables presented in Figure 2.1, this model is dynamic, showing how messages are 
constantly enacted within affective and social forces in Abu Dhabi’s digital 
assemblage. 
193 
 
Figure 5.1 is also different from that in FPSC, as it illustrates the complete cycle of 
communication 
(generation, 
dissemination, 
interpretation, 
and 
behavioural 
production), with ERIC at the top and Mobilised Cognition at the bottom of the matrix. 
This extension was based on two findings: firstly, respondents conceptualised social 
media as both a feedback mechanism and a trigger of behaviour. It directly answers 
the findings of Davies and Mann (2023) and Tian et al. (2023), who emphasise that 
digital interactivity serves as both an evaluative and motivational framework. The 
thematic evidence of ongoing message circulation, variation, and emotion at work 
underpins the need for a departure from the linear concepts in earlier models in this 
thesis to one more cyclical or recursive. In that sense, Figure 5.1 describes a 
theoretically motivated development based on empirical findings of social-media-
facilitated health communication in the culturally diverse context of Abu Dhabi. 
The arrows and relations between the elements in the framework show a two-
directional flow of influence: horizontal, from community values to individual cognition; 
and vertical, from discourse to action. The cross-links A through D from Communal 
Ethos to Perceptual Cartography represent the balancing act between cultural norms 
and personal interpretation that has bearing on issues of trust, credibility, and 
meaning-making. Digital Reverberations — the vertical series from (top) Digital 
Reverberations to (bottom) Mobilised Cognition — indicates how messages transform 
in symbolic form into bodily action. Each intersection (e.g., between Normative 
Adhesion and Compliance Drift) is a place where social influence intersects with health 
belief — flipping determination to behaviour. This recursive trajectory corresponds to 
the interpretive notion in which meaning and behaviour are co-constructed by 
interaction, congruent with the study’s epistemological position and method. 
They also provide insight into how the impact of the message is moderated by 
emotional tone and cultural proximity. The alternation between Benefit Horizon and 
Cognitive Dissonance in Discursive Pulse parallels the psychological tug-of-war 
people are engaged in when attempting to judge whether a given health 
communication is genuine. Sensory empathy and sense-making factors were reported 
as key factors in whether people adopted or discarded messages. These results 
support the work of Chaudhri et al. (2021) and Eghtesadi and Florea (2020), who 
found that the emotional resonance of messages and linguistic accessibility have a 
194 
 
significant impact on message credibility and memorability. Discursive Pulse, then, 
becomes the emotional heart-rate of communication — the way we can gauge the 
vitality of ideas. When trust, empathy, and cultural congruence are in tune, their 
heartbeat strengthens, drawing out behavioural activation; when they are out of sync, 
engagement diminishes. 
By incorporating these theoretical and empirically derived components, the model 
captures a dynamic communication ecology that encompasses more than the 
structural dimensions often found in health belief or social influence models. It locates 
the two theories in the mediated urban landscape of Abu Dhabi, negotiated by 
multiculturalism, technological entanglement, and institutional re-creation. Although 
the HBM provides understanding of individual perceptions, and SIT helps explain 
group-level influence, these concepts independently fail to reflect recursive feedback 
loops and co-creative processes that accompany interaction on social media. By 
incorporating these approaches within a dialogic and constructionist system, Figure 
5.1 overcomes this limitation and provides a more comprehensive paradigm in which 
beliefs, emotions, trust, and community identity are treated as interrelated variables 
that jointly contribute to communication outcomes. 
In conclusion, this model has been developed to explain the progression of Abu 
Dhabi’s public health communication environment — from knowledge delivery down a 
channel to the use of digital platforms for engagement. It illustrates the creation of a 
Third Space through social media, where sender and receiver are no longer separate 
entities; they share the communication process — a mutual adaptation process 
involving reciprocal learning, relationship, and trust within this meta-concept. The 
figure represents both a synthesis of theory and evidence that cognitive, social, and 
emotional factors in public health communication are intertwined elements of the same 
recursive system. It builds on the existing framework to translate abstract theoretical 
suppositions into a systematically grounded model that is both connected to participant 
experience and generative of new hypotheses, showing how, in an era of digitised life, 
engaging effectively with public health entails more than simply disseminating 
information; it requires resonance, reciprocity, and relational trust. In its overlapping, 
cyclical structure, Figure 5.1 is illustrative of the combination of beliefs, influence, and 
195 
 
discourse in Abu Dhabi’s social media environment that coalesce to create a living, 
breathing form of health communication. 
Based on the research objective — namely, to examine the potential and effectiveness 
of social media in enhancing communication and engagement — this approach offers 
an all-encompassing perspective to consider, appraise, and improve digitally mediated 
interactions in public health. At the heart of the framework is the arena of Dialogic 
Resonance, representing the dynamic dialogue between public health institutions and
