the community. This core is enveloped by three further layers — Symbolic Exchange, 
Discursive Pulse, and Participatory Echo — culminating in Conversational Agency, 
which describes how public discourse is shaped, circulated, refracted, and ultimately 
embodied by culture. These phases also illustrate how the passive receipt of 
information can be converted into action and behavioural modification, aligning with 
the goal of this study to explore whether community concerns and health outcomes 
are moulded by online engagement. 
Situated above this dialogic nucleus, Digital Reverberations represent the genesis and 
iterative amplification of public health messaging on social media. It encompasses two 
primary directional outputs: Narrative Feedback and Discourse Contours, which 
describe how public reaction and content sharing contribute to feedback loops that 
shape future communications. This highlights the network effect not only as a means 
of distribution but also as a mechanism for refining messages at scale in response to 
user engagement — demonstrating the effectiveness of social media as a 
communication vehicle. 
On the left side, Communal Ethos reflects the shared values and cultural narratives of 
Abu Dhabi’s diverse population. It links to Symbolic Alignment, Relational Trust, and 
Normative Adhesion, underscoring that community identity and trust are foundational 
for engagement. These dimensions illustrate the need for public health communication 
to intersect with local cultural beliefs in order to promote compliance and collective 
behaviour, consistent with the research objective of investigating community 
dynamics. 
On the right side, Perceptual Cartography represents the individual's internal compass 
for interpreting health information. This includes Benefit Horizon, Cognitive 
196 
 
Dissonance, Risk Intuition, and Trust Semiotics — cognitive tools individuals use to 
assess the credibility and personal relevance of health messages. These elements 
are key to understanding the effects of social media on both individual and community-
level health behaviours.  
At the bottom of the model, Mobilised Cognition is revealed as the end result — the 
shift from digital exposure to actual public health behaviours such as vaccination 
uptake, screening, or health activism. This includes forces such as Influence Valence, 
Action Catalysts, Compliance Drift, and the Self-Efficacy Loop, which bridge the gap 
between behavioural psychology and social influence theories. This figure therefore 
offers an inclusive and cyclical illustration of how digital interactions, community 
norms, social identity, and institutional communication feed into health behaviour and 
engagement. This finding aligns with the study’s objective: to demonstrate how social 
media functions as a communicative infrastructure and participatory arena through 
which Abu Dhabi’s health system is able to address public health issues, build trust, 
and enhance outcomes through effective digital participation.  
. 
 
 
 
197 
 
 
Figure 5-1 Digital Reverberations in Health Communication 
As Digital Reverberations in Health Communication  illustrates, the framework 
provides several lenses through which to evaluate social media as a communication 
and engagement mechanism in Abu Dhabi’s public health system. It weaves together 
a multitude of issues and theoretical considerations to analyse the fluid dynamics and 
dialogic exchanges of feeling, belief, trust, and collective action on social media. 
Grounded in emerging findings from our primary data and connected with established 
health theories such as the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory, this 
198 
 
model helps explain how digital communication spawns a participatory spread of 
health awareness, public opinion, and behaviour change. 
The concept of Digital Reverberations sits at the top of the framework, illustrating the 
feedback loop and cyclical processes of social media discourse. It is both the source 
and megaphone of public health stories. The streams of Narrative Response and 
Discourse Flow course outward, implying that once something is posted on social 
media, it enters public space and reacts to — and is reacted upon by — the public. 
This aligns with the cues to action in HBM, which refer to external stimuli that prompt 
health behaviours. Social media, in this context, becomes a perpetual conduit for cued 
information and affective messaging. 
The vertical central spine of the framework consists of three encased domains — 
Symbolic Exchange, Discursive Pulse, and Participatory Echo — which lead toward 
and envelop Conversational Agency, while the overarching notion of Dialogic 
Resonance provides the wider context. This defines the essential real-time, dual-
exchange loop of communication between society and public health, mirroring the 
construction of both mainstream and alternative narratives. It closely reflects perceived 
benefits and self-efficacy in HBM, as people interact with and use information they 
deem both beneficial and actionable. 
Symbolic Exchange, located at the apex of the dialogical funnel, refers to the mediated 
exchange of meaning and values. This domain highlights Sentiment Flux and Echo 
Governance — the ways in which emotional tones evolve and messages persist 
across networks. These concepts mirror internalisation and identification in Social 
Influence Theory, which suggest that people assimilate messages when they align 
with personal values or group norms. From this emblematic convergence emerges the 
concept of Relational Trust — an index at the junction of Communal Ethos and Dialogic 
Resonance. 
5.1.1 
Discursive Pulse displays the rhythm and life of public discourse 
The layer below this, Discursive Pulse, displays the rhythm and life of public discourse. 
It is the beat of the community’s interpretive interaction with health stories. Benefit 
Horizon and Cognitive Dissonance impact the pulse, reflecting the struggle between 
199 
 
the prospect of benefits and the internal conflict that can result from conflicting beliefs. 
This dimension closely mirrors perceived severity and perceived barriers in HBM, in 
that inconsistent information — or content that does not align with one’s beliefs — can 
deter behaviour change. 
Further themes such as Participatory Echo also surface here, referring to messages 
of possibility and potential that are not simply received but re-articulated and spread 
through community action. Normative Adhesion links to compliance in Social Influence 
Theory. This suggests that, in many cases, participation in the festivity is motivated 
either by a desire for social aggregation or due to community expectation. Similarly, 
Community Identity, grounded in Communal Ethos, relates to identification, where 
users model and mimic the behaviours of the perceived in-group. 
At the bottom of the funnel is Conversational Agency — a personification of 
empowerment through conversation. This reflects the capacity for people to direct the 
conversation, not just absorb it. This aligns with HBM as a form of self-efficacy — the 
belief in one’s ability to effect change — and with the final stage of internalisation in 
Social Influence Theory, where behaviour is fully integrated into the self and aligned 
with a shared norm. At a more precise level, Mobilized Cognition represents the 
outcome phase where thought becomes action. It is facilitated by the convergence of 
Influence Valence and Action Catalysts, which emerge through the combination of 
personal perceptions (HBM) and social demands (Social Influence Theory). Mobilized 
Cognition represents the dawning of action, as trust, belief, identity, and engagement 
combine to generate public health action — such as vaccine uptake, adoption of 
preventive behaviours, or advocacy for healthcare policy. 
5.1.2 
Communal Ethos and Perceptual Cartography 
Communal Ethos and Perceptual Cartography are the theoretical “edges” against 
which the side panels provide resistance. The former refers to the values, beliefs, and 
moral anchors of the community, while the latter reflects the cognitive maps that 
individuals use to navigate risk, trust, and belief in the credibility of information. These 
two expansive domains serve as bookends for the entire enterprise, situating online 
interaction within both a collective process of meaning-making (ethos) and an 
individual process of sense-making (perceptual cartography). 
200 
 
Risk Intuition and Trust Semiotics, located within Perceptual Cartography, are 
especially relevant to HBM’s perceived susceptibility and the role of source credibility, 
respectively, as they mediate individuals’ interpretations of messages concerning their 
personal health risks and institutional trust. 
This complex interplay is further emphasised through the horizontal axes of the 
framework. On the left, the relationship between Symbolic Alignment, Normative 
Adhesion, and Compliance Drift illustrates a trajectory from passive value agreement 
to behavioural enactment. On the right, the path from Benefit Horizon through Risk 
Intuition to Self-Efficacy Loop indicates that individual health action is influenced by 
awareness of benefit, interpretation of risk, and the perceived ability to act. 
The cyclical design of this model — fuelled by digital echoes — suggests that 
communication end products (e.g., health behaviour change or public engagement) 
are not final outcomes but rather re-enter the ecosystem as fresh storytelling inputs. 
This recursive interplay underscores the dynamism of the social media health 
communication space, where every public response informs future strategies and 
continually reshapes the contours of discourse. 
The framework positions social media not as a mere broadcast medium but as a 
dialogic space — a site of symbolic exchange and cognitive mobilisation. It provides 
an example of how Abu Dhabi’s public health system can harness digital engagement 
dynamics to build trust, promote behavioural change, and improve health outcomes. 
By combining the Health Belief Model (HBM) with Social Influence Theory (SIT), it links 
individual-level 
psychological 
determinants 
with 
broader 
sociocultural 
and 
communicative processes, offering a holistic framework for assessing and guiding 
social media’s role in public health engagement. 
Underlying digital-era health communication is the interplay between individual 
cognition, social environment, and technological intervention. Abu Dhabi’s public 
health system, marked by high levels of digital penetration and demographic diversity, 
presents a unique context in which to understand how individual and collective 
attitudes intersect through social media to shape engagement, trust, and health 
outcomes. This framework — evolved through the integration of HBM and SIT — offers 
201 
 
a comprehensive, multilayered model for analysing the dynamic interplay of health 
communication, public participation, and behaviour change in the emirate. 
According to the Health Belief Model (HBM), individual health behaviour is typically 
determined by a combination of perceived susceptibility, severity, benefits, barriers, 
cues to action, and self-efficacy (Ellithorpe et al., 2022; Al-Metwali et al., 2021). These 
factors can determine the extent to which individuals undertake health-related 
behaviours. In the context of Abu Dhabi, the circulation of health messaging via 
platforms such as Twitter, Instagram, and government-endorsed WhatsApp alerts 
engages these perceptual dimensions in quite distinct ways. For example, during the 
COVID-19 pandemic, the public’s willingness to be vaccinated or comply with 
quarantine measures was largely shaped by perceived vulnerability (susceptibility), 
perceived severity of illness, and belief in the effectiveness of interventions (vaccines) 
(Vatcharavongvan et al., 2023; Youssef et al., 2022). 
As such, social media can be said to function as a trigger for behaviour and a tool to 
reinforce self-efficacy using visual stories, testimonials, infographics, and influencer 
messaging (Mao et al., 2023). Aspects such as the credibility of information sources, 
the clarity of content, as well as the alignment of social media with personal beliefs 
and cultural norms, are key factors in public receptivity (Tian et al., 2023; Odunsi & 
Farris, 2023). For Abu Dhabi’s diverse population, this points to the need for culturally 
tailored messages to promote behavioural change. This also addresses common 
criticisms of the HBM, particularly its limited cultural sensitivity and emotional range 
(Alejandro et al., 2022; Nooriani et al., 2019). 
On one hand, perceptions of health threats and benefits (HBM) lead to perceptions of 
social norms and influence (SIT), forming the circular structure proposed in this study. 
Interactive communication flows between these two domains, with social media acting 
as both modulator and amplifier. It provides both a content context and a social 
context, where ongoing acts of acknowledgement — likes, shares, comments, 
endorsements — routinely reinforce and reshape perceived realities. During times of 
uncertainty, individuals often turn to online groups for validation and guidance as a 
form of informational social influence. Such behaviours impact how messages from 
institutions such as Abu Dhabi’s Ministry of Health are heard, interpreted, and acted 
202 
 
upon. These messages are filtered through both belief systems and social influence 
processes. 
5.1.3 
Underpinning of Social Influence Theory 
 
Social Influence Theory, first established by Kelman (1958), extends this model by 
focusing on compliance, identification, and internalization as key behavioural 
mechanisms. These mechanisms can be strategically utilised by health authorities in 
the emirate of Abu Dhabi. For instance, health promotion campaigns that employ 
respected local figures or community members as spokespersons foster identification 
and compliance among target audiences . When value-based messages are delivered 
consistently, people may come to see themselves as the kind of person for whom 
taking preventive health actions, such as vaccination or regular screening, feels more 
authentic and meaningful. This perspective becomes even more relevant when 
considering the various types of social influence: normative influence, driven by the 
need for social acceptance, and informational influence, driven by the desire to make 
correct decisions (Liu et al., 2020). In Abu Dhabi, normative influence is reflected in 
collective behaviours such as participation in community vaccination campaigns, while 
informational influence is evident in expert-led live question and answer sessions and 
the circulation of fact-checked infographics about symptoms and prevention (Naeem 
and Ozuem, 2021). These forms of influence reinforce each other. Interaction with 
trusted content in the public sphere promotes greater uptake of health-promotive 
actions, contributing to improved health outcomes at the population level. 
Community engagement is also a key feature of this iterative framework for 
comprehensive CPG development. Social media shares news and allows for 
feedback, expression of concern, and peer-to-peer discussion. Hita et al. (2023) 
examined media cues to action during the COVID-19 pandemic and demonstrated 
how content shared on social media platforms can encourage behaviours such as 
panic purchasing or social distancing. In Abu Dhabi, tracking these trends could 
enable public health officials to identify misinformation early in the process before 
addressing community concerns and altering communication strategies. This is where 
the circular nature of the framework is crucial. Engagement through social media 
contributes to a more nuanced construction of health perceptions and social influence 
203 
 
dynamics, which in turn shape individual behaviours and open up further 
communication needs. This feedback loop means that effective public health 
communication is a continuous and adaptive process. For instance, if a social media 
campaign reveals high levels of perceived barriers, such as fear of side effects, health 
officials might respond by sharing the stories of individuals who chose to be vaccinated 
by debunking rumours or myths (Odunsi and Farris, 2023; Davies and Mann, 2023), 
or by partnering with medical influencers who can increase self-efficacy. 
Role 
of 
trust 
as 
a 
mediating 
variable 
between 
HBM 
and 
SIT 
The model acknowledges the role of trust as a mediating variable between the Health 
Belief Model and Social Influence Theory in Abu Dhabi. Confidence in health 
institutions, bolstered by regular and culturally relevant communication, enhances the 
perception of benefits and strengthens social norms (Tetteh et al., 2022). People who 
have confidence in their health authorities are more likely to follow guidelines, share 
accurate information, and encourage others to do the same. Studies have shown this 
consistently (Alhaimer, 2022). In this way, trust is not only an outcome but also a 
condition, situated at the midpoint of the conceptual circle. 
The model also considers the emotional and cultural dimensions of behaviour, 
components frequently criticised as lacking in the HBM. In rapidly developing Abu 
Dhabi, where the population is diverse, interpretations of health and illness are 
refracted through various cultural lenses. Social media provides a valuable platform 
for customising information to incentivise behaviour change across languages, cultural 
values, and demographic segments. Campaigns that are mindful of local values, 
include familiar figures, and use locally relevant metaphors have the potential to 
change not only attitudes but also behaviours (Nooriani et al., 2019; Youssef et al., 
2022). 
Crucially, the model suggests that the impact of social media on health communication 
relies on a level of harmony between personal health beliefs and social influence. 
Communication strategies should target both, appealing to cognitions, such as 
perceptions of health risks and benefits, as described by the Health Belief Model, as 
well as social norms, emotional resonance, and community identity, as described by 
Social Influence Theory. 
204 
 
The framework presents several considerations for the public health system in Abu 
Dhabi. First, content must specifically address relevant Health Belief Model constructs, 
such as decreasing perceived barriers or increasing cues to action. Social campaigns 
can also leverage influencers and local figures to reinforce compliance, identification, 
and internalisation. Message content should incorporate social proof and normative 
references, framing protective behaviour as beneficial, socially expected, and 
endorsed. In addition, platforms should facilitate feedback loops to help health 
authorities capture emerging sentiment, correct misinformation, and adapt strategies 
in real time. 
The circular confluence of the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory in this 
conceptual framework provides a strong and dynamic vantage point from which to 
study and improve social media’s role in Abu Dhabi’s health communication and 
engagement. The model is not linear but recursive, recognising that perceptions of 
health, social factors, and communication behaviours continuously evolve in relation 
to one another. This comprehensive model offers policymakers, public health 
professionals, and communicators a theory-driven framework for creating, testing, and 
refining health promotion strategies that are effective, inclusive, and culturally relevant 
in an era of pervasive digital media use. 
5.1.4 Integration of Social Media Communication, Corporate Social 
Responsibility (CSR) 
 
This thesis has implications for understanding how social media communication, 
community engagement, and public health branding are intertwined within Abu Dhabi’s 
healthcare sector. For ease of analysis, a multidirectional model is proposed that 
includes social media as an endorser, social media as a facilitator, and social media 
as a disseminator. This model is cyclical and integrated, marking a significant 
departure from more traditional top-down public health communication models by 
giving greater voice and agency to the patient. 
Interactive internet communication via social media is the central feature of the model 
and acts as the main driver for community involvement and public health branding. 
Community engagement is framed as a dynamic and interactive process through 
205 
 
which patients and the public communicate and express ideas and insights via social 
media while also responding to suggestions and comments with tangible implications 
for practice. 
In contrast, public health branding emerges through enduring and meaningful digital 
exchanges, helping to build reputation and trust in the health services offered in Abu 
Dhabi. This interrelated view is also supported by the literature, which documents the 
historical evolution of social media in health from a source of information in the early 
two thousands to a platform for engagement and branding today (Belt et al., 2013). 
This evolution is essential to understanding the significance of the conceptual 
framework in the present day. As Chaudhri et al. (2021) and Gesser-Edelsburg (2021) 
suggest, the branding of health services through digital engagement strategies is 
critical to creating a robust and trusted public health system, especially in multiethnic 
urban locales such as Abu Dhabi. 
The addition of the concept of CSR into a discussion of social media and public health 
communication must be explicitly justified in order for it to align with the theoretical 
underpinnings of the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory that drive this 
study. Within this context, CSR is seen not simply as a corporate or marketing 
construction but as a communicative strategy that serves to uphold institutional 
legitimacy, social trust, and collective identity. These concerns are central to public 
health engagement in Abu Dhabi. The allusion to CSR is therefore conceptually 
warranted provided it is conceived of as a tool of social influence and relational trust 
among public institutions and citizens. 
Within Abu Dhabi’s health ecosystem, CSR-based communication includes 
government-driven programs, healthcare collaborations, and corporate sanctioned 
awareness efforts that work toward improving community health. Such initiatives can 
also help provide health information, but more broadly they serve as a way to promote 
institutional social responsibility through the use of social media. This institutional 
image in turn feeds public trust in the efficiency, good intentions, and ethical orientation 
of the organisations concerned. Trust, as integrated in this work, represents the 
mediating construct between individual beliefs described by the Health Belief Model 
and collective behaviour described by Social Influence Theory. CSR that 
communicates health messages contributes to this trust because such information 
206 
 
signals responsibility, empathy, and public commitment from organisations. These 
qualities facilitate credibility, message acceptance, identification, and conformity within 
the community (Injodey et al., 2019). 
Theoretically, CSR fits with Social Influence Theory in the mechanisms of compliance, 
identification, and internalisation. When a health organisation, whether public or 
private, undertakes CSR activities such as funding vaccination drives, supporting 
wellness campaigns, or backing health education initiatives, it does more than display 
moral responsibility or community integration. These efforts are interpreted by 
audiences as genuine acts of concern and public solidarity. This perception drives 
normative influence, causing people to adopt behaviours that align with health actions 
endorsed by society. It also fosters identification, as community members see 
themselves reflected in shared moral values. Repeated exposure to socially 
responsible digital communication can lead to internalisation over time, through which 
health promoting behaviours become part of an individual’s personal or cultural 
identity. CSR therefore constitutes a permissive environment in which trust is both 
created and sustained, bolstering the upward spiral between Communal Ethos and 
Perceptual Cartography that is represented in Figure 5.1. 
Theories of planned behaviour highlight that CSR also has a cognitive component that 
resembles factors found in constructs from the Health Belief Model. Humanising 
institutions and increasing perceived benefits of participation reduce perceived 
barriers. Participants are more likely to perceive messages from health workers as 
prosocial and trustworthy when health organisations demonstrate social responsibility, 
resulting in greater willingness to act on the advice. This is consistent with Ellithorpe 
et al. (2022) and Youssef et al. (2022), who found that credibility and trust were critical 
in mediating the pathway from awareness to behaviour change. Accordingly, CSR-
based communication operates as a cue to action, a signal that activates both belief 
and behaviour through affective trust and moral reciprocity. 
Given these theoretical overlaps, CSR is not an additional factor but a latent aspect 
within the construct of social influence and public trust in the model. Its legitimacy is 
evidenced by its broad socioethical focus, which accompanies health communication 
in Abu Dhabi and considers institutional reputations and civic responsibilities as 
interconnected components of public engagement. Rather than being removed, a 
207 
 
reference to CSR should be preserved but can be reformulated as a trust-building 
mechanism linking organisational ethics with influence over behaviour. In this distilled 
sense, CSR represents the ethical reach of social power, describing how institutional 
benevolence reconfigures into social legitimacy and lived health citizenship. 
The “Digital Reverberations in Health Communication” model offers a theoretical 
reconceptualisation of public health institutions and their relationship to the 
communities they aim to serve through digital media. The integrated model, developed 
to combine the core constructs of the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory, 
provides a comprehensive theoretical account of the psychological and social drivers 
of public health behaviours in the digital context. It therefore positions social media as 
both a communication tool and a transformative ecosystem in which information, 
identity, and influence converge to shape behaviour and perception. 
Applied to a specific population such as the digitally connected and multicultural 
population of Abu Dhabi, the customisable nature of the framework offers both an 
effective model and a map for strategic health communications in a specific time and 
context. It highlights the necessity for dialogic communication, community 
involvement, and culturally relevant messaging to drive successful health promotion 
and institutional trust. With its focus on evidence-based interventions and real-time 
responsiveness, the model underscores the importance of feedback loops that adapt 
messaging based on public sentiment and behavioural outcomes. 
In light of challenges ranging from misinformation to the erosion of public trust in health 
systems globally, the application of this framework in Abu Dhabi serves as a valuable 
example and major research finding. This chapter presents the foundation both as a 
set of guiding principles and as an operational blueprint for delivering inclusive, trust-
based, and impact-oriented health communication in the age of digital disruption. 
5.2 SUMMARY  
Chapter 5 offers a first-of-its-kind theoretical and empirical overview submission on 
how social media interaction constructs, reforms, and sustains public health 
communication in Abu Dhabi. We present the Digital Reverberations in Health 
Communication framework, which reimagines communication as a perpetual and 
208 
 
iterative process of co-creation of meaning between public institutions and 
communities through sustained digital interaction. The chapter frames social media 
not as an adjunctive artifact, but part of a communicative ecology where trust, identity, 
affect and behaviour meet. Through the lens of health belief model and social 
influence theory, this chapter demonstrates how health communication is a two-way 
street- both psychological and sociocultural—where perception-individual difference 
as well as norms-collective identity serve to determine public health in real time. 
The model is based in the constructed thematic analysis found in Chapter 4. It is the 
interpretive nexus between Dialogic Resonance, that signifies lived in exchanges 
where public voices and institutional responses anthropomorphically move from 
platform to platform. Around this core, the model traces how meaning is generated 
through Symbolic Exchange, Discursive Pulse and Participatory Echo — the 
emotional, linguistic and behavioural flows that typify Abu Dhabi’s digital health 
presence. These layers demonstrate how this communication travels from when it 
gets shared, to its interpretation, during resharing, through emotional processing and 
finally into behaviour. The cyclic nature of the model is in stark contrast to older linear 
communication theory, which suggests that public reaction is an end point – when it’s 
actually a new beginning that will inform future messaging. 
The chapter contributes to the broader understanding of how people make sense of 
health messages by Perceptual Cartography -the way in which individuals map 
informational cues, such as: risk perception; trust markers; cultural resonance; and 
personal relevance. At the same time, the concept of Communal Ethos indicates that 
cultural identity, collective beliefs and moral expectations imply a normative condition 
which in turn directs communication. The horizontal and vertical dimensions of the 
model express the ongoing process of negotiation between personal beliefs about 
health, on one side, and influences from others surrounding one, on the other; it 
illustrates that individual cognitive (mental) capacities and social forms of organization 
are inextricable in digital engagement. 
Mobilised Cognition at the bottom of model represents the behavioural output where 
how people engage with digital media ends up as real-world behaviour. These factors 
include the impact of emotional narratives, the motivational value of prompts, the role 
of perceptible norms and visible targets and the potency of self-efficacy when people 
209 
 
become more confident in their ability to accept health behaviours. The chapter also 
demonstrates that trust is serving as a mediating factor connecting cognitive appraisal 
and social influence. Trust is also enhanced when the institutions shows cultural 
understanding and emotional warmth, which directly increases perceived benefits 
and social conformity. 
The chapter finishes by showing how social media communication and CSR for trust 
building of CSG for forms participatory engagement to make up a relational culturally 
embedded and adaptive public health communication system. The model provides 
for policy makers and health communicators an evidence-based and contextually 
relevant framework to inform strategic approaches that are inclusive, dialogic, and 
culturally anchored. In this respect, Chapter 5 provides a theoretical base from which 
to begin to consider the role of digital ecosystems in strengthening public trust, 
enabling reciprocal social and behaviour change & lending greater effectiveness to 
present day public health activities. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
210 
 
6 CHAPTER 6: RECOMMENDATIONS AND CONCLUSION  
6.1 INTRODUCTION  
This study aimed to examine the function and impact of social media as a platform for 
communication and community engagement in the public health system of Abu Dhabi. 
The results suggest a varied but hopeful environment in which digital interaction 
intertwines with public health outcomes. The analytical lens of “Digital Reverberations 
in Health Communication,” generated in this study, explains how digital 
communication operates not as a unidirectional transmission but as a reciprocal 
process, both relying on and continually shaping societal understandings, practices, 
and organisational dynamics. In Abu Dhabi, which is a digitally sophisticated and 
demographically heterogeneous environment, social media has emerged as an active 
forum for both dialogue and participation. This setting allows health institutions to 
engage with the public, build trust, and motivate health-related behavioural change in 
ways that are immediate, inclusive, and more dynamic than those available through 
more traditional modalities. 
The framework is built around the notion of dialogic resonance between health 
organisations and the public, linking communicative actions in both directions. 
Thematically, it is organised around a surrounding ring of constructs, such as symbolic 
exchange, discursive pulse, and participatory echo, which account for how meaning, 
affect, and engagement circulate and transform within digital ecosystems. These 
constructs, although theoretical, are rooted in the specific features of Abu Dhabi’s 
health communication context. For example, during public health emergencies like the 
COVID-19 pandemic, platforms such as Twitter, Instagram, and WhatsApp played a 
key role in spreading cues to action. They shaped risk perceptions and established 
social norms, and they found resonance through storytelling, testimonials, and 
culturally appropriate content. This created momentum towards behavioural intention 
and societal mobilisation. 
This study aimed to examine the use of social media as an instrument of interactivity, 
community involvement, and trust-building in the Abu Dhabi public health system. The 
study was conducted qualitatively and interpretatively, using not only interviews with 
professional and community actors but also focusing on how dialogic communication 
211 
 
affects public understanding, engagement, and health-related behaviour. The results 
highlight the extent to which communication between public health institutions and 
citizens has been revolutionised by digital platforms, especially Twitter, Instagram, and 
WhatsApp. Across these platforms, not only one-way transmissions but reciprocal, 
participatory, and affective communications are forged that reframe the organisation-
community relationship. 
The study found that conducive health communication in Abu Dhabi rests on three 
intersecting factors: cultural saliency, institutional legitimacy, and dialogical 
adaptability. Culturally relevant messages promote inclusivity and relevance, credible 
communication builds trust, and a dialogic approach to interaction through feedback 
loops fosters responsiveness to public opinion. Combined, these factors turn virtual 
engagement into a power that changes behaviour and delivers tangible results such 
as awareness-raising, health guidance compliance, or institutional reputation 
enhancement. 
The constructed theoretical mechanism of this process, Digital Reverberations in 
Health Communication, depicts these phenomena as a mutually serving and circular 
model. It is evidence that social media operates as a communication structure, a trust-
creating device, and a space for participation. This finding challenges conventional 
linear models of public health messaging and situates Abu Dhabi as a flagship for 
digitally enabled governance aimed at promoting citizen participation and community 
welfare. 
6.2 CONTRIBUTION  
6.2.1 
Theoretical contribution 
Perhaps the essential theoretical contribution of this project is the development of a 
model, Digital Reverberations in Health Communication (see Figure 5.1). This is a 
model that integrates the Health Belief Model and Social Influence Theory, through an 
empirically based synthesis built upon themes from actual experiences. It envisions 
digital health communication as a recursive ecology rather than a linear information 
cascade. By integrating the Health Belief Model’s focus on individual cognition and 
perceived benefits with the Social Influence Theory’s emphasis on social identification 
212 
 
and normative pressure, the model offers a hybrid explanatory platform that can 
explain both psychological and social determinants of digital engagement. 
This integration is important because, until the current study, the Health Belief Model 
and Social Influence Theory had been infrequently integrated in a digital 
communication environment, especially within a multicultural Middle Eastern public 
health setting. What makes this contribution unique is that it shows that when these 
theories are combined, they provide an explanation of how people are motivated to 
act upon health messages and how digital environments support action through trust, 
emotion, and social validation. Figure 5.1 presents an image of this process in the 
form of a living, dialogic cycle in four domains: Dialogic Resonance, Communal Ethos, 
Perceptual Cartography, and Mobilised Cognition. 
Each domain is a layer of meaning that was found in the empirical data. Dialogic 
Resonance refers to the essence of mutual exchange in which institutions and citizens 
co-author stories through dialectic interaction. Communal Ethos offers a moral and 
cultural account of such interactions by explaining how trust and identity are intensified 
through common values and local forms of symbolism in Abu Dhabi’s society. 
Perceptual Cartography theorises the interpretive practices by which people assess 
risk, normative legitimacy, and benefit, mediated through both rational cognitive 
calculation and emotional heuristics. Mobilised Cognition brings these interpretive 
actions to the level of behaviour, showing how digital engagement moves awareness 
into actionable health practices. 
The originality of the framework also lies in its structural invention. By shifting from a 
linear depiction to a circular, self-referential loop in Figure 5.1, the constant feedback 
effects of communication in digital spaces are represented. Messages do not have an 
end but become the beginning of other messages; in this perspective, audience 
feedback becomes part of message production. This recursive motion is a more 
accurate representation of the nature of social media communication, in which 
institutional and public discourse co-evolve. In doing so, the model provides a 
theoretical lexicon to articulate feedback-based, emotion-modulated communication 
in contemporary networked societies. 
213 
 
The theoretical development of Dialogic Resonance also advances the Health Belief 
Model and Social Influence Theory. Traditional health models tend to conceptualise 
communication as persuasion, while Dialogic Resonance reconceives it as a 
collaborative act based on emotional attunement and symbolic exchange. It highlights 
that resonance, rather than reach or frequency, is the key to understanding how health 
communication works in the digital era. This theoretical step addresses a gap in the 
literature that has long failed to consider affective and participatory communication 
within behavioural theories. 
Perceptual Cartography also introduces a new idea by mapping how people navigate 
the digital health space. It transcends cognitive rationality by integrating trust, 
emotions, and cultural interpretation as coordinates that influence decisions. The 
framework contributes to health communication theory by noting that people do not 
receive messages in the same way; they build meaning through social validation and 
personal salience. For a city like Abu Dhabi, where language, faith, and identity 
intersect, this insight has significant explanatory power. 
The third theoretical novelty, Mobilised Cognition, explains how behaviour does not 
arise from single exposures but from repeated, emotionally charged interactions. It 
integrates the self-efficacy concept from the Health Belief Model with the 
internalisation mechanism from Social Influence Theory, suggesting that behavioural 
adoption is driven less by rational deliberation than by sustained dialogic 
reinforcement. The model extends the conventional notion of behaviour change by 
reframing it as a process shaped by culturally embedded communicative momentum. 
In short, Figure 5.1 is the most comprehensive conceptual framework of the thesis, 
integrating cognitive, social, and emotional processes into one dynamic system. It is 
novel because it transforms static behavioural constructs into a responsive, interactive 
framework for health communication in a digitally networked and culturally plural 
society. 
 
 
214 
 
6.3 CONTRIBUTION TO PRACTICE  
This study offers public health managers a responsive communication model that 
prioritises two-way, real-time interaction with the wider community. By applying the 
Digital Reverberations framework, health communication in Abu Dhabi can move 
beyond a traditional broadcast approach to an updated approach that listens, reacts, 
and adapts to public sentiment. Such a move enables a more agile response to 
misinformation, clearer policy messaging, and the cultivation of improved public trust. 
Managers can apply the model to design  communication strategies that are 
responsive to feedback and which promote a more transparent, trust-based dialogue 
aligned with both institutional aims and community expectations. 
Second, the study offers practical insights into how institutional trust might be better 
enhanced through socially engaged media. It demonstrates how public health 
institutions can use culturally resonant storytelling, influencer collaboration, and peer-
to-peer conversation to create stronger relational trust. To achieve this, 
communication teams should be trained in sentiment analysis and trust-building 
techniques 
across 
diverse 
demographics. 
By 
fostering 
relationship-based 
communication that reflects community values, public health managers can help make 
institutions appear more trustworthy. In turn, they can promote behaviours that are 
more aligned with health guidance. 
Third, the research highlights the potential of digital engagement to co-create health 
systems. Through concepts such as Participatory Echo and Conversational Agency, 
the study outlines how digital platforms can support citizen involvement in policy and 
service design. Managers might go on to use tools like online consultations, polls, and 
open feedback channels to gather public perspectives and incorporate these into 
decision-making. This participatory process supports patient-centred care and fosters 
shared ownership in terms of tackling public health issues. 
Fourth, the framework points to a need for targeted interventions supported by data-
driven strategies. Insights from Perceptual Cartography can enable managers to 
interpret behavioural patterns, emerging community concerns, and perceptual shifts. 
In addition, tools such as social listening and analytics dashboards can help tailor 
content to specific groups based on age, ethnicity, or risk perception,  thereby  
rendering communication more relevant and effective. In diverse contexts like Abu 
Dhabi, such precision is key to avoiding the pitfalls of one-size-fits-all messaging. 
215 
 
The model stresses that long-term behavioural change depends on trust-building and 
value congruence. The concept of Mobilised Cognition implies that campaigns should  
both encourage immediate uptake and also embed lasting public health habits. This 
requires consistent messaging, visible positive outcomes, and the reinforcement of 
collective norms. Public health managers should incorporate behavioural insights into 
communication planning. They should set objectives that go beyond clicks or 
impressions toward sustained behavioural impact to demonstrate multi-generational 
engagement that supports long-term public health outcomes.  
6.4 LIMITATIONS AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS  
6.4.1 
Limitations 
The most obvious limitation of this study is the fact that it has taken place in a specific 
context. Theoretical and empirical links are made with the sociocultural and 
institutional context of Abu Dhabi, which is a linguistically diverse, high digital literacy 
ecosystem with a centralised governance structure. These particular characteristics 
are informative but do confine the transferability of the model to other political 
frameworks where communication infrastructure, cultural norms, and public 
healthcare systems are significantly divergent. For example, the assumptions of this 
research, such as that a state has strong capacity and well-developed digital 
infrastructure or a multilingual population, may not be applicable to less centralised or 
relatively underdeveloped technology settings. Accordingly, the Digital Reverberations 
in Health Communication model should be considered as contextually situated rather 
than universally generalisable, and its applicability more generally must be empirically 
evaluated. 
Another limitation concerns the time-related design of the study. This is a cross-
sectional qualitative study; it provides a “snapshot” of the communication dynamics 
and not a “moving picture” over time. Public engagement, trust building, and digital 
participation are dynamic activities that take place in response to changing policies, 
crises, and cultural accommodation. The lack of longitudinal data limits the extent to 
which we can comment on how attitudes and behaviours change over time or in 
response to repeated institutional communication. Although the theory implies cycles, 
the empirical specification does not take cyclicality over time into account. Such a 
temporal constraint implies that while the model offers interpretive depth, it cannot 
216 
 
claim predictive validity with respect to how trust will evolve in the long term, nor how 
behavioural adaptation will unfold. 
Moreover, the research is limited by the nature of its qualitative data size and scope. 
Although sufficient for interpretive depth, the number of interview participants is not 
representative of the complete demographic heterogeneity of the population in Abu 
Dhabi. Some groups might be under-represented, such as transient workers, elderly 
residents, or those with low digital literacy. The use of purposive sampling, while 
logical in a qualitative framework, diminishes the statistical generalisability of this 
investigation. This methodological design valued meaning and interpretation over 
coverage and measurement, but it may bring limitations in terms of demographic 
representation and generalisability. 
Another limitation is that the proposed model has been conceptually, not empirically, 
validated. The model combines elements of the Health Belief Model and Social 
Influence Theory as thematic constructs, yet no hypotheses were tested through 
quantitative analysis. Although such a synthesis provides strong theoretical 
coherence, it does not provide data to show that these constructs mediate the 
processes of trust building, engagement, and behavioural mobilisation. Concepts such 
as Dialogic Resonance, Perceptual Cartography, and Mobilised Cognition are 
theoretically strong but are interpretative categories rather than statistically verified 
variables. The absence of numerical validation leaves the nature and direction of these 
associations, as well as their underlying pathways, open to additional empirical 
scrutiny. 
While the interpretivist nature of the study enables deep contextual understanding, it 
does compromise objectivity through researcher interpretation. Reflexivity was 
established at each stage of the analysis; however, qualitative thematic analysis 
requires interpretive judgment. Therefore, these findings are credible and theoretically 
grounded, but they might be interpretatively situated due to the researcher’s 
positioning, cultural frame of reference, or methodological orientation. It is not the 
intention that such limitations be interpreted as failings, but rather as inherent aspects 
of a qualitative exploration of meaning and interactions within a particular social 
networked environment. 
 
217 
 
6.4.2 
 Future research direction 
Future research needs to further develop, test, and elaborate the Digital 
Reverberations in Health Communication model beyond the qualitative nature of its 
current construction. A promising direction consists of testing the framework’s main 
constructs with mixed-method or quantitative designs. In order to measure trust 
formation, structural equation modelling (SEM), accompanied by social media 
analytics and surveys, could be employed for the quantification of variables such as 
interactivity, emotional response, and behavioural intention. This would enable 
researchers to measure the degree and direction of relationships between Symbolic 
Exchange, Discursive Pulse, Relational Trust, and Mobilised Cognition. This would 
empirically underpin the causal logic of the model and improve its predictive utility in 
varied public health contexts. 
Another critical direction is the study of longitudinal research. While communication 
and digital trust are dynamic phenomena whose value may change over time, 
longitudinal or cohort studies could explore the long-term effects of interactive 
communication during extended health campaigns or policy changes. Monitoring shifts 
in sentiment, engagement, and compliance at different public health intervention 
stages would illuminate how continued dialogic communication fosters enduring 
behavioural and attitudinal change. Longitudinal data would also allow testing of the 
cyclic nature of the model, for example, whether repeated exchanges build trust and 
engagement over time or reach a plateau. 
Comparative work across cultural and institutional contexts is also important. It would 
be valuable to apply the framework to other areas, particularly those with less digital 
capability and more decentralised governance, and test its transferability as well as 
understand how context specific it is. Studies in low-income settings, federated health 
systems, or culturally different societies might consider testing whether constructs 
such as Communal Ethos and Dialogic Resonance operate comparably across 
different sociopolitical contexts. Such comparisons would sharpen the framework for 
transnational applicability and protect its sensitivity to the local. 
Another promising avenue is the integration of automated and qualitative analysis. The 
framework’s domains may be explored using digital trace data, such as social media 
feeds, sentiment mapping, and discourse analysis, in order to illuminate how specific 
engagement patterns and emotional expressions unfold over time. Combining big data 
218 
 
with qualitative interpretation in mixed-method research would not only validate the 
constructs but also provide useful action-guiding information to policy-makers and 
communication experts interested in optimising public engagement. 
Future work might also consider how machinic mediation and practices of algorithmic 
accountability shape dialogic resonance and trust building. As AI increasingly curates 
and moderates digital content, this shapes how citizens see and interpret public health 
information. Exploring the interrelationship between human agency and algorithmic 
systems in public health communication may help to further the theoretical scope of 
the model, as well as connect it with recent trends in digital sociology and media ethics. 
The model could also be applied to health education and risk communication contexts 
beyond Abu Dhabi to improve its disciplinary applicability. Demonstrating that the 
Digital Reverberations in Health Communication framework functions in a mental 
health promotion context, and also for immunisation campaigns or chronic disease 
management, would illustrate its relevance across different public health areas. Trial 
investigators might explore whether dialogic and culturally situated models of 
communication lead to engagement outcomes as well as more easily quantifiable 
health-related effects, such as adherence to preventive behaviours or uptake of 
screening services. 
The cyclical nature of the model lends itself to further research on feedback within 
adaptive governance. Further examination of how organisations learn from and 
respond to public discourse could help to elucidate the mechanisms through which 
communication influences policy change. Combining insights from governance 
studies, behavioural science, and digital anthropology might help develop a 
complementary view of how participatory communication helps to turn health systems 
into reflexive learning organisations. 
Across these lines of enquiry, including quantitative evaluation, longitudinal 
examination, international application, computational evidence, and interdisciplinary 
expansion, the Digital Reverberations model can develop from a context-sensitive 
heuristic into an internationally generalisable theory of digital public health 
communication. In this process, it would not only deepen academic knowledge of trust, 
engagement, and participation but also generate practical tools for building adaptive 
219 
 
communication systems that are responsive to culture and able to address the 
complexities of public health in a digital era.

7.2 APPENDIX 4: INTERVIEW TRANSCRIPT  
 
PROF1 
Q1: Can you describe how you usually use social media in your daily life? 
Certainly. I also happen to be a healthcare administrator and social media is my life. I live on it 
— not only passively but actively, following developing conversations, observing and reading 
community sentiment and even shaping public relations strategy. Platforms like Twitter and 
Instagram are especially useful for spotting early signals from the community about current 
public health issues. I also follow LinkedIn and Facebook to see how the stories split between 
platform as well as demonstrative demographic. 
Over the years, I’ve gotten to see that public feedback is a valuable input. And to cite an 
example, “public feedback on Twitter has affected our pharmacy queuing system”—and I 
regard that change as “invaluable.” It wasn’t a response to an official report, but rather a series 
of repeated posts from users that illustrated how frustrating the wait time was for some. Those 
insights, if gleaned through regular surveys alone, may have taken months to compile. 
Q2: Have you ever learned something about health from social media? Can you share an 
example? 
Absolutely. I remember when COVID-19 first hit, how multiple doctors started anonymously at 
first, sharing their bedside experiences each day. These weren’t formal studies, but their 
warnings and pictorial posts about complications informed us of some problems long before 
official briefings. And, I hate to add, “it’s not stats that sell us; it is scary stories that make the 
threat real. We’ve seen that firsthand in the case of young victims who were sent out by 
community pages — they did more to drive urgency than any chart or briefing. 
Q3: How do you interact with health information on social media? Do you read, like, share, 
or comment on posts? 
All of the above. But, more importantly, I see the shape of engagement. I don’t just see what’s 
being shared — I see who is sharing it, how it’s framed and the language that is being used. For 
my own use, I share posts that are verified — and particularly if it is issued by the official health 
department or from someone who is known to be an expert. 
But I’ve since discovered something else: ones with human faces, stories and flesh-and-blood 
emotion behind the scenes tend to go farther. Data visuals and infographics may have their 
place, but personal stories are where engagement flows. That’s part of what we’re working to 
change internally — better aligning formal messaging and real human connection. 
Q4: When you see health information on social media, how do you decide if it is 
trustworthy? 
Trust is multi-layered. First, it’s about the source. Verified accounts, government pages and 
posts with official logos naturally gain further credibility. But even so, tone and consistency are 
important. 
235 
 
Unfortunately, “conflicting sources of information corrode our intuition about health. “So even 
when it’s with the best of intentions, it blows trust.” That sucked in the early days of the 
pandemic — you’d have one department saying one thing and a local clinic sharing something 
else. For professionals it was even confusing. For the public, it was chaos. That’s why after 
mid-2020 we moved to standardize more aggressively response formats and verification 
practices. 
Q5: Has any health information you've seen on social media influenced your health 
behaviours? 
Although my own actions are mostly based on medical guidelines and policy, frankly I would be 
lying to say that social media hasn’t had an influence. For example, a quick post on a possible 
outbreak or public concern is often followed closely by internal audits and policy reviews. 
Moreover, it drives personal reflection. One instance: a story that went viral of a frontline worker 
having lost a loved one to testing delays led to my forcing out an internal memo around 
prioritization of vulnerable community members. 
Once more, “It’s not numbers that convince us—it’s stories that bring the risk home.” 
Q6: Do you follow any health-related authorities or organizations on social media? What 
kind of information do they share? 
Yes, multiple. I track the Department of Health—Abu Dhabi, SEHA, WHO and several other 
regional hospitals’ accounts. They share advisories, event updates, vaccination schedules and 
more, these days often in videos with people who look like real patients or health care workers 
or community members — not just English speakers. 
We pushed for this change. Consistency and authenticity were the guts of the thing early on. 
There was a time when content just smacked of bureaucracy. But we have drifted toward 
something more fitting. We have the campaign we have because that’s the tone we now share 
as a country. 
Q7: Do you think the way health information is shared on social media is suitable for 
everyone in Abu Dhabi, considering our diverse cultures? 
That’s an ongoing challenge. Messaging needs to be diverse without watering down clarity. 
Certain groups do better with visual lay outs and others need to hear story telling. But for all of 
them, emotional relevance and linguistic familiarity count. 
This is why we now do cultural adaptation checks on all campaigns before they’re launched. 
We have learned that people want to feel spoken to — not just spoken at. And the data bears it 
out: Authentic posts get more engagement and, surprisingly, also a tone of respect. “There’s a 
sort of unifying in terms of the framing: It wraps people,” he said. That’s been a goal and a 
learning point. 
Q8: During health emergencies, like the COVID-19 pandemic, how helpful was the health 
information you found on social media? 
By then, social media had transformed into a real-time crisis communication machine. Our 
response depended on it — for messaging and for feedback. That two-way stream was 
236 
 
invaluable. And I would say it helped contain panic and misinformation better than some 
classic press strategies. public feedback on Twitter has influenced our pharmacy queuing 
system,” calling that kind of interaction “invaluable. It’s not the stats that sell us; it’s the scary 
stories that make the threat feel real.  
But again, quality varied. In some cases, emotional story telling succeeded over detailed policy 
explainers. We found that when they saw someone who looks like them, they engage quicker. 
“I didn’t need a chart to feel the risk — those social posts with young victims made it real.” I 
think that kind of guttural communication saved lives, I really believe that. 
Q9: What would you suggest to improve the way health information is shared on social 
media in Abu Dhabi? 
First, clarity over quantity. Second, humanize the messenger. Third, avoid contradiction and 
Consistency is the stone trust is built on; inconsistency shatters it. We are also working on 
incorporating feedback channels for the community — comment prompts, direct messaging 
response guidelines and sentiment dashboards. 
But perhaps trickiest of all, we have to relinquish control. Social media is not just a broadcast 
medium anymore — it’s a conversational space. And we have to treat it like that, not sending 
information out but taking it in just as earnestly. It is not statistics that persuade us — it is 
stories that make the threat personal. It’s incredibly enlightening.  
Q10: What kind of health topics would you like to see more of on social media?  
Preventative care, mental health and elderly care. These continue to be underrepresented, 
despite being demands from society. When a post is emotional, it is spreading faster, and it is 
sticking deeper.Also, behind-the-scenes tales from the health care work force. What people 
see they believe — if we show the humanity of our system, we help build stronger bridges. 
Clashing sources undermine our intuition related to health. Even when people have the best 
intentions, it blows trust. We learned it the hard way, without closing the feedback loop trust 
breaks down.  
We have learned that communication is not just about informing — it’s about building 
belonging. In this arena, “it’s not the statistics that sell us; it’s the scary stories that make the 
threat feel real.” And if we want to encourage the kind of public engagement that gets results, 
we need to keep infusing the human voice into our messages.
